constituents.name: Essential oil (mono- and sesquiterpenes)
constituents.note: Chamazulene (blue colour), sabinene, 1,8-cineole, camphor and alpha-bisabolol.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, rutin, quercetin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and antispasmodic flavonoids.

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones, phenolic acids, tannins and coumarins
constituents.note: Contribute to anti-inflammatory and astringent activity.

constituents.name: Betonicine and achilleine
constituents.note: Compounds traditionally linked to the haemostatic (styptic) effect.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic, rhizomatous perennial herb with erect, grooved, softly hairy stems arising in clumps from a creeping rootstock. The leaves are alternate, 2-3-pinnately dissected into narrow feathery segments, giving the whole plant a soft, fern-like texture (hence 'millefolium', thousand-leaved). Numerous small white to pale-pink flower heads are borne in dense, flat-topped compound corymbs at the stem tips. The plant has a sweet, herbaceous, slightly medicinal aroma when crushed.
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, 2-3-pinnately dissected into narrow feathery segments; aromatic when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Small white to pale pink flower heads in dense, flat-topped compound corymbs
botanical_description.stem: Erect, grooved, softly hairy/woolly, plain green (never purple-blotched)
botanical_description.fruit: Small flattened achenes without pappus

habitat.text: A common plant of grassland, meadows, roadsides, pastures and waste ground on a wide range of soils; native throughout Europe and temperate Asia and now naturalised across North America, Australia and New Zealand.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaf, stem and flower) are cut as the flat-topped corymbs open, in mid- to late summer, and dried quickly in a warm, shaded, airy place to preserve the essential oil and colour.
harvesting.season: Summer, at full flower (June-September)

traditional_uses.text: Yarrow has an ancient reputation as a wound herb (its old name 'staunchweed' and its association with Achilles reflect traditional use to stop bleeding and heal wounds) and as a bitter digestive and diaphoretic remedy for feverish colds, spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints, hepatobiliary disorders and gynaecological complaints. The flowering herb is classically taken as a hot infusion at the onset of a cold or fever to promote sweating, and used as a bitter tonic to stimulate appetite and digestion.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water as a bitter digestive and diaphoretic tea, traditionally taken hot at the onset of colds and fevers.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the fresh or dried flowering herb, typically around 1:5 in 45% alcohol.

preparations.name: Poultice/compress
preparations.text: Fresh crushed leaf or flowering tops applied directly to minor cuts and wounds, reflecting the traditional styptic (bleeding-stopping) and vulnerary use.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the flowering tops; the chamazulene-rich blue oil is used in low dilution for its anti-inflammatory action.

dosage.name: Dried herb infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest roughly 2-4 g of dried flowering herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 1-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily in adults. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Preclinical antiproliferative activity of yarrow flavonoids and sesquiterpenoids (e.g. centaureidin, casticin) against human tumour cell lines.
Antispasmodic (cramp easing)
Bitter digestive tonic (stimulates appetite and bile flow)
Diaphoretic (supports the body during colds and fevers)
Bitter digestive tonic (stimulates appetite and bile flow)
Styptic / haemostatic (slows bleeding from minor cuts)
Vulnerary (wound healing)

indications.note: Antiproliferative activity of Achillea millefolium extracts and constituents (centaureidin, casticin) against HeLa, MCF-7 and A431 cancer cell lines (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Diaphoretic (supports the body during colds and fevers)
indications.label: Cold & flu

indications.note: Diaphoretic (supports the body during colds and fevers)
indications.label: Fever

indications.note: Styptic / haemostatic (slows bleeding from minor cuts)
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Generally very safe. Rarely causes contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals (sesquiterpene lactones, same Asteraceae family). Avoid high doses during pregnancy (emmenagogue at large doses). May increase photosensitivity. Allergic reactions uncommon but possible in those sensitive to Asteraceae plants.

Duke (2002) rates yarrow as ++ with clinical evidence (score 2) for antibacterial, antiseptic, antispasmodic, choleretic, aperitif, and astringent activities, consistent with Commission E (KOM) approval for loss of appetite and dyspeptic ailments. Yarrow contains achillin, achilleic acid, and apigenin — compounds with anti-inflammatory and hemostatic properties. Traditional use for wound healing, fever management, and digestive complaints is well-supported. Dose: 2–4 g dried herb as tea three times daily. Caution: yarrow belongs to the Asteraceae family and can cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals; rare contact dermatitis from sesquiterpene lactones has been reported. Contraindicated in pregnancy due to potential emmenagogue effects (Duke, 2002).

As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae) it can cause allergic reactions or contact dermatitis in sensitive people, and handling the fresh plant in sunlight can occasionally cause photosensitivity.
Medicinal use is traditionally avoided in pregnancy (can stimulate the uterus and influence menstruation); caution while breastfeeding.
May interact with anticoagulant, sedative and blood-pressure medications and with drugs metabolised by the liver; seek professional advice if on regular medication.
Correct identification is essential, as several poisonous white-flowered umbellifers can be confused with yarrow; clinical evidence in humans is limited.
Additional omniasana.bio sources cited for Achillea millefolium (identification, distribution, foraging, traditional use and supporting evidence; see bibliography).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young feathery, finely divided leaves gathered for tea, salad or medicine; poison hemlock's young leaves are also feathery and finely divided.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) is foraged for its soft, feathery, fern-like leaves. As Grow Forage Cook Ferment warns, yarrow is sometimes mistaken for poison hemlock (Conium maculatum), which is very toxic - hemlock contains coniine and kills by ascending paralysis and respiratory failure, and a few leaves can be fatal. Young hemlock leaves are more feathery and can resemble yarrow before the plant grows tall. Because yarrow is a popular forage and hemlock is widespread, this is a potentially fatal confusion.
distinguishing_features: SMELL: yarrow smells sweet and herbaceous, often likened to fresh pine or a pleasant medicinal scent. Poison hemlock smells rank and musty/mousy.
Stem: yarrow has a solid, green, slightly hairy/woolly grooved stem with no purple. Poison hemlock has a hairless stem conspicuously blotched and streaked with red-purple, especially near the base.

Leaf and size: yarrow leaves are soft, very finely divided and feathery on a low plant (usually under 60 cm). Poison hemlock is a tall (1.5-3 m) branching biennial with larger, glossy, more open fern-like leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Check the stem and smell. A solid green (often woolly) stem with soft feathery leaves and a sweet pine-like herbaceous scent = yarrow. A hairless stem with red-purple blotches and a musty/mousy smell = poison hemlock - deadly, do not put it to your mouth. If the stem is purple-blotched or the smell is musty, do not use it.

constituents.name: Phenylpropanoid asarones (alpha- and beta-asarone)
constituents.note: Characteristic aromatic constituents - but beta-asarone is the toxic, safety-limiting compound.

constituents.name: Essential oil and lignans
constituents.note: Other aromatic and bioactive constituents of the rhizome.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic, semi-aquatic perennial herb spreading by a thick, branching, aromatic rhizome that creeps horizontally in mud at the water's edge. The leaves are erect, sword-shaped (ensiform), bright green and arranged in a fan like an iris, with a distinctive off-centre midrib and one crinkled, wavy margin. A small, finger-like, greenish-yellow flower spike (spadix) emerges obliquely part-way up a leaf-like flowering stem in summer, but in cool temperate climates the plant rarely sets viable seed and spreads mainly by rhizome division.
botanical_description.leaves: Erect, sword-shaped, iris-like, with an off-centre midrib and one wavy margin; strongly aromatic when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Small greenish-yellow flowers crowded on a finger-like spadix borne obliquely on a leaf-like stalk
botanical_description.stem: Leaf-like flowering stalk arising from the rhizome; the true stem is the underground rhizome
botanical_description.fruit: Rarely sets viable seed in temperate climates; propagates mainly by rhizome division

habitat.text: Grows in shallow water and wet mud at the margins of ponds, lakes, slow-moving rivers and marshes. Native to Asia and North America and long naturalised across Europe, where it was historically cultivated and traded for its aromatic rhizome.

harvesting.text: The rhizome is dug from autumn to early spring, when its aromatic oil content is highest, then washed, peeled and dried; the strong sweet-spicy fragrance released on cutting helps confirm correct identification against non-aromatic look-alikes such as yellow flag iris.
harvesting.season: Autumn to early spring

traditional_uses.text: Sweet flag rhizome is a classic aromatic bitter and nervine of Ayurvedic and East Asian traditional medicine, used for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite, and as a nervine tonic for memory, concentration and mild nervous complaints.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried, peeled rhizome simmered in water as a bitter digestive and nervine decoction.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried rhizome.

preparations.name: Powder
preparations.text: Dried rhizome powdered and traditionally taken in small amounts, often with honey, as a digestive and nervine remedy.

dosage.name: All preparations
dosage.text: Because beta-asarone is hepatotoxic, mutagenic and carcinogenic in animal studies and its content varies widely between varieties, no general dose is given here; internal use should be restricted to properly characterised, beta-asarone-free preparations under professional guidance. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Rhizome extracts and the constituent beta-asarone show antiproliferative, pro-apoptotic, anti-invasive and chemosensitizing/chemopreventive activity across gastric, bladder and esophageal cancer models (preclinical).
Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)
Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)
Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)
Aromatic bitter / carminative for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite (traditional)
Aromatic bitter / carminative for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite (traditional)
Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)

indications.note: Aromatic bitter / carminative for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite (traditional)
indications.label: Loss of appetite

indications.note: Aromatic bitter / carminative for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite (traditional)
indications.label: Bloating

indications.note: Acorus calamus extract inhibits gastric cancer (AGS) growth and angiogenesis; its constituent beta-asarone induces apoptosis and suppresses invasion/EMT in gastric, esophageal and bladder cancer cells and sensitizes them to chemotherapy (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)
indications.label: Cognitive function

indications.note: Aromatic bitter / carminative for indigestion, bloating, flatulence and poor appetite (traditional)
indications.label: Indigestion

indications.note: Traditional nervine for memory and concentration (preclinical neuroprotective, anticonvulsant and antidepressant-like activity)
indications.label: Memory

contraindications.note: SERIOUS: beta-asarone is hepatotoxic, mutagenic and carcinogenic in animal studies. Regulators have restricted internal use of asarone-containing Acorus calamus (the Asian high-beta-asarone varieties), and it is banned as a food additive in the USA. Avoid internal use except properly characterised beta-asarone-free preparations.
Do not use in pregnancy or breastfeeding, or for prolonged periods. The North American variety (Acorus americanus) is essentially beta-asarone-free, but most commercial calamus is not.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Sword-shaped leaves and the aromatic rhizome gathered from wet ground; yellow flag iris grows in the same wet habitat with near-identical sword-shaped leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Sweet flag (Acorus calamus) is foraged in marshes and pond margins for its aromatic rhizome and leaves. Yellow flag iris (Iris pseudacorus) grows in exactly the same wet habitat and has near-identical erect sword-shaped leaves - its very name pseudacorus means 'false sweet-flag.' Iris pseudacorus is a low-severity poison: all parts, especially the rhizome, cause salivation, vomiting and diarrhoea if eaten, and the sap can irritate skin. Because a non-flowering iris clump is easily taken for sweet flag, and the prized part (the rhizome) is the most toxic part of the iris, this is a genuine foraging hazard.
distinguishing_features: SMELL (decisive): crush a leaf or the rhizome. Sweet flag is strongly and sweetly aromatic (spicy/citrus). Yellow flag iris has no such fragrance - it is odourless.
Leaf detail: sweet flag has a prominent off-centre raised midrib and one crinkled/wavy leaf margin. Iris leaves are flat and plain with a central midrib and straight (not wavy) margins.

Flower/structure: sweet flag bears a unique finger-like green spadix angling out from the side of a leaf-like stalk. Yellow flag iris bears showy bright-yellow iris flowers. If it has a yellow iris flower, it is not sweet flag.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush a leaf and smell it. A strong sweet, spicy-citrus aroma with a wavy leaf margin and an off-centre midrib = sweet flag. No fragrance, flat leaves with straight margins, and (in season) bright-yellow iris flowers = yellow flag iris - do not eat; its rhizome causes vomiting and diarrhoea. If there is no aroma, do not use it.

constituents.name: Triterpene glycosides (actein, 23-epi-26-deoxyactein/cimicifugoside)
constituents.note: The compounds to which standardised extracts are normalised; act without measurable systemic oestrogenic effect.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids (fukinolic and cimicifugic acids)
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents of the root.

botanical_description.text: Tall, woodland perennial herb rising from a thick, knotty, dark rhizome with wiry black roots. The leaves are large and compound (2-3-ternately divided), with sharply toothed, oval to lance-shaped leaflets. In summer it sends up tall, wand-like racemes of small, unpleasant-smelling white flowers held well above the foliage, giving rise to the old name 'bugbane'; the flowers lack petals and consist mainly of a fluffy cluster of white stamens.
botanical_description.leaves: Large, 2-3-ternately compound, with sharply toothed oval to lance-shaped leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Small, malodorous, petal-less white flowers with fluffy stamens in tall wand-like racemes
botanical_description.stem: Tall, erect flowering stem well above the basal foliage
botanical_description.root: Thick, knotty, dark rhizome with numerous wiry black roots
botanical_description.fruit: Dry, follicle-like seed pods

habitat.text: Native to rich, shaded deciduous woodland and woodland margins of eastern North America, from southern Canada to Georgia, typically on moist, humus-rich soils.

harvesting.text: The root and rhizome are dug in autumn, after the seeds have ripened and the foliage has died back and the plant's energy has returned to the rootstock; the rhizome is cleaned, cut and dried.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Black cohosh root was used by Native American peoples for gynaecological complaints, rheumatism and general malaise, and entered nineteenth-century Eclectic medicine as a remedy for menstrual and menopausal disorders. It remains best known today as a standardised extract for menopausal hot flashes, night sweats and mood disturbance.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Isopropanolic or ethanolic extracts of the dried root and rhizome, standardised to triterpene glycoside content, are the best-studied clinical form.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root and rhizome, typically around 1:10.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Traditional decoction of the dried, chopped root and rhizome.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical studies commonly use standardised extracts equivalent to around 40 mg dried root/rhizome daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:10)
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 mL of a 1:10 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Black cohosh (Actaea racemosa) extracts and the triterpene glycoside actein inhibit proliferation of human breast, colon and liver cancer cells and potentiate chemotherapy (preclinical).

indications.note: Reduces neurovegetative and psychological menopausal symptoms (including anxiety and low mood)
indications.label: Anxiety

indications.note: Ethanolic black cohosh extract inhibits growth of MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells; actein induces apoptosis/G1 arrest, inhibits breast, colon and HepG2 liver cancer cell growth and synergizes with doxorubicin, 5-FU, paclitaxel and simvastatin (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Reduces neurovegetative and psychological menopausal symptoms (including anxiety and low mood)
indications.label: Low mood / depression

indications.note: Relief of menopausal symptoms - hot flashes, sweating, sleep and mood disturbance (a meta-analysis supports benefit, though an earlier systematic review judged the evidence inconsistent)
indications.label: Hot flashes

indications.note: Reduces neurovegetative and psychological menopausal symptoms (including anxiety and low mood); Relief of menopausal symptoms - hot flashes, sweating, sleep and mood disturbance (a meta-analysis supports benefit, though an earlier systematic review judged the evidence inconsistent)
indications.label: Menopause

contraindications.note: Rare reports of liver injury have prompted a precaution; large meta-analytic data found no evidence of hepatotoxicity, but stop use and seek advice if signs of liver problems occur (jaundice, dark urine, upper-abdominal pain).

Current evidence does not show an increased risk of breast cancer or proliferation of oestrogen-sensitive tissue, but women with or at risk of hormone-sensitive cancer should use it only under medical guidance.
Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Woodland rhizome dug as black cohosh; toxic baneberries (doll's eye and red baneberry) share very similar foliage and rhizomes and are gathered in mistake for it.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Black cohosh (Actaea racemosa) is dug for its rhizome, but several related North American Actaea species - notably doll's eye (Actaea pachypoda) and red baneberry (Actaea rubra) - have very similar woodland foliage and rhizomes and are easily mistaken for it during harvesting. Those baneberries are toxic in all parts: ingestion causes burning of the mouth, severe vomiting and diarrhoea and seizures, and red baneberry can be fatal, especially in children. Peer-reviewed work confirms commercial black cohosh is frequently adulterated with other Actaea species, contributing to safety concerns. Because the plants look alike - especially in spring before flowers or fruit appear - this is a genuine hazard.
distinguishing_features: Fruit (decisive when present): black cohosh bears a tall wand of fluffy white flowers that ripens to dry, papery seed pods. Baneberries instead bear clusters of shiny berries - chalk-white with a black eye (doll's eye) or glossy red (red baneberry). Any fleshy berries mean baneberry, not black cohosh.
Flower: black cohosh has long, showy white bottlebrush flower spikes; baneberry flowers are in short, rounded fluffy clusters.

Vegetative caution: in spring the leaves alone are very similar (Actaea foliage), so do not dig by leaf alone - the species are separated mainly on flowers and fruit. Confirm the plant in flower or fruit, or buy authenticated black cohosh.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Identify the plant in flower or fruit before digging. Black cohosh has tall white bottlebrush flower spikes that turn into dry papery pods; baneberry makes clusters of shiny white-with-black-dot or red berries. If you see fleshy berries - or cannot confirm flowers or fruit - do not dig the root; the baneberries are toxic. When in doubt, buy authenticated black cohosh.

botanical_description.text: Vigorous, low, patch-forming perennial herb spreading rapidly by white, brittle, creeping rhizomes. The leaves are broad and twice-ternate (in threes of threes), with oval, sharply toothed leaflets, borne on long stalks. In summer it sends up hollow, grooved, unspotted green stems topped with flat umbels of small white flowers, typical of the carrot family. The whole plant smells mildly of parsley or carrot when crushed.
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, twice-ternate, with oval sharply toothed leaflets in groups of three
botanical_description.flowers: Small white flowers in flat compound umbels
botanical_description.root: Thin, white, brittle, fast-spreading creeping rhizome
botanical_description.fruit: Small ridged schizocarp typical of the carrot family

habitat.text: A common weed of gardens, hedgerows, shaded waste ground and disturbed soil, often near old dwellings; native to Europe and temperate Asia and widely naturalised elsewhere, where its invasive spreading rhizomes make it a persistent garden weed.

harvesting.text: The young leaves and stems are gathered in spring, before flowering, when they are most tender and least bitter; the root can be dug at the same time. Careful identification against poisonous Apiaceae look-alikes (poison hemlock, hemlock water-dropwort, fool's parsley) is essential before harvesting.
harvesting.season: Spring, before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Ground elder has long been used in European folk medicine as a remedy for gout and rheumatic and arthritic joint pain (reflected in the name 'podagraria', from podagra, gout), typically applied as a poultice or taken as a decoction. The young leaves have also been eaten as a spring pot-herb, valued for their nutrient content as well as their medicinal reputation.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried or fresh leaf, stem and root simmered in water as a traditional remedy for gout and rheumatic pain.

preparations.name: Poultice
preparations.text: Fresh leaves crushed or boiled and applied directly to gouty or arthritic joints.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf (culinary/medicinal)
preparations.text: Young spring leaves eaten as a cooked pot-herb, similarly to spinach, continuing the traditional food-medicine use.

dosage.name: Decoction / infusion
dosage.text: Traditional use has no standardised modern dose; herbal references suggest a small handful of fresh leaf and stem per cup as an infusion or light decoction. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Polyacetylenes (falcarindiol)
constituents.note: Falcarindiol, a potent COX-1 inhibitor (IC50 0.3 microM), identified as a key anti-inflammatory constituent underlying the plant's traditional anti-rheumatic and analgesic use.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Contribute to the plant's antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Coumarins and essential oil
constituents.note: Minor aromatic and coumarin constituents of the aerial parts.

contraindications.note: Generally safe as a food plant when young leaves are harvested. Contains furocoumarins (photosensitising compounds) — handling large quantities in bright sunlight may cause skin irritation. Individuals with Apiaceae allergies should exercise caution. Not recommended in therapeutic doses during pregnancy.
Duke (2002) does not appear to include a dedicated entry for ground elder (Aegopodium podagraria) in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young leaves and foliage gathered in spring, before flowering.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Poison hemlock is a carrot-family plant with lacy foliage that can be mistaken for edible umbellifer greens; its alkaloid coniine causes an ascending paralysis that stops breathing, and even a small amount can be fatal. It grows in the same disturbed ground, hedgerows and waste places as ground elder.
distinguishing_features: Ground elder leaves are in neat groups of three (twice-ternate) with broad, oval, sharply toothed leaflets. Poison hemlock leaves are finely divided, feathery and fern-like — not in groups of three.
Poison hemlock has a hairless stem blotched and streaked with purple; ground elder's stem is plain green and grooved, never purple-spotted.
Crushed ground elder smells mildly of parsley or carrot; crushed poison hemlock smells foul and musty ('mousy').
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Check the stem: purple blotches or streaks on a smooth, hairless stem = poison hemlock — do not eat. Ground elder has a plain green grooved stem, broad toothed leaflets in threes, and a mild parsley smell (never mousy).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Foliage (mistaken for celery or parsley) and roots (mistaken for parsnip), where the plant grows near water.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Hemlock water-dropwort is probably the most poisonous plant in Britain; its toxin oenanthotoxin causes violent convulsions, and its parsnip-like roots and celery-like leaves have repeatedly been eaten by mistake, often fatally. It grows in and beside water.
distinguishing_features: Habitat is the first filter: hemlock water-dropwort grows in or right beside water (wet ditches, streams, marshes); ground elder grows in dry gardens, hedgerows and disturbed ground.
Hemlock water-dropwort has a cluster of pale, swollen, finger-like root tubers ('dead man's fingers'); ground elder spreads by thin white creeping rhizomes, not fleshy tubers.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: If the plant is growing in or at the edge of water and has a cluster of swollen finger-like root tubers, treat it as hemlock water-dropwort — do not touch the roots. Ground elder grows in dry ground from thin creeping rhizomes.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young leaves and foliage in spring.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Fool's parsley is a common carrot-family weed of gardens and disturbed ground — named for being mistaken for parsley — that contains toxic polyacetylenes and can cause burning of the mouth, vomiting and neurological effects, especially in children.
distinguishing_features: Fool's parsley leaves are finely dissected, thin, dark green and fern-like; ground elder leaves are broad, oval, toothed leaflets in neat groups of three — a coarser, very different leaf.
In flower, fool's parsley shows three long, narrow bracteoles hanging down beneath each small flower cluster (a 'beard'); ground elder umbels have no such drooping beard.
Crushed fool's parsley smells foul and acrid ('mousy'); ground elder smells mildly of parsley or carrot.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Finely dissected fern-like leaves with a foul mousy smell — and, if flowering, a drooping three-part 'beard' under each little umbel — mean fool's parsley, not ground elder (which has broad toothed leaflets in threes and a mild parsley scent).

constituents.name: Triterpene saponins (escin / aescin)
constituents.note: The active mixture responsible for the venotonic and anti-oedematous effects; extracts are standardised to it. Escin is available as oral drages and a transdermal gel, with efficacy shown in chronic venous insufficiency and blunt-trauma injury.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and coumarins (aesculin)
constituents.note: Supporting constituents of the seed.

botanical_description.text: Large deciduous tree, to 25-30 m, with a broad domed crown and stout, upward-arching branches. The leaves are opposite and palmately compound, with five to seven large, obovate, toothed leaflets radiating from a long stalk. In spring the tree bears large, showy, upright pyramidal flower spikes ('candles') of white flowers marked with yellow or pink at the base. The glossy brown seeds ('conkers') develop inside a spiky green husk and fall in autumn.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, palmately compound with 5-7 large obovate toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Showy, upright, pyramidal spikes ('candles') of white flowers marked yellow/pink at the base
botanical_description.stem: Stout trunk with a broad domed crown of upward-arching branches
botanical_description.fruit: Glossy brown seed ('conker') enclosed in a spiny green husk

habitat.text: Native to the Balkan Peninsula, and widely planted and naturalised as an ornamental and avenue tree across temperate Europe, North America and elsewhere.

harvesting.text: The seeds are gathered as they fall from the spiny husks in autumn; medicinal use requires a standardised, processed extract, since the raw seed, bark and leaves contain toxic esculin and must never be eaten raw.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Horse chestnut seed has a long folk history as a remedy for varicose veins, haemorrhoids and 'heavy legs', and was also used topically for rheumatic pain and bruising. Modern use is almost exclusively as a standardised seed extract for chronic venous insufficiency, confirming this traditional venous-support reputation.

preparations.name: Standardised extract (oral)
preparations.text: Standardised seed extract, normalised to escin content, taken as tablets or capsules for chronic venous insufficiency; this is the best-studied clinical form.

preparations.name: Topical gel
preparations.text: Escin-containing transdermal gel applied to the skin over affected veins or bruised tissue.

dosage.name: Standardised oral extract
dosage.text: Clinical studies commonly use extracts standardised to around 100-150 mg escin daily, in divided doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Topical gel
dosage.text: Applied topically per product labelling to the affected area, typically several times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-oedematous and anti-inflammatory - escin reduces vascular permeability in inflamed tissue and exerts venotonic effects on veins
Anti-oedematous and anti-inflammatory - escin reduces vascular permeability in inflamed tissue and exerts venotonic effects on veins

Escin, the triterpene saponin of Aesculus hippocastanum, induces apoptosis and cell-cycle arrest and chemosensitizes breast, hepatocellular, lung and pancreatic cancer cells (preclinical, including in vivo xenograft models).
Anti-oedematous and anti-inflammatory - escin reduces vascular permeability in inflamed tissue and exerts venotonic effects on veins; Relief of haemorrhoid symptoms (venotonic)

indications.note: Escin shows antiproliferative and pro-apoptotic activity and enhances chemotherapy/immunotherapy efficacy in breast (MCF-7), hepatocellular, lung and pancreatic cancer models; it modulates NF-kappaB, p53, p38 MAPK/ERK and PD-L1 (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Chronic venous insufficiency - reduces leg pain, swelling (oedema), heaviness and itching; supports varicose veins
indications.label: Pain (general)

indications.note: Chronic venous insufficiency - reduces leg pain, swelling (oedema), heaviness and itching; supports varicose veins
indications.label: Swelling / fluid retention

indications.note: Chronic venous insufficiency - reduces leg pain, swelling (oedema), heaviness and itching; supports varicose veins
indications.label: Varicose veins

contraindications.note: Use only standardised, processed seed extract. Raw conkers, leaves and bark contain toxic esculin and can cause poisoning - never eat raw horse chestnut.
May increase bleeding risk, so use caution with anticoagulant/antiplatelet medicines; use caution in kidney disease and avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Horse chestnut seed contains aescin and coumarin-like constituents (esculin) that may add to the effect of blood-thinning or antiplatelet drugs, so combined use should be monitored.

constituents.name: Tannins (condensed and hydrolysable, including agrimoniin)
constituents.note: Responsible for the characteristic astringency.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, quercetin and their glycosides)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Triterpenes, volatile oil and silica
constituents.note: Minor constituents of the herb.

botanical_description.text: Slender, downy perennial herb with a basal rosette of pinnately compound leaves and a single, unbranched, upright flowering stem. The leaflets are toothed, alternating large and small, and silvery-downy beneath. Small, five-petalled, bright yellow flowers are borne closely along a slender terminal spike (raceme), opening a few at a time from the base upwards, giving the old name 'church steeples'. After flowering, small bristly, hooked burrs form that cling to clothing and animal fur for dispersal.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnately compound, toothed leaflets of alternating large and small size, silvery-downy beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, five-petalled, bright yellow flowers crowded on a slender terminal spike
botanical_description.stem: Single, unbranched, upright, downy flowering stem
botanical_description.fruit: Small bristly, hooked burr that clings to clothing and fur

habitat.text: Grows in dry grassland, hedgerows, field margins, roadsides and open woodland edges on well-drained, often calcareous soils; native and common throughout Europe, temperate Asia and North Africa.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts are cut in summer, at the start of flowering, and dried promptly in a warm, airy, shaded place.
harvesting.season: Summer, at the start of flowering

traditional_uses.text: Agrimony has a long folk history as an astringent remedy for mild diarrhoea, as a gargle for sore throats and mild mouth/throat inflammation, and as a mild bitter digestive tonic; it has also been applied topically to minor wounds and skin inflammation. It is officially recognised in the European Union herbal monograph for traditional use in mild diarrhoea and mild inflammation of the mouth or throat.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water, taken internally for mild diarrhoea and digestive complaints or used as a gargle/mouthwash for sore throat.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried flowering herb.

preparations.name: Poultice/compress
preparations.text: Crushed or infused herb applied externally to minor wounds and skin inflammation.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: The EU herbal monograph describes traditional use of about 2.5-5 g dried herb as an infusion, taken up to three times daily, or used as a gargle/mouthwash. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle (mild inflammation of the mouth and throat / sore throat)
Agrimonia eupatoria ethanolic extract is cytotoxic to breast, colon, lung and cervical cancer cells and inhibits the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway (preclinical/in silico).
Astringent (relief of mild diarrhoea)
Astringent (relief of mild diarrhoea)
Mild bitter digestive tonic (indigestion)
Mild bitter digestive tonic (indigestion)
Vulnerary (minor skin inflammation and small superficial wounds, topical)

indications.note: Agrimonia eupatoria ethanolic extract shows dose-dependent cytotoxicity against MCF-7 (breast), HT-29 (colon), A549 (lung) and HeLa (cervical) cancer cells and inhibits the PI3K/Akt/mTOR/COX-2/MMP-2 axis (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle (mild inflammation of the mouth and throat / sore throat); Vulnerary (minor skin inflammation and small superficial wounds, topical)
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle (mild inflammation of the mouth and throat / sore throat)
indications.label: Sore throat

indications.note: Vulnerary (minor skin inflammation and small superficial wounds, topical)
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Traditional-use indication only: if diarrhoea persists longer than 3-4 days, or symptoms of mouth/throat inflammation persist beyond about a week, a doctor should be consulted.
Use during pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended, as safety has not been established.
Tannin-rich; very high or prolonged intake may cause gastrointestinal upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.

constituents.name: Phenolic compounds and tannins
constituents.note: Lady's mantle is rich in ellagitannins and other tannins (the basis of its astringency) together with phenolic acids (gallic and caffeic acids) and flavonoids such as quercetin and catechin, which underlie its antioxidant activity.

botanical_description.text: Low, clump-forming perennial herb with distinctive rounded, softly hairy leaves divided into 7-11 shallow, scalloped, toothed lobes, fanning out like a cloak (giving the genus name Alchemilla, 'little alchemist', after the dew drops that collect in the leaf centre, once prized by alchemists). Tiny, petal-less, yellow-green flowers are borne in loose, branching clusters above the foliage.
botanical_description.leaves: Rounded, softly hairy, palmately lobed into 7-11 shallow scalloped, toothed lobes
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, petal-less, yellow-green flowers in loose branching clusters
botanical_description.stem: Short, softly hairy flowering stems rising above the leaf clump
botanical_description.fruit: Small dry achene

habitat.text: Grows in damp meadows, pastures, mountain grassland, streamsides and open woodland, typically on moist, nutrient-rich soils; native to Europe and parts of temperate Asia, favouring cooler upland and northern regions.

harvesting.text: The leaves and flowering tops are gathered in summer while the plant is in flower, and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Summer flowering period

traditional_uses.text: Lady's mantle has long been used in European herbal medicine as a gentle astringent for menstrual complaints and as a mild gynaecological tonic, and topically for minor wounds and skin inflammation, reflecting its tannin-rich astringent character.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried leaf and flowering tops infused in hot water, traditionally taken for menstrual complaints and mild digestive upset.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb.

preparations.name: Poultice/wash
preparations.text: Infused herb used as a topical wash or poultice for minor wounds and skin irritation.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-4 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Alchemilla vulgaris extracts (aerial parts and root) show antioxidant, genoprotective and antitumor activity against multiple human cancer cell lines (preclinical).

indications.note: Alchemilla vulgaris extracts suppress the growth of MCF-7, A375, A549, HCT116 (aerial parts) and PC-3, MCF-7, Caco-2 (root) cancer cells via caspase-dependent apoptosis and autophagy, while protecting normal cells (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

contraindications.note: Generally considered very safe. One of the gentlest astringent herbs in European herbalism. No significant drug interactions. Tannin content means very high doses should be avoided in sensitive stomachs. Not significantly contraindicated in pregnancy at normal herbal tea doses, but medicinal doses should be used cautiously.

Duke (2002) rates lady's mantle as ++ and notes astringent, angioprotective, anti-inflammatory, and vulnerary activities at the experimental level (score 1). The plant is rich in tannins and is traditionally used for diarrhea, wound healing, and as a mild gynecological tonic. Duke notes its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties as experimentally supported. No significant safety concerns are noted at usual herbal doses; the plant is generally well-tolerated (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Allicin and organosulfur compounds (ajoene, S-allyl-cysteine)
constituents.note: Allicin (diallyl thiosulfinate), formed enzymatically when raw garlic is crushed, together with ajoene and the aged-garlic compound S-allyl-cysteine, are the principal bioactives behind garlic's antimicrobial and cardiovascular effects — acting partly by raising nitric oxide and by targeting microbial thiol-containing enzymes.

botanical_description.text: Bulbous perennial herb usually grown as an annual, forming an underground compound bulb ('head') of several angular cloves wrapped in papery white to purplish skin. The leaves are flat, narrow and strap-shaped, greyish-green, arising from the bulb. A tall, leafless flowering stem (scape) may rise from the bulb bearing a rounded umbel of small pink-white flowers, often mixed with small bulbils, though cultivated garlic rarely sets true seed.
botanical_description.habit: Bulbous perennial herb, usually grown as an annual
botanical_description.leaves: Flat, narrow, strap-shaped, greyish-green
botanical_description.flowers: Small pink-white flowers in a rounded umbel, often mixed with bulbils; true seed is rare in cultivated garlic
botanical_description.stem: Tall, leafless flowering scape when present
botanical_description.root: Fibrous roots below a compound bulb of several cloves
botanical_description.fruit: Rarely sets true seed; propagated mainly by cloves

habitat.text: Believed to originate in Central Asia; cultivated worldwide in temperate and subtropical climates as a garden and field crop, thriving in well-drained, sunny sites.

harvesting.text: Bulbs are lifted in mid- to late summer once the leaves have yellowed and died back, then cured (dried) in a warm, airy place before storage; young leaves and immature flower scapes ('sprouts') can be cut earlier in the season.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer for the bulb; spring for young leaves and sprouts

traditional_uses.text: Garlic has one of the longest continuous medicinal histories of any plant, used across many traditional systems (Ayurvedic, Chinese, Egyptian, Greco-Roman and European folk medicine) as an antimicrobial, cardiovascular and respiratory remedy, for colds and infections, and as a digestive and immune tonic. This traditional reputation is now among the most extensively clinically studied of all herbal medicines.

preparations.name: Fresh clove
preparations.text: Fresh clove crushed or chopped and left to stand briefly (activating allicin formation) before eating raw or lightly cooked.

preparations.name: Standardised extract/powder
preparations.text: Dried garlic powder standardised to allicin content, or aged garlic extract, taken as capsules or tablets.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the fresh bulb.

dosage.name: Fresh clove
dosage.text: Traditional and clinical references commonly cite around one fresh clove (about 4 g) daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Standardised powder extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials commonly use around 300 mg of standardised dried powder extract (about 1.3% allicin), up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Garlic organosulfur compounds (allicin, diallyl trisulfide, ajoene, S-allylcysteine) induce apoptosis and cell-cycle arrest and inhibit proliferation, invasion and metastasis across leukaemia, breast, colon and other cancers (preclinical); a randomized trial found garlic supplementation reduced gastric-cancer mortality.

indications.note: Garlic and its organosulfur compounds show broad preclinical anticancer/chemopreventive activity (apoptosis, cell-cycle arrest, anti-invasion); in the randomized Shandong Intervention Trial, 7 years of garlic supplementation significantly reduced gastric-cancer mortality over 22-year follow-up (preclinical + RCT).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

contraindications.note: Garlic is generally very safe at culinary and supplemental doses for most healthy adults. Raw garlic contains allicin (formed when crushed) at concentrations that can irritate the gastrointestinal tract — nausea, heartburn, flatulence, and diarrhoea are the most common side effects, especially with large amounts of raw garlic on an empty stomach. Garlic may inhibit platelet aggregation and enhance anticoagulant effects of warfarin — use caution with blood-thinning medications, especially before surgery (stop 7–10 days prior). May lower blood pressure and blood sugar — caution when combined with antihypertensive or antidiabetic medications. Topical raw garlic can cause chemical burns (contact dermatitis) and should not be applied undiluted to skin for prolonged periods. Odour (from allicin metabolites) affects breath and perspiration. Pregnancy: culinary amounts safe; avoid medicinal-dose supplements due to blood-thinning risk (National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health, 2021).

Duke (2002) rates garlic as +++ with exceptional clinical evidence (score 3) for antiseptic activity, and score 2 for anti-aggregant (antiplatelet), antiatherosclerotic, antiatherogenic, antibacterial, and antimycotic effects — among the most clinically validated herbal medicines. Commission E approves garlic for arteriosclerosis prevention and elevated blood lipids. Dose: 4 g (1 clove) fresh garlic daily, or 300 mg standardized dry powder extract (1.3% allicin) three times daily. Important interaction: garlic significantly potentiates anticoagulant medications (warfarin, aspirin) due to its anti-aggregant effects; use with caution in patients on blood thinners (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Garlic supplements reduced saquinavir exposure by about half in healthy volunteers, which could lower the effectiveness of some HIV protease inhibitors.
Garlic has antiplatelet activity (ajoene) and may add to the bleeding risk of anticoagulant or antiplatelet drugs; interactions with warfarin have been reported.

Garlic may modestly lower blood glucose (a meta-analysis in diabetic patients found reduced fasting glucose and HbA1c); watch for an additive effect with diabetes medication (an interaction with chlorpropamide has been reported).

Garlic can lower blood pressure (a meta-analysis found meaningful reductions in people with high blood pressure); combined with blood-pressure medicines it may add to their effect, so monitor blood pressure.

pairings.note: Garlic and ginger both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Garlic and ginseng both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Garlic and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Garlic and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Spring leaves, when foraging wild garlic (ramsons) foliage before flowering.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: When people gather wild garlic (ramsons) leaves in spring, autumn crocus leaves — which emerge at the same time in the same damp meadows and woodland edges — are the deadliest thing they can pick by mistake. Autumn crocus contains colchicine, a cell poison with no reliable antidote; a few leaves can kill, and poisoning is repeatedly documented from 'wild garlic' that was actually autumn crocus, progressing from stomach upset to multi-organ failure.
distinguishing_features: Crushed wild-garlic leaves smell strongly of garlic or onion; autumn crocus leaves have NO garlic smell.

Each wild-garlic leaf grows singly on its own slender stalk; autumn crocus leaves are thicker, stiffer and glossy, several clasped together from a common base at ground level, with no individual stalks.
Wild garlic pulls up with a small white bulb and onion-like roots; autumn crocus has no garlic bulb.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush one leaf with clean hands and smell it: a strong garlic or onion smell = wild garlic (safe); no garlic smell = do not pick it. Hands already smelling of garlic give false reassurance, so test with clean hands.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Spring leaves, when foraging wild garlic (ramsons) foliage before flowering.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Lily-of-the-valley leaves emerge at the same time and in the same shady, damp woodland as wild garlic and are a classic, sometimes fatal, mix-up. The plant contains cardiac glycosides (convallatoxin) that disturb the heartbeat like an overdose of heart medicine, and there is no reliable antidote; foragers who ate leaves they took for wild garlic have arrived at hospital with a dangerously slow heart and low blood pressure.
distinguishing_features: Crushed wild-garlic leaves smell strongly of garlic or onion; lily-of-the-valley leaves have NO garlic smell.
Wild garlic has ONE leaf per individual stalk; lily-of-the-valley has a pair (sometimes three) of leaves wrapped together around a single common stalk, often purplish at the base.
Lily-of-the-valley leaves are stiffer and glossier, and there is no garlic bulb underneath.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush one leaf with clean hands and smell: strong garlic or onion = wild garlic (safe); no garlic smell = discard. Then check the leaves — one leaf per separate stalk (wild garlic) versus a pair of leaves sheathed around one common stalk (lily-of-the-valley).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young spring leaves, when foraging wild garlic (ramsons) foliage.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Lords-and-ladies pushes up its young leaves at the same time and in the very same damp woods as wild garlic, often mixed in with it. Every part contains needle-like calcium-oxalate crystals: biting a leaf drives them into the mouth and throat, causing immediate intense burning, blistering and swelling — occasionally enough to threaten the airway.
distinguishing_features: Crushed wild-garlic leaves smell strongly of garlic or onion; lords-and-ladies leaves have NO garlic smell.
Wild-garlic leaves have parallel veins running the length of the leaf; lords-and-ladies leaves have a branching net of veins.

Lords-and-ladies leaves are arrow- or spear-shaped with two backward-pointing lobes ('tails') at the base, often glossy and sometimes dark-spotted; wild-garlic leaves are smooth ovals with no basal lobes.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush one leaf with clean hands and smell: garlic or onion = wild garlic (safe); no smell = discard. Reject any leaf that is arrow-shaped with basal 'tail' lobes or has net-like (not parallel) veins — that is lords-and-ladies.

botanical_description.text: Succulent, rosette-forming perennial with thick, fleshy, lance-shaped leaves edged with soft, pale teeth, each leaf filled with a clear, slippery, mucilaginous gel beneath a tougher green rind. In favourable conditions it produces a tall flowering spike bearing tubular yellow (sometimes orange) flowers.
botanical_description.height: Leaves 40-80 cm; flower spike to about 1 m
botanical_description.leaves: Thick, fleshy, lance-shaped, edged with soft pale teeth, filled with clear mucilaginous gel
botanical_description.flowers: Tubular yellow (occasionally orange) flowers on a tall, unbranched or sparsely branched spike
botanical_description.stem: Very short, often inconspicuous, below the leaf rosette
botanical_description.fruit: Capsule (rarely produced in cultivation)
botanical_description.flowering_period: Varies with climate; typically under warm, dry conditions

habitat.text: Believed native to the Arabian Peninsula, and now cultivated and naturalised throughout arid and semi-arid tropical and subtropical regions worldwide as a garden, medicinal and commercial crop.

harvesting.text: Outer, mature leaves are cut close to the base as needed; the clear inner gel (mucilage) is separated from the outer rind and from the bitter yellow latex (aloin-containing exudate) just under the rind, since the gel and the latex have different uses and safety profiles.
harvesting.season: Year-round where climate permits, from mature outer leaves

traditional_uses.text: Aloe vera has an ancient reputation, recorded from ancient Egypt onward, as a topical remedy for burns, wounds and skin inflammation (the 'burn plant' / 'first aid plant'), and the bitter leaf latex has been used traditionally as a stimulant laxative. It remains one of the most widely used topical botanicals for skin care and minor wound healing.

preparations.name: Fresh gel
preparations.text: Clear inner leaf gel applied fresh, or as a stabilised commercial gel, directly to the skin for burns, minor wounds and skin irritation.

preparations.name: Oral juice/gel
preparations.text: Inner leaf gel (latex-free) taken orally, used traditionally for digestive support.

preparations.name: Latex/dried juice
preparations.text: The bitter yellow leaf exudate (containing anthraquinone glycosides) used traditionally as a stimulant laxative; not recommended for prolonged use.

dosage.name: Topical gel
dosage.text: Applied liberally to the affected skin area as needed. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Oral gel/juice
dosage.text: Traditional references suggest around 1 tablespoon of gel, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Polysaccharides (acemannan)
constituents.note: Acemannan and other glucomannans are the principal gel polysaccharides, underlying the emollient and wound-healing effects.

constituents.name: Anthraquinones (aloin, aloe-emodin)
constituents.note: Found mainly in the leaf latex (not the inner gel); responsible for the traditional laxative effect and requiring caution with prolonged use.

constituents.name: Enzymes, vitamins and minerals
constituents.note: Minor bioactive enzymes, vitamins (including C and E) and minerals contribute to the antioxidant and skin-conditioning properties of the gel.

actions.note: Aloe vera and its anthraquinone aloe-emodin show dose-dependent antiproliferative and pro-apoptotic activity against multiple cancer types in vitro and in vivo (preclinical).

indications.note: Aloe-emodin, an Aloe vera anthraquinone, induces apoptosis and inhibits growth of gastric, oral squamous, hepatocellular, lung (incl. xenograft) and glioblastoma cancer cells, modulating caspases, Bcl-2/Bax, PI3K/Akt and MEK/ERK signalling (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

contraindications.note: Topical use of aloe gel is generally safe for most people. Oral use of aloe latex as a laxative is potentially unsafe with prolonged use due to risk of dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and potential kidney damage. Aloe latex contains anthraquinone glycosides which may cause abdominal cramping and diarrhea. Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding. May interact with diabetes medications, diuretics, and laxatives. Oral aloe gel at typical doses appears safe short-term but long-term safety is unclear. Allergic reactions possible in sensitive individuals.

Duke (2002) reports clinical evidence (score 2/3) supporting aloe gel's use in psoriasis, constipation (in adults), and as a vulnerary. Gel preparations have been clinically validated for peptic ulcers (excluding stress-induced), radiation burns, and skin ulcers. Dose: 1 tablespoon gel three times daily or 50–200 mg dried juice equivalent per day as a laxative. An important safety distinction is made: the gel (inner leaf mucilage) is AHPA Class 1 (safe), while the inner leaf juice or dried latex is Class 2b due to anthraquinone content and is contraindicated in intestinal inflammation, colitis, Crohn's disease, appendicitis, and during menstruation (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Aloe vera (taken internally) can lower blood sugar - a meta-analysis found improved fasting glucose in prediabetes and improved HbA1c in type 2 diabetes - so combining it with diabetes medicines may increase the risk of hypoglycaemia; monitor blood glucose.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Fleshy leaves harvested for their soothing gel; agave forms a very similar large rosette of thick, pointed succulent leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Aloe vera is grown and harvested for the clear soothing gel inside its fleshy leaves. Agave (Agave americana and relatives) forms a very similar rosette of thick, pointed succulent leaves and, as Gardening Know How notes, the two are easily confused if you have not grown both. But agave is not soothing: its sap contains calcium oxalate raphides, steroidal saponins and acrid oils, and peer-reviewed dermatology reports document painful irritant contact dermatitis (even purpura) from agave sap. Applying or ingesting agave sap in mistake for aloe gel can burn the skin, mouth and gut. Because both are common houseplants and garden succulents, this is a realistic mix-up.
distinguishing_features: Inside the leaf (decisive): snap or cut a leaf. Aloe leaves are soft and filled with clear, slippery gel. Agave leaves are tough and fibrous/stringy with no gel - you cannot easily snap them by hand.
Leaf tip and edge: agave leaves end in one hard, sharp terminal spine and often have stiff sharp teeth; aloe has softer, more pliable leaves with soft whitish teeth and no rigid terminal spike.
Whole plant over time: aloe flowers repeatedly on a branched stalk and lives on; agave typically flowers only once, on a huge tall stalk, then dies.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Cut a leaf. Clear, slippery gel inside a soft leaf = aloe (safe gel). A tough, fibrous leaf with no gel, ending in a hard sharp spine = agave - its sap is an irritant that can burn skin, mouth and gut, so do not use it. When in doubt, do not apply or consume it unless you are certain it is aloe.

constituents.name: Essential oil (neral and geranial, i.e. citral)
constituents.note: Gives the lemon aroma and contributes to the calming and antimicrobial effects.

constituents.name: Verbascoside (acteoside)
constituents.note: The principal polyphenol of the extract and a major antioxidant constituent.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Additional antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous, aromatic shrub with slender, brittle branches and whorls of narrow, lance-shaped, rough-textured leaves that release a strong lemon scent when crushed. Small, pale lilac to white flowers are borne in loose terminal and axillary spikes in late summer.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, rough-textured, borne in whorls of three; strongly lemon-scented when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale lilac to white flowers in loose terminal and axillary spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Small dry schizocarp

habitat.text: Native to South America (Argentina, Chile, Peru) and widely cultivated as a culinary and medicinal herb in warm-temperate and Mediterranean climates worldwide, typically in sunny, well-drained garden sites.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked through the growing season, with the essential oil content and aroma at their best just before flowering; leaves are used fresh or dried quickly in a warm, shaded, airy place to preserve the lemon-scented volatile oil.
harvesting.season: Spring to autumn, best just before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Lemon verbena leaf is a traditional South American and Mediterranean remedy for digestive complaints (indigestion, flatulence), as a calming, mildly sedative tea for anxiety and sleep, and is widely drunk as a fragrant culinary herbal tea.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Fresh or dried leaf infused in hot water as a fragrant digestive and calming tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried leaf.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Concentrated leaf extract (rich in verbascoside) used in some clinical studies of muscle recovery and antioxidant support.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-3 g of dried leaf per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Aloysia citrodora (lemon verbena) essential oil and extracts inhibit growth and migration of breast cancer and melanoma cells and reduce tumour size in vivo (preclinical).

Antioxidant - supports muscle recovery and reduces exercise-induced muscle soreness and oxidative stress; a placebo-controlled RCT of a lemon verbena extract found less loss of muscle strength and less soreness after exhaustive exercise
Anxiolytic / calming (anxiety) - also supported by a clinical trial of the closely related Aloysia polystachya
Carminative digestive aid (indigestion, flatulence)
Carminative digestive aid (indigestion, flatulence)
Sedative / sleep support (insomnia)

indications.note: Anxiolytic / calming (anxiety) - also supported by a clinical trial of the closely related Aloysia polystachya
indications.label: Anxiety

indications.note: Lemon verbena (Aloysia citrodora) essential oil and extracts show antiproliferative and pro-apoptotic activity against breast cancer and melanoma cells in vitro and reduce tumour growth in mouse models (targeting HB-EGF/EGFR and ERK signalling) (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Antioxidant - supports muscle recovery and reduces exercise-induced muscle soreness and oxidative stress; a placebo-controlled RCT of a lemon verbena extract found less loss of muscle strength and less soreness after exhaustive exercise
indications.label: Muscle soreness

indications.note: Antioxidant - supports muscle recovery and reduces exercise-induced muscle soreness and oxidative stress; a placebo-controlled RCT of a lemon verbena extract found less loss of muscle strength and less soreness after exhaustive exercise
indications.label: Stress

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; human safety and efficacy are not fully established. Its sedative effect can add to that of alcohol, sedatives and sleep medicines.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Lemon verbena is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

constituents.name: Mucilage polysaccharides (rhamnogalacturonans, arabinans, glucans, arabinogalactans)
constituents.note: The principal soothing, film-forming constituents; the isolated rhamnogalacturonan also shows direct antitussive activity in animal models.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Pectin and starch
constituents.note: Other carbohydrate constituents of the root.

botanical_description.text: Soft, downy perennial herb with an erect, branching stem and broad, soft, velvety, greyish-green leaves, shallowly 3-5-lobed and toothed. Pale pink to white five-petalled flowers, typical of the mallow family, are borne singly or in small clusters in the leaf axils. The thick, fleshy, whitish taproot is the main medicinal part, rich in soothing mucilage.
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, soft, velvety, greyish-green, shallowly 3-5-lobed and toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Pale pink to white, five-petalled, typical mallow flowers, borne singly or in small axillary clusters
botanical_description.root: Thick, fleshy, whitish taproot, rich in mucilage
botanical_description.fruit: Flat, disc-shaped cluster of small seeded segments ('cheeses')

habitat.text: Grows in damp meadows, salt marshes, ditches and riverbanks, especially near brackish or coastal wetlands; native to Europe, Western Asia and North Africa.

harvesting.text: The root is dug in autumn from plants at least two years old, when mucilage content is highest, then washed, peeled and dried quickly to prevent mould, given its high mucilage and sugar content.
harvesting.season: Autumn, from two-year-old (or older) plants

traditional_uses.text: Marshmallow root has an ancient reputation, reflected in its Greek name 'Althaea' (to heal), as a soothing demulcent for irritated mucous membranes: dry cough and sore throat, and mild inflammation and discomfort of the stomach and gut. It has also been applied topically as a soothing poultice for irritated skin.

preparations.name: Cold infusion (maceration)
preparations.text: Dried root macerated in cold water for several hours to extract the maximum mucilage without degrading it, then strained; the classic preparation for cough and digestive demulcent use.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried root gently simmered in water; a warmer alternative preparation, though prolonged heat can reduce mucilage yield.

preparations.name: Syrup
preparations.text: Root extract combined with sugar syrup as a soothing preparation for cough, especially in children.

dosage.name: Cold infusion (root)
dosage.text: The EU herbal monograph describes traditional use of about 0.5-3 g dried root per cup as a cold macerate, taken up to several times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Althaea officinalis root extract and its polysaccharides show chemopreventive/anticancer activity, including protection against chemically induced hepatocarcinogenesis (preclinical).

Antitussive (calms dry, irritating cough) - in animal studies the root rhamnogalacturonan suppresses the cough reflex dose-dependently, comparably to codeine, via 5-HT2 receptors rather than bronchodilation

Demulcent for irritation of the mouth or throat and associated dry cough (mucilage coats and soothes); Eases mild stomach and gut discomfort, including heartburn / acid reflux and indigestion (protective mucilage)
Topical soothing for irritated skin

indications.note: Eases mild stomach and gut discomfort, including heartburn / acid reflux and indigestion (protective mucilage)
indications.label: Acid reflux

indications.note: Althaea officinalis extract is chemoprotective against N-diethylnitrosamine-induced liver carcinogenesis, and marshmallow polysaccharides show documented anticancer activity (preclinical/chemopreventive).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Antitussive (calms dry, irritating cough) - in animal studies the root rhamnogalacturonan suppresses the cough reflex dose-dependently, comparably to codeine, via 5-HT2 receptors rather than bronchodilation; Demulcent for irritation of the mouth or throat and associated dry cough (mucilage coats and soothes)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Eases mild stomach and gut discomfort, including heartburn / acid reflux and indigestion (protective mucilage)
indications.label: Indigestion

contraindications.note: Very well tolerated. The mucilage can slow the absorption of other medicines, so take other drugs about an hour before or after marshmallow.
It may mildly lower blood sugar (caution alongside antidiabetic medicines). Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of adequate data.

constituents.name: Diterpenoid lactones
constituents.note: Andrographolide is the principal active compound; also neoandrographolide and deoxyandrographolide.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Polyphenols and other phenolics
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant activity.

botanical_description.text: Erect, branching annual herb with a slender, quadrangular (square-sectioned), dark green stem. The leaves are opposite, lance-shaped and glabrous, with a short stalk. Small, two-lipped, white flowers with purple-pink markings are borne in loose, spreading axillary and terminal racemes. The whole plant, and especially the leaves, is intensely and characteristically bitter.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, lance-shaped, glabrous, short-stalked
botanical_description.flowers: Small, two-lipped, white flowers marked with purple-pink, in loose spreading racemes
botanical_description.stem: Slender, quadrangular (square in cross-section), dark green, branching
botanical_description.fruit: Small, elastically dehiscent capsule

habitat.text: Native to India and Sri Lanka and widely distributed through South and Southeast Asia, growing wild in plains, hillsides, roadsides and cultivated fields; also grown commercially as a medicinal crop.

harvesting.text: The aerial parts (leaves and tender stems) are cut just before or at the onset of flowering, when andrographolide content is typically highest, and dried in shade to preserve the bitter diterpenoid lactones.
harvesting.season: Before to early flowering

traditional_uses.text: Andrographis, the 'king of bitters', is a cornerstone bitter tonic of Ayurvedic and traditional Chinese medicine (Chuan xin lian), used for fevers, colds, sore throat, infections and liver complaints. This traditional reputation for treating the common cold and upper respiratory infections is now supported by clinical trials of standardised extracts.

preparations.name: Infusion/decoction
preparations.text: Dried aerial parts infused or lightly decocted in hot water as a very bitter traditional fever and cold remedy.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Extract standardised to andrographolide content, taken as tablets or capsules; the form used in most clinical trials for cold/URTI symptom relief.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for upper respiratory infection commonly use extracts standardised to around 60-120 mg andrographolide daily, in divided doses, for up to 5-7 days. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Dried herb
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried aerial parts, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Andrographolide, the principal diterpene lactone of Andrographis paniculata, suppresses proliferation and induces apoptosis across breast, lung, colon, cervical, hepatic and haematological cancers via NF-kappaB and PI3K/Akt inhibition (preclinical).
Antimicrobial and antiviral
Antipyretic (fever)
Antimicrobial and antiviral
Immunostimulant / immune support

indications.note: Andrographolide (from Andrographis paniculata) acts as a chemopreventive and antitumour agent against breast, lung, colorectal, cervical, prostate and hepatocellular cancers and leukaemia, inducing apoptosis and inhibiting NF-kappaB and PI3K/AKT signalling (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Symptomatic relief of the common cold and acute upper respiratory tract infection (reduces symptom severity and duration)
indications.label: Cold & flu

indications.note: Symptomatic relief of the common cold and acute upper respiratory tract infection (reduces symptom severity and duration)
indications.label: Infection (general)

contraindications.note: Avoid in pregnancy and when trying to conceive: high doses have shown reproductive toxicity in animals and the herb has a traditional reputation as an abortifacient.
High doses have caused nephrotoxicity and reproductive toxicity in studies; use standardized products at recommended doses and avoid prolonged high-dose use.
May lower blood pressure and blood sugar and may interact with anticoagulant/antiplatelet, antihypertensive and immunosuppressant medicines; allergic reactions can occur.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic annual herb with a slender, hollow, ridged, blue-green stem. The leaves are finely divided into thread-like segments, feathery and soft, similar to fennel but more delicate. Small yellow flowers are borne in flat, wide compound umbels at the stem tips in summer, ripening into flattened, ridged, aromatic seeds (fruits).
botanical_description.leaves: Finely divided into thread-like, feathery segments
botanical_description.flowers: Small yellow flowers in flat, wide compound umbels
botanical_description.fruit: Small, flattened, ridged, aromatic seed (mericarp)

habitat.text: Believed native to the eastern Mediterranean, Western Asia and southern Russia; widely cultivated as a culinary and medicinal herb in gardens and fields across Europe, Asia and the Americas.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked fresh through the growing season; the seeds are harvested in late summer once the umbels have browned and dried, then threshed and dried further before storage.
harvesting.season: Leaf through summer; seed in late summer to early autumn

traditional_uses.text: Dill seed and leaf have a long culinary-medicinal history as a carminative digestive remedy for colic, dyspepsia and flatulence, and as a traditional lactagogue (galactagogue) to support breastfeeding; dill water ('gripe water') is a classic infant remedy for colic.

preparations.name: Infusion (seed)
preparations.text: Crushed dill seed infused in hot water as a traditional carminative digestive tea.

preparations.name: Infusion (leaf)
preparations.text: Fresh or dried leaf infused as a milder digestive tea, also used culinarily.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the seed; used in low dilution for its aromatic carminative and antimicrobial properties.

dosage.name: Seed infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-3 g of crushed seed per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture
dosage.text: About 1-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (carvone, limonene)
constituents.note: Seed essential oil dominated by the monoterpene ketone carvone and limonene, giving dill its characteristic aroma and much of its carminative and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoids and phenolics identified in metabolomic analysis of leaf and seed extracts.

actions.note: Anethum graveolens (dill) extract and essential oil induce ROS-mediated apoptosis in melanoma and lung adenocarcinoma cells (preclinical).

indications.note: Anethum graveolens (dill) extract induces ROS-mediated apoptosis in HT-144 melanoma cells, and its essential oil is selectively cytotoxic and pro-apoptotic against A549 lung adenocarcinoma cells (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

contraindications.note: Widely used as a food herb/spice; WHO monograph notes a “generally regarded as safe” status as a flavouring agent (World Health Organization, 2007). Allergic reactions (including oral itching, swelling, urticaria, vomiting/diarrhoea) are reported; avoid if you react to Apiaceae-family plants (World Health Organization, 2007; Egyptian Drug Authority, n.d.).

Duke (2002) provides clinical evidence (score 2) for dill's antibacterial and antispasmodic activities, consistent with Commission E and PhEur approvals. Traditional uses include management of colic, dyspepsia, and flatulence. The plant is a well-documented lactagogue (promotes milk production) and has a mild hypotensive effect. Dose: 1–3 g seeds as tea three times daily. Dill is generally safe (++) with no known significant contraindications at culinary and medicinal doses (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young feathery, finely divided thread-like leaves and the umbels of tiny white flowers, which closely resemble dill; and poison-hemlock volunteer seedlings appearing among planted dill.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) is the classic fatal mimic of feathery parsley-family herbs. Washington State University Extension states poison hemlock's tiny white flowers form an umbel similar to dill, parsnip or carrot, and its finely divided fern-like foliage looks much like dill weed; a poison-hemlock seedling volunteering among garden dill, or a forager mistaking wild hemlock for dill, is a realistic and potentially lethal hazard. Conium contains coniine and kills by ascending paralysis and respiratory failure - a few leaves or seeds can be fatal.
