Documented dill-specific poisonings are sparse (most fatal hemlock confusions involve carrot, parsnip or fennel), so the value of this record is the rock-solid tests below rather than a claim that this exact swap is common.
distinguishing_features: SMELL (decisive): crushed dill smells fresh, sweet and unmistakably of dill. Poison hemlock smells rank, musty and mouse-like, with no dill scent.
Stem: poison hemlock has a hairless, hollow stem conspicuously blotched and streaked with red-purple, especially near the base. Dill stems are plain blue-green and never carry purple blotches.
Stature: poison hemlock is a tall (often 1.5-3 m) branching biennial; cultivated dill is a slender annual usually under 1 m. A large fern-leaved umbellifer with a purple-blotched stem is not dill.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush a leaf and smell it, and look at the stem. A fresh sweet dill scent with a plain blue-green stem = dill. A musty/mousy smell and/or a hairless stem with red-purple blotches = poison hemlock - do not eat. If either warning sign is present, discard it.

constituents.name: Coumarins and furanocoumarins
constituents.note: Including columbianetin, angelicin, imperatorin and xanthotoxin.

constituents.name: Essential (volatile) oil
constituents.note: Rich in monoterpenes such as alpha-pinene and beta-phellandrene; gives the aromatic, carminative character.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids and flavonoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Tall, robust biennial or short-lived perennial herb with a stout, hollow, ridged stem, often tinged purple. The leaves are large, bright green, 2-3-pinnately divided into broad, coarsely toothed leaflets, borne on inflated, sheathing stalks. Large, rounded compound umbels of small greenish-white to yellow-green flowers are borne at the stem tips, followed by pale, ribbed, winged fruit. The whole plant is strongly and pleasantly aromatic (sweet, celery- or juniper-like) when crushed.
botanical_description.habit: Tall, robust biennial to short-lived perennial herb
botanical_description.leaves: Large, 2-3-pinnately divided, coarsely toothed leaflets on inflated sheathing stalks
botanical_description.flowers: Large, rounded compound umbels of small greenish-white to yellow-green flowers
botanical_description.stem: Stout, hollow, ridged, often purple-tinged, hairless
botanical_description.fruit: Pale, ribbed, winged fruit (mericarp)

habitat.text: Native to northern and eastern Europe and parts of Asia, growing in damp meadows, riverbanks, ditches and woodland margins on moist, rich soils; also widely cultivated as a culinary and medicinal herb.

harvesting.text: The root is dug in autumn of the first year (or spring of the second, before flowering), when its aromatic oil content is highest; the ripe fruit/seed is collected in late summer as the umbels dry; leaves for standardised extract are picked through the growing season. Careful identification against deadly Apiaceae look-alikes (poison hemlock, water hemlock, hemlock water-dropwort) is essential before wild-harvesting.
harvesting.season: Root in autumn/early spring; fruit and seed in late summer; leaf through the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Angelica root has a long Northern European tradition as an aromatic bitter and carminative digestive remedy for indigestion, bloating and flatulence, as a diaphoretic and expectorant support during colds, and the seed/fruit as a calming, anxiolytic nervine. It has also been candied and used as a flavouring.

preparations.name: Decoction/infusion (root)
preparations.text: Dried root decocted or infused in hot water as a bitter digestive and carminative tea.

preparations.name: Infusion (seed)
preparations.text: Crushed fruit/seed infused as a calming, carminative tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root, typically around 1:5.

preparations.name: Standardised leaf extract
preparations.text: Concentrated leaf extract studied in a pilot clinical trial for overactive bladder symptoms.

dosage.name: Root infusion/decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried root per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 1-3 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory and gastroprotective
Angelica archangelica fruit tincture and its furanocoumarins (imperatorin, xanthotoxin) show antiproliferative activity against pancreatic and larynx cancer and rhabdomyosarcoma cells (preclinical).
Anticonvulsant and cognition-supporting (preclinical)
Anxiolytic / sedative (anxiety, nervous tension)
Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)
Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)
Diaphoretic / expectorant support during colds
Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)
Diaphoretic / expectorant support during colds
Anti-inflammatory and gastroprotective
Anticonvulsant and cognition-supporting (preclinical)
Anxiolytic / sedative (anxiety, nervous tension)

indications.note: Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)
indications.label: Bloating

indications.note: Angelica archangelica fruit tincture is antiproliferative against PANC-1 pancreatic cancer cells; its furanocoumarins imperatorin and xanthotoxin drive this activity and inhibit larynx cancer and rhabdomyosarcoma cell growth (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

indications.note: Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)
indications.label: Indigestion

indications.note: Overactive bladder - a pilot RCT of an Angelica archangelica leaf extract reduced daytime voids and storage symptoms and improved quality of life, with near-significant benefit for nocturia
indications.label: Urinary support

contraindications.note: Contains furanocoumarins, which can cause photosensitivity; avoid excessive sun or UV exposure while using the herb medicinally.
Medicinal doses should be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

Coumarin constituents may interact with anticoagulant medicines, and furanocoumarins with photosensitising drugs; when wild-harvesting, take great care to distinguish garden angelica from poisonous Apiaceae look-alikes such as hemlock.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Angelica is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The tall hollow stems, ferny leaves and white umbels — the parts cut for candied or cooked stem or leaf.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Poison hemlock is the classic deadly umbellifer confused with edible ones. It shares angelica's tall hollow stem and white umbels, and both can show purple on the stem — but hemlock's is discrete spots and blotches while angelica's is an even flush. Coniine causes a paralysis that stops breathing; a mouthful can be lethal.
distinguishing_features: Stem markings: poison hemlock has distinct purple-red spots and blotches scattered on a smooth, hairless stem; angelica's stem is uniformly green or evenly purple-flushed, never discretely spotted.
Smell: crushed angelica is strongly and pleasantly aromatic (sweet, celery- or juniper-like); crushed poison hemlock smells rank and mousy. A foul mousy smell is a hard no.
Leaf cut: hemlock leaves are finely divided and fern-like; angelica leaflets are broad, large, bright green and coarsely toothed.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Inspect the lower stem in good light: discrete purple-red spots or blotches on a smooth, hairless stem = poison hemlock — do not eat. Angelica's purple, if any, is an even flush, and it smells sweetly aromatic, never mousy.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Wet-habitat plants: the rootstock and lower stem, plus the tall hollow stems and white umbels.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Water hemlock is among the most violently toxic temperate plants; its cicutoxin causes sudden severe convulsions, often fatal. Its roots have been eaten by mistake for 'wild carrot' and other roots, and it shares angelica's wet, streamside habitat.
distinguishing_features: Cut the rootstock lengthwise: water hemlock's is hollow and chambered (with horizontal cross-partitions) and exudes a yellowish oily juice; angelica's root is solid. This is the single most reliable trait.
Water hemlock's lateral leaf-veins run to the notches between the marginal teeth ('vein to the cut'); angelica's veins run to the tips of the teeth.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Slice the rootstock lengthwise: a hollow, horizontally chambered root with a yellowish oily juice = water hemlock — do not eat. Angelica's root is solid.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Wet-habitat plant: the fleshy root tubers and celery- or parsley-like leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Hemlock water-dropwort is one of the deadliest plants in Britain and western Europe; its oenanthotoxin causes violent convulsions, and its pleasant-smelling fleshy roots and celery-like leaves are notoriously mistaken for edible wild vegetables. It shares angelica's wet streamside habitat.
distinguishing_features: Hemlock water-dropwort has a cluster of pale, fleshy, finger-like root tubers ('dead man's fingers'); angelica has a single solid taproot, not a bunch of tubers.
Its roots can smell pleasant and sweetish, so never use smell to clear it — go by the root form and the wet habitat.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Examine the roots: a cluster of pale, fleshy, finger-like tubers in wet ground = hemlock water-dropwort — do not eat. Angelica has one solid taproot. Because its roots can smell pleasant, never rely on smell here.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The huge hollow stems and large divided leaves of a tall umbellifer.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Giant hogweed has the same broad silhouette as angelica (very tall hollow stem, big divided leaves, large white umbels) and grows in similar damp margins. Its main hazard is contact, not eating: its sap contains furanocoumarins that make skin blister and burn severely in sunlight (phytophotodermatitis), leaving long-lasting scars.
distinguishing_features: Size: giant hogweed is enormous — 2 to 5 m tall, with leaves up to 1 to 1.5 m and umbels up to about 50 cm across — dwarfing angelica.
Stem surface: hogweed stems are green with purple blotches AND covered in coarse, stiff white bristles; angelica's stem is smooth and hairless.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: A massive (over about 2 m) stem with purple blotches AND coarse white bristly hairs = giant hogweed — do not cut or handle it with bare skin, because its sap causes severe sunlight burns. Angelica's stem is smooth and hairless.

constituents.name: Inulin-type fructans and caffeoylquinic acids
constituents.note: Burdock root is a rich source of prebiotic inulin-type fructans (fructooligosaccharides) along with chlorogenic and caffeic acids, the lignans arctiin and arctigenin, cynarine and quercetin, which underlie its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and gut-supporting activity.

botanical_description.text: Robust biennial herb forming a large rosette of huge, heart-shaped (rhubarb-like), long-stalked leaves, dark green above and whitish-woolly beneath, in its first year. In the second year it sends up a tall, branching, grooved flowering stem bearing purple thistle-like flower heads enclosed in a globe of stiff, hooked bracts that ripen into the familiar clinging burrs.
botanical_description.habit: Robust biennial herb; large rosette in year one, tall branching stem in year two
botanical_description.leaves: Huge, heart-shaped, long-stalked, dark green above and whitish-woolly beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Purple thistle-like flower heads enclosed in a globe of stiff, hooked bracts
botanical_description.stem: Tall, branching, grooved flowering stem (second year only)
botanical_description.fruit: Bristly, hooked burr enclosing small achenes, clinging to fur and clothing

habitat.text: A common weed of disturbed ground, waste places, roadsides, field margins and riverbanks on rich, moist soils; native to Europe and temperate Asia and naturalised in North America and elsewhere.

harvesting.text: The root is dug at the end of the first growing season (autumn) or early in the second spring, before the flowering stem develops, when it is at its most tender and richest in inulin; leaves are picked through the first year, and the seed/fruit is collected from the dried burrs in the second autumn. Careful identification against deadly nightshade, whose root can resemble burdock, is essential before digging.
harvesting.season: Root at the end of year one or start of year two; leaf through year one; seed in year-two autumn

traditional_uses.text: Burdock root is a classic 'alterative' or blood-purifying herb of European and East Asian (gobo) traditional medicine, used for chronic skin conditions, as a mild diuretic supporting elimination, and as a digestive bitter; the root is also eaten as a root vegetable (gobo) in Japanese cuisine.

preparations.name: Decoction (root)
preparations.text: Dried or fresh root simmered in water as a traditional alterative and digestive decoction.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root, typically around 1:5.

preparations.name: Fresh root (culinary)
preparations.text: Fresh root peeled and cooked as a root vegetable (gobo), continuing the traditional food-medicine use.

dosage.name: Root decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried root per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Burdock (Arctium lappa) extracts and the lignan arctigenin inhibit proliferation and induce apoptosis in colon, breast, lung and gastric cancer cells, attenuate melanoma progression and reduce preneoplastic colon lesions in vivo (preclinical).

indications.note: Arctium lappa root reduces diet-induced preneoplastic colon lesions and suppresses melanoma progression; its lignan arctigenin induces apoptosis in colon (HT-29), breast (in vitro/in vivo), lung and gastric cancer cells and chemosensitizes them to doxorubicin (preclinical).
indications.label: Cancer (anticancer research)

contraindications.note: Generally safe as a food plant. Allergic reactions are possible in those sensitive to Asteraceae. Avoid in pregnancy due to uterine-stimulating effects reported in animal studies. The burs (seed heads) can cause mechanical irritation of skin and eyes on contact. Not recommended in biliary obstruction.

Duke (2002) rates burdock as +++ and notes antibacterial, antimutagenic, antitumor, choleretic, diuretic, and prebiotic (bifidogenic) activities at the experimental level (score 1). The root is rich in inulin (a prebiotic fructooligosaccharide), which promotes growth of beneficial intestinal bacteria. Dose: 1–2 g dried root in tea three times daily, or 2–4 ml of tincture. Duke notes burdock's general safety, though it belongs to the Asteraceae family and can trigger reactions in sensitive individuals. It should be distinguished from belladonna and similar plants, which resemble it in early growth (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: First-year taproots dug for use as food or 'burdock root tea'; the root of deadly nightshade closely resembles a burdock root and the two grow as weeds in the same rough ground.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Greater burdock (Arctium lappa) is dug for its long first-year taproot, used as a vegetable and as 'burdock root tea.' Deadly nightshade (Atropa belladonna) grows as a weed in the same disturbed ground and its root closely resembles a burdock root, so belladonna root can be gathered or processed in mistake for burdock. This has caused real poisonings: a JAMA case report (Bryson et al., 1978) describes atropine poisoning from a commercial burdock root tea, and belladonna-contaminated herbal teas have caused severe anticholinergic poisoning from as little as a few milligrams of atropine. Belladonna contains tropane alkaloids (atropine, hyoscyamine, scopolamine); poisoning causes dry flushed skin, widely dilated pupils, blurred vision, fever, a racing heart, agitation, hallucinations, seizures and can be fatal, especially in children.
distinguishing_features: Whole plant: only dig burdock when you can match the whole living plant - burdock has huge rhubarb-like heart-shaped leaves that are whitish-woolly underneath and, in its second year, the familiar hooked burrs. Deadly nightshade is a branching plant with dull green oval pointed leaves, solitary purple-brown bell-shaped flowers and shiny black cherry-sized berries.

Berries (decisive if present): burdock never bears berries - it makes bristly burrs. A plant near your 'burdock' bearing shiny black berries in green star-shaped calyces is deadly nightshade - do not dig roots from that ground.
Best practice: because the roots alone are hard to tell apart, do not wild-harvest burdock root unless an expert has confirmed the living plant - buy it from a reputable supplier instead.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Identify the whole living plant before digging any root. Confirm large rhubarb-like leaves that are woolly-white underneath (and hooked burrs in the second year) for burdock. If there are shiny black berries, purple-brown bell-shaped flowers or oval pointed leaves among the plants, deadly nightshade may be present - do not dig or brew root tea from there. When in doubt, buy burdock rather than foraging the root.

constituents.name: Inulin (prebiotic fructan, up to ~50% of the root)
constituents.note: The major storage carbohydrate; a prebiotic fibre.

constituents.name: Lignans (arctigenin, arctiin)
constituents.note: Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Polyphenolic acids (caffeic, chlorogenic), polyacetylenes and tannins
constituents.note: Antimicrobial and antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Biennial herb closely related to greater burdock, forming a first-year rosette of large, heart-shaped, long-stalked leaves, green above and grey-woolly beneath, generally smaller than Arctium lappa. In the second year it produces a branching flowering stem with smaller purple thistle-like flower heads in a looser, more sprawling habit, each enclosed in a globe of hooked bracts that ripen into clinging burrs.
botanical_description.habit: Biennial herb; first-year rosette, sprawling branched second-year stem
botanical_description.leaves: Large, heart-shaped, long-stalked, green above and grey-woolly beneath (smaller than A. lappa)
botanical_description.flowers: Smaller purple thistle-like flower heads in a looser, sprawling arrangement, enclosed in hooked bracts
botanical_description.stem: Branching, more sprawling than A. lappa (second year only)
botanical_description.fruit: Bristly, hooked burr enclosing small achenes

habitat.text: A common weed of waste ground, roadsides, hedgerows and disturbed soil; native to Europe and widely naturalised in North America, where it is often the most common wild burdock species.

harvesting.text: The root is dug at the end of the first growing season or early in the second spring, before flowering; leaf may be gathered through the first year. Careful identification against deadly nightshade, whose root can resemble burdock, is essential before digging.
harvesting.season: End of year one or start of year two, before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Lesser burdock is used interchangeably with the better-studied Arctium lappa in Western herbal medicine, as a traditional alterative/depurative for chronic skin conditions such as eczema, psoriasis and acne, and as a mild diuretic supporting elimination.

preparations.name: Decoction (root)
preparations.text: Dried or fresh root simmered in water as a traditional alterative and skin-support decoction.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root, typically around 1:5.

preparations.name: Topical wash
preparations.text: Cooled decoction used as a topical wash for eczema, acne and other skin irritation, reflecting the traditional external alterative use.

dosage.name: Root decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried root per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Traditional 'alterative' / depurative for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, acne and boils (internally and topically)
Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant
Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant
Mild diuretic supporting elimination ('detox', traditional)

indications.note: Sebaceous-gland regulator - helps balance skin oil (acne); Traditional 'alterative' / depurative for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, acne and boils (internally and topically)
indications.label: Acne

indications.note: Mild diuretic supporting elimination ('detox', traditional); Traditional 'alterative' / depurative for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, acne and boils (internally and topically)
indications.label: Detox / cleansing

indications.note: Traditional 'alterative' / depurative for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, acne and boils (internally and topically)
indications.label: Eczema

indications.note: Traditional 'alterative' / depurative for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, acne and boils (internally and topically)
indications.label: Psoriasis

contraindications.note: As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae), it can cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to ragweed and related plants. The high inulin content can cause temporary bloating or gas.
May lower blood sugar (caution with antidiabetic medicines) and add to diuretics; avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: First-year taproots dug for use as food or 'burdock root tea'; the root of deadly nightshade closely resembles a burdock root and the two grow as weeds in the same rough ground.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Lesser burdock (Arctium minus) is dug for its long first-year taproot, used as a vegetable and as 'burdock root tea.' Deadly nightshade (Atropa belladonna) grows as a weed in the same disturbed ground and its root closely resembles a burdock root, so belladonna root can be gathered or processed in mistake for burdock. This has caused real poisonings: a JAMA case report (Bryson et al., 1978) describes atropine poisoning from a commercial burdock root tea, and belladonna-contaminated herbal teas have caused severe anticholinergic poisoning from as little as a few milligrams of atropine. Belladonna contains tropane alkaloids (atropine, hyoscyamine, scopolamine); poisoning causes dry flushed skin, widely dilated pupils, blurred vision, fever, a racing heart, agitation, hallucinations, seizures and can be fatal, especially in children.
distinguishing_features: Whole plant: only dig burdock when you can match the whole living plant - burdock has huge rhubarb-like heart-shaped leaves that are whitish-woolly underneath and, in its second year, the familiar hooked burrs. Deadly nightshade is a branching plant with dull green oval pointed leaves, solitary purple-brown bell-shaped flowers and shiny black cherry-sized berries.

Berries (decisive if present): burdock never bears berries - it makes bristly burrs. A plant near your 'burdock' bearing shiny black berries in green star-shaped calyces is deadly nightshade - do not dig roots from that ground.
Best practice: because the roots alone are hard to tell apart, do not wild-harvest burdock root unless an expert has confirmed the living plant - buy it from a reputable supplier instead.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Identify the whole living plant before digging any root. Confirm large rhubarb-like leaves that are woolly-white underneath (and hooked burrs in the second year) for burdock. If there are shiny black berries, purple-brown bell-shaped flowers or oval pointed leaves among the plants, deadly nightshade may be present - do not dig or brew root tea from there. When in doubt, buy burdock rather than foraging the root.

constituents.name: Arbutin (and methylarbutin)
constituents.note: Hydroquinone glycoside; hydrolysed and excreted as antibacterial hydroquinone in the urine.

constituents.name: Tannins (gallotannins)
constituents.note: Up to ~20%; responsible for the astringency and for gastric irritation at high doses.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents of the leaf.

botanical_description.text: Low, mat-forming evergreen shrub with trailing, woody stems that root as they spread. The leaves are small, leathery, spatula-shaped (spoon-shaped), glossy dark green above and paler beneath, with a smooth, untoothed margin and a rounded tip. Small, urn-shaped, pink-and-white flowers hang in short clusters in spring, followed by bright red, mealy berries.
botanical_description.leaves: Small, leathery, spatula-shaped, glossy dark green above, entire (untoothed) margin, rounded tip
botanical_description.flowers: Small, urn-shaped, pink-and-white, in short drooping clusters
botanical_description.stem: Trailing, woody, rooting as it spreads
botanical_description.root: Woody, fibrous roots along trailing stems
botanical_description.fruit: Bright red, mealy berry

habitat.text: Grows on dry, acidic, sandy or rocky ground in open woodland, heath, moorland and mountain slopes across the northern hemisphere (Europe, Asia and North America), often in exposed, cold, upland or subarctic sites.

harvesting.text: The leaves are gathered in autumn to early spring (outside the flowering and fruiting season), when arbutin content is at its highest, and dried carefully to preserve the glycoside.
harvesting.season: Autumn to early spring

traditional_uses.text: Bearberry leaf is a long-established European folk and official (EU herbal monograph) remedy for mild, uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections and cystitis, used short-term as a urinary antiseptic; it has also been used traditionally as an astringent for mild diarrhoea.

preparations.name: Cold infusion (maceration)
preparations.text: Dried leaf macerated in cold water for several hours, which extracts less tannin (reducing gastric irritation) than a hot infusion; the preferred traditional preparation.

preparations.name: Infusion/decoction
preparations.text: Dried leaf infused or lightly decocted in hot water; alkalinising the urine (e.g. with dietary measures) is traditionally recommended alongside use, since arbutin's antibacterial action requires alkaline urine.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried leaf.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: The EU herbal monograph describes traditional use of about 3 g dried leaf per cup, up to four times daily, for no more than one week at a time and no more than about five times a year. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antibacterial in the urinary tract (arbutin is converted to antibacterial hydroquinone, favoured by alkaline urine); Urinary antiseptic for mild, uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection / cystitis (short-term)
Astringent (tannins)

Antibacterial in the urinary tract (arbutin is converted to antibacterial hydroquinone, favoured by alkaline urine); Urinary antiseptic for mild, uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection / cystitis (short-term)

indications.note: Urinary antiseptic for mild, uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection / cystitis (short-term)
indications.label: Infection (general)

indications.note: Antibacterial in the urinary tract (arbutin is converted to antibacterial hydroquinone, favoured by alkaline urine); Urinary antiseptic for mild, uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection / cystitis (short-term)
indications.label: Urinary tract infection (UTI)

contraindications.note: Short-term use only: not for more than 1 week at a time and not more than about 5 times per year. If symptoms persist beyond ~4 days, worsen, or are accompanied by fever, blood in the urine or pain, seek medical care.
Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding and not for use in children or adolescents under 18 years.

Works best when the urine is alkaline; avoid taking with substances that acidify the urine. The hydroquinone released can be irritant/potentially hepatotoxic, so prolonged or repeated use must be avoided.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Small leathery evergreen leaves gathered as the medicinal 'uva-ursi'; the poisonous leaves of box look similar and have been used to adulterate bearberry.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Bearberry (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi) leaves are collected as a urinary herb. As documented in pharmacognosy, during collection bearberry is commonly confused with cowberry (edible) and with box (Buxus sempervirens), and poisonous box - which lacks bearberry's active arbutin - has occasionally been used to adulterate the drug. Box leaves are poisonous, containing steroidal alkaloids that cause vomiting, diarrhoea, dizziness and, in quantity, convulsions and respiratory failure. Because the small leathery evergreen leaves are similar, box is a genuine toxic adulterant/look-alike of bearberry.
distinguishing_features: Leaf apex (decisive): bearberry leaves are spatulate with an entire (untoothed) margin and a rounded apex. Box leaves have a small notch cut out at the tip (emarginate).
Leaf underside: box leaves have a loose, separable epidermis on the underside and lack the astringency of bearberry; bearberry leaves are firm and astringent with a net of sunken veins.
Source: buy uva-ursi only from suppliers who authenticate the leaf, since adulteration with box cannot be judged casually.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Check the leaf tip. Bearberry leaves are spoon-shaped with a smooth margin and a ROUNDED tip. A leaf with a notch cut out of the tip (and a loose skin on the underside) is box - poisonous, not bearberry. Use only authenticated uva-ursi.

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (helenalin, dihydrohelenalin)
constituents.note: The main anti-inflammatory principles; also responsible for the plant's toxicity and contact-allergy potential.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Essential oil, phenolic acids and coumarins
constituents.note: Aromatic and supporting constituents of the flower.

constituents.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (trace)
constituents.note: Present in small amounts; relevant to the caution against internal use.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic mountain perennial herb with a basal rosette of oval, hairy leaves and one or two pairs of smaller opposite leaves on an erect, softly hairy flowering stem. Each stem bears one or a few large, bright golden-yellow, daisy-like flower heads with narrow, slightly twisted ray florets.
botanical_description.leaves: Basal rosette of oval, hairy leaves; one or two pairs of smaller opposite stem leaves
botanical_description.flowers: Large, bright golden-yellow, daisy-like flower heads with narrow, slightly twisted ray florets
botanical_description.stem: Erect, softly hairy, sparsely leaved flowering stem
botanical_description.root: Short, blackish rhizome with fibrous roots
botanical_description.fruit: Small, hairy achene with a pappus

habitat.text: Grows in acidic, nutrient-poor mountain meadows, pastures and open woodland, typically at moderate to high elevations across central and northern Europe; increasingly scarce in the wild due to habitat loss and overharvesting.

harvesting.text: The flower heads are picked by hand as they open, in mid- to late summer, and dried quickly in a warm, shaded, airy place; wild populations are protected in many regions, so cultivated or sustainably wildcrafted material is preferred.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer, as flowers open

traditional_uses.text: Arnica flower has a long central European folk reputation, reflected in the name 'leopard's bane' and 'mountain tobacco', as a topical remedy for bruising, sprains, muscle soreness and swelling after minor injury, applied only to unbroken skin. Internal use of the whole herb has always been considered dangerous and is confined to highly diluted homeopathic preparations.

preparations.name: Topical cream/gel/ointment
preparations.text: Standardised flower extract formulated into a cream, gel or ointment for external application to bruises, sprains and muscle soreness on unbroken skin.

preparations.name: Diluted tincture (topical)
preparations.text: Alcoholic tincture of the dried flower heads, diluted before topical application as a compress; never taken internally in this form.

preparations.name: Infused oil
preparations.text: Flower heads infused in a carrier oil for topical massage use.

dosage.name: Topical cream/gel
dosage.text: Applied to the affected area 2-3 times daily on unbroken skin only, per product labelling. Educational reference only, not a prescription. Never taken by mouth except as a highly diluted homeopathic preparation.

actions.note: Topical analgesic for muscle soreness and pain
Topical anti-inflammatory for bruising, swelling and sprains
Antimicrobial and antioxidant (in vitro)
Antimicrobial and antioxidant (in vitro)
Antiecchymotic / vulnerary (helps disperse bruises)

indications.note: Antiecchymotic / vulnerary (helps disperse bruises); Topical anti-inflammatory for bruising, swelling and sprains
indications.label: Bruising

indications.note: Topical analgesic for muscle soreness and pain
indications.label: Muscle soreness

indications.note: Topical analgesic for muscle soreness and pain
indications.label: Pain (general)

indications.note: Topical anti-inflammatory for bruising, swelling and sprains
indications.label: Swelling / fluid retention

contraindications.note: EXTERNAL USE ONLY on unbroken skin. Arnica is toxic if swallowed and can cause stomach pain, vomiting, diarrhoea, breathing difficulty, irregular heartbeat and, in serious cases, cardiac arrest; the only safe oral form is the highly diluted homeopathic preparation.
Do not apply to broken or damaged skin, open wounds, eyes or mucous membranes.
As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae) it can cause allergic contact dermatitis; prolonged topical use can also irritate the skin.
Avoid medicinal use during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic, silvery-grey perennial herb with finely divided, silky-hairy leaves and numerous small, pale yellow, drooping, globe-shaped flower heads borne in loose branched panicles. The whole plant has a characteristic intensely bitter taste and a pungent, sage-like aroma.
botanical_description.leaves: Finely divided (2-3-pinnatisect), silky-hairy, silvery-grey
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale yellow, drooping, globe-shaped flower heads in loose branched panicles
botanical_description.stem: Erect, ridged, silvery-hairy, woody at the base
botanical_description.fruit: Small achene without pappus

habitat.text: Grows on dry, sunny, stony or waste ground, roadsides and field margins; native to Europe, North Africa and temperate Asia, and naturalised in North America.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaf, stem and flower) are cut as flowering begins, in mid- to late summer, and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer, at the start of flowering

traditional_uses.text: Wormwood is one of the classic bitter digestive herbs of European tradition, used to stimulate appetite and bile flow for dyspeptic complaints, and historically as a vermifuge (its common name reflects traditional use against intestinal worms) and in the production of absinthe and vermouth.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused briefly in hot water as a very bitter digestive tea, taken before meals to stimulate appetite.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb, used in small bitter doses.

dosage.name: Short-term use only
dosage.text: Because of its thujone content, traditional herbal references limit internal use to small bitter doses (around 0.5-1 g dried herb per cup) for no more than about 4 weeks at a time. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (absinthin, artabsin)
constituents.note: Intensely bitter sesquiterpene lactones responsible for the classic digestive-bitter action.

constituents.name: Essential oil (thujone, other monoterpenes)
constituents.note: Volatile oil containing thujone (alpha- and beta-isomers), the neurotoxic ketone that limits safe internal use, along with other monoterpenes.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents contributing to the plant's antioxidant activity.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: CAUTION: Contains thujone, which is neurotoxic in significant amounts. Internal use should be limited in duration and dose — short-term use only (under 4 weeks). Contraindicated in pregnancy (powerful uterine stimulant). Avoid in epilepsy, renal disease, and in children. The small amounts present in properly prepared bitter tinctures and vermouth are generally considered safe. The thujone content of absinthe was historically exaggerated.

Duke (2002) rates wormwood as + (use with caution) and provides clinical evidence (score 2) for use as an aperitif and bitter digestive tonic, consistent with Commission E approval for dyspeptic complaints and loss of appetite. The plant contains absinthin and artabsin (bitter sesquiterpene lactones) responsible for digestive stimulation, and also thujone — a neurotoxic ketone that is the active agent responsible for 'absinthism' (historical neurological syndrome linked to absinthe consumption). Duke strongly cautions that thujone content limits safe long-term use, and wormwood should not be taken internally for more than 4 weeks at a time. Strictly contraindicated in pregnancy (abortifacient, neurotoxic) and epilepsy (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Tall, aromatic perennial herb with deeply lobed, dark green leaves that are distinctively silvery-white and woolly beneath. Small, dull reddish-brown to yellowish flower heads are borne in dense, branched, leafy spikes. The reddish-brown, ridged stems and the leaf's contrasting silver underside are characteristic field marks.
botanical_description.leaves: Deeply lobed, dark green above, distinctively silvery-white woolly beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, dull reddish-brown to yellowish flower heads in dense, branched, leafy spikes
botanical_description.stem: Reddish-brown, ridged, often woody at the base
botanical_description.fruit: Small achene without pappus

habitat.text: A common plant of waste ground, roadsides, hedgerows and riverbanks; native to Europe, Asia and North Africa and widely naturalised in North America.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaf and stem) are cut in summer as flowering begins and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place; the root can be dug in autumn.
harvesting.season: Summer for aerial parts; autumn for root

traditional_uses.text: Mugwort is a widely used traditional bitter digestive tonic and calming nervine, and has a long reputation as an emmenagogue for menstrual complaints (reflected in its traditional use to bring on delayed menstruation, which is also why it is contraindicated in pregnancy). It has also been burned as 'moxa' in East Asian moxibustion and used as an insect repellent ('sailor's tobacco').

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried leaf and flowering tops infused in hot water as a bitter digestive and calming tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb.

preparations.name: Moxa (external heat therapy)
preparations.text: Dried, processed leaf ('moxa') burned near or on the skin over acupuncture points in traditional East Asian moxibustion.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily, for short-term use. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Sesquiterpenoids and triterpenoids
constituents.note: Multiple undescribed and known sesquiterpenoids and triterpenoids isolated from the leaves, several with anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Essential oil (thujone, cineole, camphor)
constituents.note: Volatile oil containing thujone and other monoterpenes; thujone content is the basis of the caution on prolonged internal use.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and coumarins
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory flavonoid and coumarin constituents.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Contains thujone; avoid high doses or prolonged use. Contraindicated in pregnancy (uterine stimulant and abortifacient). May cause allergic reactions in individuals sensitive to the Asteraceae family. Avoid in epilepsy. Not recommended for children.

Duke (2002) rates mugwort as + (use with caution) and notes abortifacient (score 1), emmenagogue, aperitif (score 1), and insecticide activities. Duke clearly flags mugwort as contraindicated in pregnancy due to well-documented abortifacient and uterotonic properties. Traditional use has been as a digestive bitter, and the plant contains volatile oils, sesquiterpene lactones, and flavonoids. Duke notes that the plant may cause allergic reactions in individuals sensitive to Asteraceae and warns against long-term use due to potential neurotoxicity from thujone-containing essential oil (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Mugwort is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young deeply lobed leaves gathered for use; young monkshood - the most poisonous plant in Europe - has similar deeply divided leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris) is foraged for its aromatic, deeply lobed leaves. As Totally Wild UK warns, it would be a very deadly mistake to confuse young mugwort with monkshood (Aconitum napellus), whose young leaves are similarly deeply divided. Monkshood is considered the most poisonous plant in Europe: all parts contain aconitine, the lethal dose is only 2-6 mg, and poisoning can kill within hours through respiratory and cardiac failure - the toxin is even absorbed through the skin. Although monkshood is less common, its extreme toxicity makes this confusion critical to avoid.
distinguishing_features: Leaf underside (decisive): mugwort leaves have a distinctive silvery-white woolly underside. Monkshood leaves are green and hairless on both sides, with no silver felt. No silver underside = not mugwort.
Flowers: mugwort has small, dull reddish-brown flower spikes; monkshood has tall spikes of unmistakable hooded blue-purple flowers.
Handling: do not handle a suspected monkshood with bare hands - aconitine can be absorbed through the skin.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Turn the leaf over. A silvery-white woolly underside (with small dull flowers) = mugwort. Green, hairless undersides - and especially any hooded blue-purple flowers - mean monkshood, the most poisonous plant in Europe; do not gather or even handle it with bare hands.

constituents.name: Cardiac glycosides (cardenolides such as asclepin, calactin and calotropin)
constituents.note: Heart-active compounds that make dosing and safety critical.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and amino acids
constituents.note: Supporting constituents of the root.

botanical_description.text: Erect perennial herb with hairy, unbranched stems and narrow, lance-shaped, alternate leaves (unlike most milkweeds, its sap is clear rather than milky). Vivid orange-red flowers are borne in showy, flat-topped umbel-like clusters at the stem tips, making it a prized butterfly and pollinator plant. The stout, fibrous, branching root is the medicinal part.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, alternate
botanical_description.flowers: Vivid orange-red flowers in showy, flat-topped umbel-like clusters
botanical_description.stem: Erect, hairy, unbranched, exuding clear (not milky) sap when cut
botanical_description.fruit: Spindle-shaped seed pod (follicle) releasing silky-tufted seeds

habitat.text: Native to dry prairies, open fields, roadsides and sandy or rocky open ground across most of eastern and central North America.

harvesting.text: The root is dug in autumn after the plant has finished flowering and seeding, then cleaned and dried; given its cardiac glycoside content and near-absence of modern safety data, harvesting and use should only be undertaken with expert guidance.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Pleurisy root has a Native American and Eclectic-medicine reputation, reflected in its name, as an expectorant and diaphoretic for chest complaints, bronchitis and pleurisy, used to promote sweating during feverish illness and help clear phlegm.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried root simmered in water as a traditional expectorant and diaphoretic remedy; given its cardiac glycoside content, only under qualified practitioner guidance.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root.

dosage.name: All preparations
dosage.text: Because the root contains cardiac glycosides and modern safety/efficacy data are very limited, no general dose is given here; use only under the guidance of a qualified practitioner, and never in pregnancy, breastfeeding, infancy, or with a heart condition or cardiac glycoside medicines. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory for lung inflammation (traditional)
Diaphoretic - promotes sweating to support feverish illness
Traditional expectorant - helps clear phlegm in chest conditions, bronchitis and pleurisy

indications.note: Traditional expectorant - helps clear phlegm in chest conditions, bronchitis and pleurisy
indications.label: Bronchitis

contraindications.note: Contains cardiac glycosides: do NOT use in pregnancy, breastfeeding or infancy, in anyone with a heart condition, or alongside cardiac glycoside medicines (e.g. digoxin). The plant's cardenolide-rich latex is directly toxic - a documented case caused corneal injury through Na/K-ATPase inhibition.
Large doses are emetic and purgative (cause vomiting and diarrhoea). There is very little modern safety or efficacy data, so use only under the guidance of a qualified practitioner.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young shoots (and the root) gathered from the wild; toxic dogbane sends up very similar shoots in the same habitat.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Butterfly weed / pleurisy root (Asclepias tuberosa) is a milkweed whose young shoots and root have been used. Its main dangerous look-alike is dogbane (Apocynum cannabinum): at the shoot stage the two are easily confused and grow in similar places. Dogbane is a high-severity poison - its milky sap contains cardiac glycosides, all parts are toxic to humans, and poisoning can cause vomiting, weakness and cardiac arrest, while the sap blisters skin. Because the young shoots look alike, the bitterness test below matters.
distinguishing_features: Taste/bitterness (decisive): dogbane is horribly, intensely bitter - an immediate warning to spit it out; edible milkweed shoots are not. Never swallow a bitter 'milkweed' shoot.
Growth habit: dogbane shoots are thinner and firmer and branch early; milkweeds do not branch at the shoot stage. An early-branching, thin, firm shoot is likely dogbane.
General: both milkweeds and dogbane have milky sap, so sap alone does not distinguish them - rely on bitterness and branching, and confirm the species before eating.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Taste a tiny piece and check the branching (then spit it out). An intensely bitter shoot, or one that is thin, firm and branches early, is dogbane - toxic, do not eat. Milky sap alone does not prove it is safe; if in any doubt, discard it.

constituents.name: Astragalus polysaccharides (APS)
constituents.note: Principal immunomodulatory constituents.

constituents.name: Cycloartane triterpenoid saponins (astragaloside IV)
constituents.note: Major bioactive saponin with cardio-, neuro-, hepato- and renoprotective effects.

constituents.name: Isoflavonoids (calycosin, formononetin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoid constituents of the root.

botanical_description.text: Erect perennial herb with a long, fibrous, pale yellow taproot. The leaves are pinnately compound with numerous small, oval leaflets, giving a soft, ferny appearance. Small, pale yellow, pea-like flowers are borne in loose axillary clusters (racemes), followed by membranous, inflated seed pods that give the species its name.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnately compound with numerous small, oval leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale yellow, pea-like flowers in loose axillary racemes
botanical_description.root: Long, fibrous, pale yellow taproot (the medicinal part, huang qi)
botanical_description.fruit: Membranous, inflated seed pod

habitat.text: Native to the grasslands, sunny slopes and forest margins of northern and north-eastern China, Mongolia and parts of Korea; widely cultivated as a medicinal crop across East Asia.

harvesting.text: The root is dug from plants at least four years old, typically in spring or autumn, then cleaned and dried, often sliced into the characteristic pale, fibrous 'coin' slices used in traditional decoctions.
harvesting.season: Spring or autumn, from four-year-old (or older) plants

traditional_uses.text: Astragalus root (huang qi) is one of the most widely used tonic herbs in traditional Chinese medicine, classified as a 'Qi tonic' for fatigue, low vitality and reduced resistance to colds and infections, and used to support immune, cardiovascular and kidney health.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried, sliced root simmered in water, often alongside other tonic herbs, as the classic traditional Chinese medicine preparation.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Concentrated extract standardised to polysaccharide or astragaloside IV content, taken as capsules or tablets.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root.

dosage.name: Decoction
dosage.text: Traditional Chinese medicine references suggest around 9-30 g of dried root per day, decocted. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical studies commonly use extracts providing the equivalent of several grams of dried root daily, in divided doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Adaptogenic tonic for fatigue and low vitality (traditional 'Qi' tonic)
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Immune support / immunomodulation (activates macrophages, NK cells, T and B lymphocytes)
Renoprotective (kidney support) - as an adjunct in chronic kidney disease, reduced proteinuria and raised haemoglobin and serum albumin across trials (Cochrane review; study quality low)

indications.note: Supports resistance to colds and respiratory infection (immune)
indications.label: Cold & flu

indications.note: Adaptogenic tonic for fatigue and low vitality (traditional 'Qi' tonic)
indications.label: Fatigue / low energy

indications.note: Immune support / immunomodulation (activates macrophages, NK cells, T and B lymphocytes); Supports resistance to colds and respiratory infection (immune)
indications.label: Immune support

indications.note: Supports resistance to colds and respiratory infection (immune)
indications.label: Infection (general)

indications.note: Renoprotective (kidney support) - as an adjunct in chronic kidney disease, reduced proteinuria and raised haemoglobin and serum albumin across trials (Cochrane review; study quality low)
indications.label: Kidney support

contraindications.note: Astragalus stimulates immune activity; on theoretical grounds it should be used cautiously in autoimmune disease and with immunosuppressant medicines (for example after organ transplant) - discuss with a clinician.
Generally well tolerated; high-quality long-term human safety and toxicity data remain limited.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding has not been established; avoid medicinal doses.

botanical_description.text: Low, creeping, succulent perennial herb that roots at the nodes as it spreads across wet ground. The leaves are small, fleshy, spatula-shaped and arranged oppositely along the trailing stems. Small, pale lavender to white, four- to five-petalled flowers are borne singly in the leaf axils.
botanical_description.leaves: Small, fleshy, spatula-shaped, opposite
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale lavender to white, four- to five-petalled, borne singly in leaf axils
botanical_description.stem: Trailing, succulent, rooting at the nodes
botanical_description.root: Fine roots at each node along the creeping stem
botanical_description.fruit: Small ovoid capsule

habitat.text: Grows in wet, marshy ground, muddy shorelines, rice paddies and shallow water throughout tropical and subtropical regions of Asia, and also found in parts of Africa, Australia and the Americas.

harvesting.text: The whole plant (leaf and trailing stem) is harvested during active growth, typically cut a few centimetres above the mud or water line, and dried or used fresh.
harvesting.season: During active growth (warm season)

traditional_uses.text: Bacopa (brahmi) is a classic Ayurvedic 'medhya rasayana' (brain-nourishing) herb, traditionally used to support memory, learning and mental clarity, and as a calming nervine for anxiety and disturbed sleep. This traditional cognitive reputation is now supported by randomised controlled trials of standardised extracts.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Extract standardised to bacoside content, taken as capsules or tablets; the form used in most clinical trials for cognitive support.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Fresh or dried leaf/whole plant infused in hot water as a traditional nervine tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for cognitive support commonly use extracts providing around 300 mg dried extract daily, taken for at least several weeks, since benefits accrue gradually. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Triterpenoid saponins (bacosides A and B)
constituents.note: Principal bioactive saponins linked to the plant's neuroprotective and cognition-enhancing effects.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and alkaloids
constituents.note: Additional antioxidant and neuroactive constituents.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated at standard doses. The most commonly reported side effects are gastrointestinal — nausea, stomach cramps, and increased bowel movements — which can be reduced by taking with food. Not recommended during pregnancy. May interact with medications that increase acetylcholine levels (e.g. Alzheimer's medications). Use with caution in thyroid conditions, as bacopa may increase thyroid hormone levels.
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry for Bacopa monnieri (water hyssop/brahmi) in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Bacopa (brahmi) is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Elegant deciduous tree with a slender trunk, distinctive smooth white bark that peels in papery horizontal strips and becomes dark and fissured near the base with age, and characteristically drooping ('pendulous') branchlets. The leaves are small, triangular to diamond-shaped, doubly toothed and long-pointed. Male and female flowers are borne in separate catkins on the same tree in spring.
botanical_description.habit: Slender deciduous tree with drooping branchlets
botanical_description.leaves: Small, triangular to diamond-shaped, doubly toothed, long-pointed
botanical_description.flowers: Separate male and female catkins on the same tree
botanical_description.stem: Slender trunk with smooth white, papery, peeling bark, dark and fissured at the base with age
botanical_description.fruit: Tiny winged seed (samara) shed from the female catkin

habitat.text: A pioneer tree of light, well-drained, often poor or acidic soils, growing in woodland, heathland and waste ground across Europe and much of temperate Asia.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked in spring and early summer while young and tender; bark is collected from felled or fallen wood (living trees should not be stripped of bark, which can kill them); sap is tapped in early spring, before leaf-out, through a small hole bored in the trunk.
harvesting.season: Leaf in spring/early summer; sap in early spring before leaf-out; bark from felled wood

traditional_uses.text: Birch leaf is a classic European 'aquaretic' diuretic used for urinary gravel, kidney stones and as a spring detoxifying tonic, and for rheumatic and joint complaints; the bark and its extracts have a long folk history for skin conditions, while the sap has been drunk fresh as a traditional spring tonic.

preparations.name: Infusion (leaf)
preparations.text: Dried leaf infused in hot water as a traditional diuretic and 'detox' tea, classically taken as a course in spring.

preparations.name: Decoction (bark)
preparations.text: Bark simmered in water or processed into extracts for topical skin use.

preparations.name: Fresh sap
preparations.text: Sap collected fresh in early spring and drunk directly as a traditional tonic, or fermented into birch wine/vinegar.

dosage.name: Leaf infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-3 g of dried leaf per cup, up to three times daily, with adequate fluid intake. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, myricetin, hyperoside)
constituents.note: Principal diuretic and antioxidant constituents of the leaf.

constituents.name: Triterpenes (betulin, betulinic acid)
constituents.note: Characteristic bark triterpenes, betulin giving birch bark its white colour; studied for anticancer and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Tannins and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Contribute to the astringent and antioxidant properties of the bark and leaf.

contraindications.note: Generally considered safe when used appropriately. Side effects may include diarrhea, nausea, and allergic reactions (itching, rash, stuffy nose). Not recommended for individuals with edema caused by heart or kidney dysfunction. Ensure adequate fluid intake when using as a diuretic. Frequency of side effects is unknown.

Duke (2002) provides clinical evidence (score 2) for birch leaf's diuretic activity, as well as for its use in urinary gravel, kidney stones, and rheumatic conditions — consistent with Commission E (KOM) and German Phytotherapy (PIP) approvals. It acts as an aquaretic, increasing urine volume without electrolyte loss. Antimelanomic activity has been demonstrated in experimental studies. The plant has a good safety profile and is classified as non-toxic at usual therapeutic doses (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Tannins (15-36% of the rhizome: oligomeric proanthocyanidins, gallotannins, catechins)
constituents.note: Very high tannin content is the basis of the strong astringency.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids and flavonoids
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant and antibacterial activity.

constituents.name: Starch and oxalic acid
constituents.note: Other rhizome constituents.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb rising from a thick, twisted, S- or snake-shaped rhizome (giving the names 'bistort' and 'snakeroot'), blackish outside and pink inside. The basal leaves are broadly oval to lance-shaped on long stalks; stem leaves are smaller and stalkless. A single dense, cylindrical spike of small, pale pink flowers is borne atop each unbranched flowering stem.
botanical_description.habit: Erect perennial herb from a twisted rhizome
botanical_description.leaves: Basal leaves broadly oval to lance-shaped, long-stalked; stem leaves smaller, stalkless
botanical_description.flowers: Dense, cylindrical spike of small pale pink flowers atop an unbranched stem
botanical_description.stem: Erect, unbranched, one flower spike per stem
botanical_description.root: Thick, twisted, S-shaped (snake-like) rhizome, blackish outside, pink inside
botanical_description.fruit: Small, glossy, three-angled achene

habitat.text: Grows in damp meadows, pastures, streamsides and mountain grassland on moist, often calcareous soils; native to Europe and temperate Asia, favouring cooler upland regions.

harvesting.text: The rhizome is dug in autumn or early spring, when tannin content is highest, cleaned and dried; aerial parts are gathered in summer while flowering.
harvesting.season: Rhizome in autumn or early spring; aerial parts in summer flowering

traditional_uses.text: Bistort rhizome, one of the most tannin-rich temperate herbs, has a long European folk history as a powerful astringent for mild diarrhoea, as a gargle for sore throat and mouth inflammation, and topically as a vulnerary and styptic for minor wounds and bleeding; young leaves have also traditionally been eaten as a spring pot-herb ('Easter-ledge pudding').

preparations.name: Decoction (rhizome)
preparations.text: Dried rhizome simmered in water as a strongly astringent traditional remedy for diarrhoea, or used as a gargle/mouthwash for sore throat.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried rhizome.

preparations.name: Poultice/wash
preparations.text: Crushed rhizome or aerial parts applied externally, or used as a wash, for minor wounds and bleeding.

dosage.name: Decoction/gargle
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried rhizome per cup, up to three times daily, or used as a gargle. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat)
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
Antibacterial (topical) - GC-MS-characterised extracts showed antibacterial activity
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
Vulnerary and styptic for minor wounds (topical)
Vulnerary and styptic for minor wounds (topical)

indications.note: Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat)
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat)
indications.label: Sore throat

indications.note: Vulnerary and styptic for minor wounds (topical)
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Very tannin-rich: high or prolonged intake may cause stomach upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.
Traditional use only; diarrhoea persisting beyond a few days, or mouth/throat inflammation that does not settle, needs medical assessment.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established; avoid medicinal doses.

botanical_description.text: Robust, bristly annual herb with hollow, branching stems covered in coarse, stiff hairs. The leaves are large, oval and wrinkled, also coarsely hairy, and smell of cucumber when crushed. The flowers are strikingly bright blue, star-shaped with five pointed petals and a prominent black central cone of anthers, borne in nodding clusters.
botanical_description.leaves: Large, oval, wrinkled, coarsely hairy, smelling of cucumber when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Bright blue, star-shaped, five-pointed, with a prominent black central cone, in nodding clusters
botanical_description.stem: Hollow, branching, covered in coarse stiff hairs
botanical_description.fruit: Small, dark, oily nutlet (seed)

habitat.text: Grows readily on disturbed, nutrient-rich ground, gardens, waste places and field margins; native to the Mediterranean region and widely naturalised and cultivated across Europe and elsewhere as a culinary and oil-seed crop.

harvesting.text: Leaves and flowers are picked fresh through the growing season, at their best just as the flowers open; the seed is collected in late summer once the seed heads have dried, pressed for its gamma-linolenic-acid-rich oil.
harvesting.season: Leaf and flower through summer; seed in late summer

traditional_uses.text: Borage has a long folk reputation, reflected in the old saying 'borage for courage', as a mood-lifting, cooling herb for feverish colds and respiratory complaints, and as a digestive and anti-inflammatory remedy; the fresh leaves and flowers have also been used culinarily. Modern use is centred on the seed oil, rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), for inflammatory and skin conditions.

preparations.name: Seed oil
preparations.text: Cold-pressed seed oil, standardised for GLA content, taken as capsules; the preferred modern medicinal form, since the whole herb carries pyrrolizidine-alkaloid caution.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf/flower (culinary)
preparations.text: Fresh leaves and flowers used in salads and as a garnish, continuing the traditional food use, and only in the short term given PA content.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried leaf infused as a traditional cooling tea for fevers, though use should be short-term and PA-aware.

dosage.name: Seed oil (standardised)
dosage.text: Products are commonly standardised to around 20-24% GLA, with typical intakes of one to a few 300 mg softgels daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Whole-herb preparations
dosage.text: Because of pyrrolizidine alkaloid content, whole-herb (leaf/flower) preparations should only be used short-term and from PA-controlled sources; avoid in pregnancy, breastfeeding and liver disease. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) seed oil
constituents.note: Borage seed oil has one of the highest known GLA contents of any plant oil (typically 20-26%), the basis of its anti-inflammatory reputation.

constituents.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids
constituents.note: Unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids present in the whole herb are hepatotoxic and genotoxic on cumulative exposure, limiting internal whole-herb use; the refined seed oil is largely free of them.

constituents.name: Mucilage and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute to the plant's traditional demulcent and astringent properties.

contraindications.note: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs): Many Boraginaceae can produce PAs; unsaturated PAs are hepatotoxic and genotoxic (risk increases with cumulative exposure). Borage as food/tea: PA presence in borage consumed as herb/tea has been specifically studied; EU has set PA maximum levels for certain foods including borage (context for why sourcing/limits matter). Pregnancy/breastfeeding: avoid internal use of borage herb products due to PA-related concerns and risk uncertainty. Liver disease / long-term use: avoid (PA risk + cumulative exposure logic). Seed oil vs herb: refined/quality-controlled seed oil is generally the preferred form when borage is used medicinally, because the main target compound is GLA; however, product quality and contamination control still matter. Drug interactions (caution): GLA oils have been discussed with anticoagulants (bleeding risk caution is better established for evening primrose oil; borage oil is often grouped in the same “GLA oil” category). Use caution if on anticoagulants/antiplatelets.

Duke (2002) rates borage as a single-plus herb (+) with predominantly folklore-level evidence. Borage seed oil is rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) and has been used for inflammatory conditions such as arthritis and PMS, and for cardiovascular support; typical dose is one 300 mg softgel containing 24% GLA or 2-4 ml liquid leaf extract. Commission E does not approve borage for any indication, and the herb contains hepatotoxic and carcinogenic pyrrolizidine alkaloids, making long-term use inadvisable (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Pre-flowering rosette leaves gathered for cooking (salads, savoury pies, pasta fillings).
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Foxglove's soft, wrinkled first-year leaves closely resemble borage leaves gathered for food, and the mix-up is repeatedly documented — including people who developed high digitoxin levels and heart block after eating a few 'borage' leaves, one batch bought from a nursery mislabelled as borage. Foxglove leaves contain cardiac glycosides and can be fatal.
distinguishing_features: Borage leaves bear coarse, stiff, bristly hairs that feel rough or prickly; foxglove leaves are softly downy and velvety.
Crushed borage foliage smells clearly of cucumber; foxglove foliage has no cucumber smell.

In flower they are unmistakable: borage has bright blue five-pointed star flowers with a black central cone; foxglove has tall one-sided spikes of pendulous tubular thimble flowers. Do not identify from leaves alone if you can wait for flowers.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Rub and crush a leaf: borage feels coarse and bristly and smells of cucumber; a soft, velvety leaf with no cucumber smell should be treated as foxglove — do not eat it.

constituents.name: Boswellic acids (pentacyclic triterpenes, especially KBA and AKBA)
constituents.note: The principal anti-inflammatory compounds; AKBA inhibits 5-lipoxygenase. Absorption improves when taken with a high-fat meal.

constituents.name: Other triterpenes and essential oil of the resin
constituents.note: Additional constituents of the oleo-gum-resin.

botanical_description.text: Small to medium deciduous tree with thin, papery, peeling bark. The leaves are pinnately compound with numerous small, toothed leaflets, clustered toward the branch tips. Small, pale yellow to white flowers are borne in axillary racemes. When the bark is cut, it exudes a fragrant, resinous gum (oleo-gum-resin) that hardens into pale, waxy tears - the medicinal part.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnately compound with numerous small, toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale yellow to white flowers in axillary racemes
botanical_description.stem: Thin, papery, peeling bark, exuding resin when cut
botanical_description.fruit: Small, three-valved capsule
botanical_description.flowering_period: Varies with climate; typically before or with new leaves

habitat.text: Native to dry, rocky, hilly terrain of India and parts of the Middle East and North Africa, where it is tapped for its resin much like frankincense (Boswellia sacra).

harvesting.text: The bark is deliberately incised and the exuded oleo-gum-resin is allowed to harden and is collected over several days to weeks, then cleaned and graded before use or extraction.
harvesting.season: Tapped through the dry season

traditional_uses.text: Boswellia resin (salai guggul) is a mainstay of Ayurvedic medicine for inflammatory joint and respiratory conditions, and has been burned as incense across many cultures. Modern standardised extracts, rich in boswellic acids, are used for osteoarthritis, inflammatory bowel disease and other inflammatory conditions, directly building on this traditional anti-inflammatory reputation.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Resin extract standardised to boswellic acid (often AKBA) content, taken as capsules or tablets, ideally with a fat-containing meal to aid absorption.

preparations.name: Resin/incense
preparations.text: Raw resin tears burned as incense or chewed traditionally; not a standardised medicinal dose.

preparations.name: Topical cream
preparations.text: Resin extract formulated into topical creams for joint and skin application.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials in osteoarthritis commonly use extracts providing around 100-250 mg of boswellic acids (or several hundred mg of standardised extract) daily, in divided doses, taken with food. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Analgesic (pain relief)

Anti-inflammatory (inhibits 5-lipoxygenase / leukotriene synthesis and NF-kB signalling); Reduces inflammatory markers (e.g. hs-CRP) and, in one RCT, improved knee joint-space and reduced osteophytes on radiograph

indications.note: Osteoarthritis: reduces joint pain and stiffness and improves function (over at least 4 weeks); Supports inflammatory arthritis including rheumatoid arthritis
indications.label: Arthritis / joint pain

indications.note: Supportive in inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis, Crohn's disease)
indications.label: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)

indications.note: Analgesic (pain relief); Osteoarthritis: reduces joint pain and stiffness and improves function (over at least 4 weeks)
indications.label: Pain (general)

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; the most commonly reported side effects are mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and allergic reactions are rare.
Boswellic acids can affect drug-metabolising enzymes, so caution is advised when taking other medicines; safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic annual or short-lived perennial herb with soft, hairy, oblong leaves and branching, slightly sticky stems. The flower heads are bright orange to yellow, daisy-like, with numerous narrow ray florets surrounding a darker central disc, opening in sunlight and closing at dusk.
botanical_description.habit: Erect, branching annual or short-lived perennial herb
botanical_description.leaves: Soft, hairy, oblong to lance-shaped
botanical_description.flowers: Bright orange to yellow, daisy-like flower heads with numerous narrow ray florets
botanical_description.fruit: Curved, ridged achene, without pappus

habitat.text: Widely cultivated as a garden and medicinal plant; believed native to southern Europe and the Mediterranean, now naturalised in gardens and waste ground worldwide.

harvesting.text: The flower heads (or just the ray-floret petals) are picked by hand as they open, through the flowering season, and dried quickly in a warm, shaded, airy place to preserve colour and resin content.
harvesting.season: Through the flowering season (summer to autumn), as flowers open

traditional_uses.text: Calendula flower is one of the most widely used topical wound-healing and skin-soothing herbs in Western herbal medicine, applied to cuts, grazes, minor burns and skin inflammation, and used internally as a gentle anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial remedy and as a gargle for mouth and throat irritation.

preparations.name: Infused oil
preparations.text: Dried petals infused in a carrier oil, used as a soothing base for topical creams and balms for skin healing.

preparations.name: Cream/ointment
preparations.text: Standardised flower extract formulated into a cream or ointment for topical wound and skin care; the best-studied modern form.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flower infused in hot water, taken internally or used as a gargle/mouthwash.

dosage.name: Topical cream/ointment
dosage.text: Applied to the affected skin area 2-3 times daily, per product labelling. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Infusion/gargle
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried flower per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Triterpene saponins (oleanolic acid glycosides, calendulosides)
constituents.note: Principal wound-healing and anti-inflammatory constituents of the flower.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and carotenoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoids and carotenoids give the flower its orange-yellow colour and contribute to its antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Essential oil and resin
constituents.note: Minor aromatic and resinous constituents of the flower head.

contraindications.note: Generally very safe for topical use. May cause contact dermatitis in rare cases (Asteraceae family allergy). Internal use is safe in normal doses. Avoid during pregnancy at therapeutic doses (uterine stimulant potential). Generally safe during breastfeeding for topical use.

Duke (2002) rates calendula (Calendula officinalis) and notes anti-inflammatory, wound-healing (vulnerary), antifungal, and antispasmodic activities at experimental levels. Commission E (KOM) approves calendula flower for wound healing and anti-inflammatory uses, both topically and as a gargle. The plant contains flavonoids, triterpene saponins (oleanolic acid glycosides), and essential oils. Dose: 1–2 g dried flower as tea (for mouth rinse/gargle); or as cream/ointment containing 2–5% calendula extract for topical use. Duke notes that calendula is generally very well-tolerated, though Asteraceae sensitivity can occasionally cause allergic reactions (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Evergreen shrub or small tree with glossy, dark green, leathery, finely toothed leaves. Small, fragrant, white flowers with a prominent central boss of yellow stamens are borne singly or in small clusters in the leaf axils. Commercial tea is made from the young leaves and unopened leaf buds ('sprouts'), processed differently to yield green, black, white or oolong tea from the same species.
botanical_description.height: 1-9 m (kept pruned to 1-1.5 m under cultivation)
botanical_description.habit: Evergreen shrub or small tree, usually kept pruned as a shrub
botanical_description.leaves: Glossy, dark green, leathery, finely toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, fragrant, white, with a prominent boss of yellow stamens
botanical_description.fruit: Woody capsule containing a few large seeds

habitat.text: Native to the subtropical and tropical forests and hillsides of East and South Asia (China, India, Myanmar); cultivated extensively across Asia, Africa and elsewhere on well-drained, acidic upland soils.

harvesting.text: The youngest leaves and unopened leaf buds ('flush') are hand- or machine-picked repeatedly through the growing season; how the fresh leaf is subsequently processed (withered, rolled, oxidised, fixed and dried) determines whether it becomes green, black, white or oolong tea.
