indications.note: Anxiolytic for anxiety and nervous tension - also supported by a double-blind RCT of a fixed California poppy + hawthorn (Crataegus) + magnesium combination in mild-to-moderate anxiety
indications.label: Nervous tension

contraindications.note: Has sedative activity: it can add to the effects of alcohol, sedatives and sleep medicines, so avoid combining and do not use before driving.

Alcoholic (ethanol) extracts inhibit or induce several cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 2D6) and activate the pregnane X receptor, so there is a risk of herb-drug interactions; the water-based tea appears safer in this respect. Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: California poppy has calming, sedative effects; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Large, fast-growing evergreen tree with distinctive two-stage leaves: rounded, silvery-blue juvenile leaves borne in opposite pairs, giving way to narrow, sickle-shaped, aromatic, dark green mature leaves as the tree ages. Small, cream-white, fluffy flowers with numerous stamens are borne singly or in small clusters, followed by a woody, cup-shaped seed capsule ('gum nut').
botanical_description.leaves: Juvenile leaves rounded, silvery-blue, opposite; mature leaves narrow, sickle-shaped, aromatic, dark green
botanical_description.flowers: Small, cream-white, fluffy, with numerous stamens
botanical_description.stem: Tall trunk with smooth, peeling, often bluish-grey bark
botanical_description.fruit: Woody, cup-shaped seed capsule ('gum nut')

habitat.text: Native to south-eastern Australia and Tasmania; widely planted worldwide in warm-temperate and Mediterranean climates for timber, essential oil production and as an ornamental/windbreak tree.

harvesting.text: Mature leaves are picked or pruned year-round and used fresh, dried, or steam-distilled for essential oil, which is the main commercial and medicinal form.

traditional_uses.text: Eucalyptus leaf has a long Australian Aboriginal and, after global spread, worldwide tradition as a respiratory remedy - inhaled as steam or applied as chest rubs for colds, coughs, bronchitis and catarrh - and as a topical antiseptic and rubefacient for minor wounds and muscular aches.

preparations.name: Steam inhalation
preparations.text: A few drops of essential oil, or fresh/dried leaf, added to hot water and the vapour inhaled for respiratory congestion; the classic and best-established use.

preparations.name: Topical chest rub/ointment
preparations.text: Diluted essential oil in a carrier oil or standardised ointment applied to the chest and back for coughs and colds.

preparations.name: Infusion (leaf)
preparations.text: Dried leaf infused in hot water as a milder traditional respiratory tea.

dosage.name: Steam inhalation
dosage.text: A few drops of essential oil (or a handful of leaf) added to a bowl of hot water, inhaled for several minutes as needed; the oil is never taken internally or applied undiluted to skin, and is avoided in infants and young children. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (1,8-cineole/eucalyptol)
constituents.note: Leaf essential oil dominated by 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol, typically 70%+), the principal compound responsible for the plant's antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and expectorant/decongestant activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Antioxidant and astringent constituents of the leaf.

contraindications.note: Eucalyptus globulus essential oil is highly concentrated and rich in the monoterpene 1,8-cineole; it should never be applied undiluted to the skin or ingested outside of standardized pharmaceutical preparations. Improper use can cause irritation, nausea, dizziness, or neurological symptoms, and inhalation at high concentrations may provoke respiratory distress, particularly in infants, young children, or individuals with asthma or other airway sensitivities. For this reason, essential oil use is generally discouraged in children and during pregnancy unless under professional guidance. Preparations such as leaf-infused oils or external applications using whole plant material are considerably milder, but they should still be used with care and an understanding that different preparation methods carry different levels of risk.

Duke (2002) provides clinical evidence (score 2) for eucalyptus as an expectorant and topical hyperemic (rubefacient), consistent with Commission E approval. Clinical evidence also supports its use in asthma, bronchitis, and catarrh. The essential oil (primarily 1,8-cineole) is the active constituent. Therapeutic use as steam inhalation is well-established. Internal use of the essential oil at high doses is contraindicated due to toxicity; even small amounts (3.5 ml) can be fatal in children. Contraindicated for inflammatory diseases of the gastrointestinal tract or bile ducts (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (notably aucubin)
constituents.note: Give the plant its anti-inflammatory action on irritated tissue.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Astringent constituents useful in watery, irritated eyes.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin) and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Small, semi-parasitic annual herb (drawing some nutrients from the roots of neighbouring grasses) with tiny, opposite, toothed leaves and delicate, two-lipped white to pale lilac flowers marked with purple veins and a yellow throat spot, giving a bright, eye-like appearance that inspired both its common and Latin names.
botanical_description.leaves: Tiny, opposite, toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, two-lipped, white to pale lilac, purple-veined with a yellow throat spot
botanical_description.root: Slender root system that taps into neighbouring grass roots (hemiparasitic)
botanical_description.fruit: Small, flattened, hairy capsule

habitat.text: Grows in short, unimproved grassland, pastures, meadows and dunes on well-drained soils, always in association with grasses on which it partially parasitises; native to Europe.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts are cut in summer while in flower and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Summer, at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Eyebright has a centuries-old European folk reputation, reflected in its common name, as a soothing remedy for irritated, red or watery eyes and conjunctivitis, traditionally applied as a cooled compress or eyewash, and taken internally as an astringent for catarrh and mild digestive upset.

preparations.name: Cooled compress/eyewash
preparations.text: Strained, cooled infusion applied as a compress over closed eyelids, or used (only when sterile, commercially prepared) as an eyewash for irritated or red eyes.

preparations.name: Sterile eye drops
preparations.text: Commercially prepared, sterile Euphrasia eye drops used for conjunctivitis; a prospective clinical cohort found improvement in most users.

preparations.name: Infusion (internal)
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water, taken internally as an astringent tea for catarrh.

dosage.name: Compress/eyewash
dosage.text: A strained, cooled infusion applied as a compress several times daily; only sterile, commercially prepared products should ever be put directly into the eye. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Astringent and anti-inflammatory for eye irritation, redness and conjunctivitis (traditional, as an eye compress or bath) - an open prospective cohort of Euphrasia single-dose eye drops reported recovery or clear improvement in most patients with conjunctivitis

Astringent and anti-inflammatory for eye irritation, redness and conjunctivitis (traditional, as an eye compress or bath) - an open prospective cohort of Euphrasia single-dose eye drops reported recovery or clear improvement in most patients with conjunctivitis

indications.note: Eases eye strain and watery, itchy eyes from allergy (hay fever / allergic conjunctivitis)
indications.label: Allergy / hay fever

indications.note: Soothes upper-respiratory catarrh and mild infection (traditional)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Astringent and anti-inflammatory for eye irritation, redness and conjunctivitis (traditional, as an eye compress or bath) - an open prospective cohort of Euphrasia single-dose eye drops reported recovery or clear improvement in most patients with conjunctivitis; Eases eye strain and watery, itchy eyes from allergy (hay fever / allergic conjunctivitis)
indications.label: Eye strain / eye health

indications.note: Eases eye strain and watery, itchy eyes from allergy (hay fever / allergic conjunctivitis)
indications.label: Fever

indications.note: Soothes upper-respiratory catarrh and mild infection (traditional)
indications.label: Infection (general)

indications.note: Soothes upper-respiratory catarrh and mild infection (traditional)
indications.label: Respiratory support

contraindications.note: CRITICAL: do not put non-sterile, homemade eyebright preparations directly into the eye - this risks serious eye infection. Use only commercially prepared sterile products in the eye, or apply a strained, cooled infusion as a compress over closed eyelids.
Traditional use only; persistent eye symptoms, pain, discharge or vision change need assessment by an eye-care professional. Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established.

botanical_description.text: Tall, moisture-loving perennial herb with pinnately compound leaves, dark green above and whitish-downy beneath, the leaflets sharply toothed. Dense, fluffy, creamy-white flower clusters with a strong, sweet, almond-like fragrance are borne atop reddish, grooved stems.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnately compound, dark green above, whitish-downy beneath, sharply toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Dense, fluffy, creamy-white clusters with a strong sweet almond-like fragrance
botanical_description.root: Fibrous roots from a short rhizome
botanical_description.fruit: Small, spirally twisted achenes

habitat.text: Grows in damp meadows, marshes, riverbanks, ditches and wet woodland margins; native to Europe and temperate Asia, favouring moist, nutrient-rich ground.

harvesting.text: The flowering tops, leaf and stem are cut in summer as the flowers open, when the characteristic salicylate content is highest, and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Summer, at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Meadowsweet is historically significant as the plant from which salicylic acid derivatives (the chemical basis of aspirin) were first isolated, and has long been used in European folk medicine as an anti-inflammatory, analgesic and gastroprotective remedy for feverish colds, rheumatic and joint pain, and digestive complaints such as acid reflux and indigestion - notably, its natural mucilage is thought to buffer the gastric irritation associated with isolated salicylates.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flower, leaf and stem infused in hot water as a traditional anti-inflammatory and digestive tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Concentrated extract studied in vitro and in vivo for anti-inflammatory and gastroprotective activity.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-4 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Salicylates (methyl salicylate, salicylic acid derivatives)
constituents.note: Historically the source of aspirin's chemical inspiration; provide the plant's analgesic and anti-inflammatory activity, buffered by co-occurring mucilage and tannins.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, rutin, hyperoside)
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents contributing to the plant's anti-inflammatory and gastroprotective effects.

constituents.name: Tannins and essential oil
constituents.note: Contribute to astringency and the characteristic sweet almond-like fragrance.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Generally safe in normal culinary and medicinal doses. Avoid in salicylate sensitivity or aspirin allergy. Not recommended for children with viral illnesses (Reye's syndrome risk, as with other salicylate-containing plants). Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

Duke (2002) rates meadowsweet as ++ and notes analgesic, antiulcer, antirheumatic, astringent, and anti-aggregant activities at the experimental level (score 1). The plant is historically significant as a precursor to aspirin — salicylate compounds in meadowsweet were originally used to derive acetylsalicylic acid. Unlike aspirin, meadowsweet's salicylates are combined with protective mucilaginous compounds that may reduce gastric irritation. Duke notes that individuals with aspirin (salicylate) sensitivity should avoid meadowsweet; the plant also has anticoagulant properties that may interact with blood-thinning medications (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Erect perennial herb of the rose family, 20-80 cm tall, growing from a short rootstock whose fibrous roots bear characteristic small tuber-like swellings (the 'drop-wort' beads). The leaves are mostly in a basal rosette and are distinctive: long, fern-like and interruptedly pinnate, with many (up to 20+ pairs) small, deeply toothed leaflets. Small creamy-white flowers, often pink-tinged in bud and 6-petalled, are borne in an irregular terminal cyme. Unlike its sibling meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria) of wet ground, dropwort grows in dry calcareous grassland.
botanical_description.habit: Erect perennial herb from a short rootstock bearing small root tubers
botanical_description.leaves: Mostly a basal rosette; long, fern-like, interruptedly pinnate with many (to 20+ pairs) small, deeply toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Creamy-white, often pink-tinged in bud, 6-petalled, in an irregular terminal cyme; faintly scented
botanical_description.stem: Erect, slender, little-branched and nearly leafless above the basal rosette
botanical_description.root: Fibrous roots bearing characteristic small tuber-like swellings ('dropwort' beads)
botanical_description.fruit: A cluster of small, straight, hairy achenes

habitat.text: Dry, unimproved calcareous (chalk and limestone) grassland, meadows, roadside banks and open scrub. Native across most of Europe, western Asia and North Africa. Unlike meadowsweet (F. ulmaria), which grows in wet meadows and beside water, dropwort is a plant of dry ground.

harvesting.text: The flowering tops and aerial parts are gathered in summer while in flower; the fern-like leaves and the small root tubers are also used. The material is dried for infusions and decoctions.
harvesting.season: Aerial parts and flowers in summer (May-August); roots and tubers in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Dropwort shares much of the European folk-medicine history of its sibling meadowsweet. The salicylate- and tannin-rich herb has been used as an astringent and anti-inflammatory remedy for diarrhoea and other gastrointestinal complaints, for rheumatic and arthritic pain, for kidney and bladder 'gravel' and urinary complaints, and as a wound and mouth wash. In Serbian and Balkan ethnomedicine the flowers and roots are used; Russian traditional medicine uses dropwort extracts as a nootropic and calming remedy for the nervous system.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering tops or leaves infused as an astringent, anti-inflammatory tea for digestive and rheumatic complaints.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: The root and root-tubers decocted; used traditionally for diarrhoea and urinary 'gravel'.

preparations.name: Aromatic distillate / essential oil
preparations.text: The flowers yield an aromatic oil dominated by salicylaldehyde with antimicrobial activity (as in meadowsweet).

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract (about 1:5) of the aerial parts.

dosage.name: Dried herb infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal use: roughly 2-4 g of dried flowering herb per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily. Educational reference only.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture, up to three times daily in adults. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Flavonol glycosides
constituents.note: Flavonol glycosides including spiraeoside (quercetin 4'-O-glucoside), hyperoside, rutin and kaempferol derivatives - the signature Filipendula flavonoids.

constituents.name: Hydrolysable tannins
constituents.note: Ellagitannins and gallotannins (astringent constituents), giving the traditional astringent/antidiarrhoeal action.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids
constituents.note: Gallic, caffeic, chlorogenic and ferulic acids; antioxidant.

constituents.name: Condensed tannins / proanthocyanidins
constituents.note: Catechin-based condensed tannins.

constituents.name: Salicylate essential oil
constituents.note: Flower volatile oil dominated by salicylaldehyde, with methyl salicylate - the salicylate character shared with meadowsweet and willow, underlying the anti-inflammatory/analgesic and antimicrobial effects.

actions.note: Antihyperalgesic in a rat inflammatory-pain model (salicylate-mediated).
Traditional use for rheumatic/arthritic pain, consistent with salicylate content and analgesic/anti-inflammatory activity.
Protective against carbon-tetrachloride liver injury in rats.
Hydrolysable-tannin astringency underlying traditional antidiarrhoeal and wound/gargle use.
Nootropic and brain-bioenergetic effects in animal models (Russian traditional nervous-system use).
Dropwort extract restrained the YAP/TAZ/TEAD oncogenic axis in mesothelioma (preclinical).

indications.note: inferred from astringent (tannin) action and traditional use
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: inferred from astringent/antimicrobial action and traditional wound-wash use
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Dropwort contains salicylate compounds (salicylaldehyde, methyl salicylate), as in meadowsweet and willow. Avoid in salicylate or aspirin sensitivity, and use caution alongside anticoagulant or antiplatelet medicines. As with other salicylate-containing plants, avoid in children with viral illness (theoretical Reye's syndrome risk).
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding has not been established; avoid medicinal doses during pregnancy and lactation.
Generally well tolerated in traditional amounts; genotoxicity testing of dropwort extracts found them non-genotoxic and antigenotoxic (DNA-protective) at the doses studied.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Dropwort contains salicylates (as in meadowsweet and willow); combined with anticoagulant or antiplatelet drugs it may theoretically add to bleeding risk.
The salicylate constituents are NSAID-like; taken with NSAIDs they could add to gastrointestinal and bleeding effects.
Dropwort shows anticancer activity in preclinical models; any interaction with chemotherapy is theoretical and unstudied.

pairings.note: Sibling species: both are salicylate- and tannin-rich Rosaceae with overlapping anti-inflammatory, analgesic, astringent and gastroprotective uses (dropwort of dry grassland, meadowsweet of wet meadows); studied together in the same trials.
Both are salicylate-containing anti-inflammatory and analgesic herbs (the classic plant-aspirin group), traditionally combined for rheumatic pain and fever.
Rosaceae astringents rich in hydrolysable tannins, traditionally used for diarrhoea and as gargles; studied together for antioxidant and genotoxicity profile.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Name confusion: 'dropwort' (this plant) vs 'hemlock water-dropwort' (Oenanthe crocata).
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: The share of the name 'dropwort' is a hazard: hemlock water-dropwort (Oenanthe crocata) is one of the deadliest plants in Europe (oenanthotoxin, violent convulsions). The two plants look nothing alike, but a forager going by common name alone could confuse them. This entry exists to make the difference unmistakable.
distinguishing_features: Family and form: true dropwort (Filipendula vulgaris, Rosaceae) is a slender plant with a basal rosette of fern-like pinnate leaves and creamy 6-petalled flowers in a loose cyme; hemlock water-dropwort (Oenanthe crocata, Apiaceae/carrot family) is a stout hollow-stemmed plant with celery-like leaves and flat white UMBELS.
Habitat: Filipendula vulgaris grows in DRY calcareous grassland; Oenanthe crocata grows in WET ground - ditches, streamsides, marshes.
Root: dropwort has fibrous roots with small bead-like tubers; hemlock water-dropwort has a cluster of large pale fleshy finger-like tubers ('dead man's fingers').
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: If it has flat white umbels, celery-like leaves, a hollow stem and grows in wet ground with fleshy finger-like root tubers, it is hemlock water-dropwort (deadly) - NOT dropwort. True dropwort has fern-like basal leaves, creamy 6-petalled flowers and grows in dry grassland.

constituents.name: Essential oil (mainly trans-anethole, with fenchone and estragole/methyl chavicol)
constituents.note: The aromatic volatile oil of the seed; trans-anethole is the principal component and is associated with the antispasmodic, carminative and estrogen-like activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, rutin and kaempferol glycosides)
constituents.note: Antioxidant polyphenolic constituents.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids (chlorogenic, caffeic and rosmarinic acids)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Fatty acids (chiefly petroselinic acid) and amino acids
constituents.note: Major constituents of the fruit/seed oil.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic, robust perennial (often grown as an annual/biennial) with a smooth, waxy, blue-green, hollow stem and finely divided, thread-fine, feathery leaves. Small yellow flowers are borne in large flat compound umbels in summer, ripening into ridged, strongly aniseed-scented seeds.
botanical_description.habit: Aromatic, robust perennial herb (often grown as annual/biennial)
botanical_description.leaves: Finely divided into thread-fine, hair-like, feathery segments
botanical_description.flowers: Small yellow flowers in large flat compound umbels
botanical_description.root: Thick taproot, swollen and bulb-like in the Florence fennel variety
botanical_description.fruit: Ridged, aromatic, aniseed-scented seed (mericarp)

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean region; naturalised and widely cultivated worldwide in temperate and subtropical climates as a culinary herb, vegetable (Florence fennel) and spice crop.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked fresh through the growing season; the seed is harvested in autumn once the umbels have browned and dried, then threshed; the swollen root/bulb of Florence fennel is harvested when mature, before flowering.
harvesting.season: Leaf through summer; seed in autumn; bulb before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Fennel seed has an ancient Mediterranean and Asian tradition as a carminative digestive remedy for indigestion, bloating and colic (including infant colic, historically), a mild expectorant for respiratory catarrh, and a traditional lactagogue and emmenagogue owing to its mild estrogen-like activity.

preparations.name: Infusion (seed)
preparations.text: Crushed seed infused in hot water as the classic carminative digestive tea.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Seed extract studied in randomised trials for primary dysmenorrhoea (menstrual pain).

preparations.name: Culinary use
preparations.text: Seed, leaf ('fronds') and bulb used extensively as a food spice and vegetable.

dosage.name: Seed infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1.5-2.5 g crushed seed per cup, up to three times daily, for short periods. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

indications.note: Relieves primary dysmenorrhoea (menstrual) pain - meta-analyses of RCTs find fennel reduces pain intensity versus placebo, comparable to mefenamic acid
indications.label: Menstrual cramps

indications.note: Relieves primary dysmenorrhoea (menstrual) pain - meta-analyses of RCTs find fennel reduces pain intensity versus placebo, comparable to mefenamic acid
indications.label: Pain (general)

contraindications.note: When taken by mouth: Fennel is commonly consumed in foods. It is possibly safe when used as medicine at appropriate doses for a short period of time. There isn't enough reliable information to know whether fennel is safe when used long-term. Although rare, side effects might include stomach upset and seizures.

When applied to the skin: Fennel is possibly safe. Fennel can make skin extra sensitive to the sun and make it easier to get a sunburn. Wear sunblock if you are light-skinned. Special Precautions and Warnings When taken by mouth: Fennel is commonly consumed in foods. It is possibly safe when used as medicine at appropriate doses for a short period of time. There isn't enough reliable information to know whether fennel is safe when used long-term. Although rare, side effects might include stomach upset and seizures.
When applied to the skin: Fennel is possibly safe. Fennel can make skin extra sensitive to the sun and make it easier to get a sunburn. Wear sunblock if you are light-skinned.
Pregnancy: Fennel is possibly unsafe to use when pregnant. Regularly using fennel has been linked to preterm birth.

Breast-feeding: Fennel is possibly unsafe. There are some reports of breast-feeding infants with damage to their nervous systems after they were exposed to herbal tea containing fennel through breastmilk.
Children: Fennel is possibly safe when used at appropriate doses for up to one week in young infants with colic.
Allergy to celery, carrot or mugwort: Fennel might cause an allergic reaction in people who are sensitive to these plants.
Bleeding disorders: Fennel might slow blood clotting. Taking fennel might increase the risk of bleeding or bruising in people with bleeding disorders.

Hormone-sensitive condition such as breast cancer, uterine cancer, ovarian cancer, endometriosis, or uterine fibroids: Fennel might act like estrogen. If you have any condition that might be made worse by estrogen, do not use fennel. (WebMD, n.d.b)

Duke (2002) rates fennel as ++ and notes antispasmodic, carminative, and digestive activities at experimental levels (score 1). Its estrogenic activity is well-documented, and it is traditionally used as a lactagogue. Commission E approves fennel for dyspeptic complaints and upper respiratory tract catarrh. Dose: 1.5–7.5 g seed or equivalent daily. Duke cautions that fennel essential oil in large amounts may be neurotoxic (estrole content), and prolonged use of concentrated extracts should be avoided during pregnancy (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Feathery foliage, stems and seeds - the whole above-ground plant. Both are tall Apiaceae with finely divided leaves, white or greenish compound umbels and hollow jointed stems, so a forager reaching for aniseed 'wild fennel' can grasp poison hemlock instead.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) is explicitly named as an edible confused with poison hemlock: King County (Washington) states that poison hemlock can be confused with many edible members of the parsley family including fennel, chervil and anise. Conium maculatum contains the piperidine alkaloid coniine and kills by ascending flaccid paralysis and respiratory failure; ingestion mistaken for edible umbellifers has caused fatal and near-fatal poisonings, and it is the plant analysed as the likely agent in the death of Socrates (Dayan 2024).
distinguishing_features: CRUSH-AND-SMELL (decisive): fennel smells strongly and pleasantly of anise/liquorice. Poison hemlock has a rank, musty, mouse-like smell with no anise note.
Stem markings: fennel has a smooth, waxy, blue-green stem with NO purple. Poison hemlock's smooth hairless hollow stem is conspicuously spotted and streaked with red-purple, especially near the base.
Leaf texture: fennel leaves are thread-fine, almost hair-like filaments. Poison hemlock leaves are broader, flat, fern-like segments with toothed edges.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush a leaf or stem and smell it: sweet anise/liquorice = fennel; rank musty/mousy = poison hemlock. Confirm by checking the lower stem for red-purple blotching (present on hemlock, absent on fennel's clean blue-green waxy stem). If the anise smell is absent OR any purple stem-spotting is present, do not eat it. Very young hemlock may not yet show strong purple, so rely first on the smell.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Finely divided foliage and small white compound umbels. As a slender Apiaceae with dissected leaves and white umbels, fool's parsley falls into the same lacy white-flowered umbellifer pool a fennel forager must rule out.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Aethusa cynapium (fool's parsley, dog poison, lesser hemlock) is a toxic umbellifer historically confused with edible parsley-family plants. It contains toxic alkaloids; documented effects include inflammation of the mouth and throat, abdominal pain, confusion, blurred vision and pupil dilation, and its genuine toxicity is confirmed experimentally (Teuscher et al. 1990). Direct fennel confusion is weaker than for hemlock because fool's-parsley leaves are flat and parsley-like rather than thread-fine, but it shares the fennel umbel gestalt and is dangerous, so it must be ruled out.
distinguishing_features: BRACTEOLE 'beard' (in flower): below each secondary umbel, fool's parsley bears three to four long, narrow, downward-hanging strongly reflexed bracteoles forming a distinctive drooping beard. Fennel has no such conspicuous drooping bracteoles under its umbellets.
Smell when crushed: fennel is sweetly aniseed/liquorice; fool's parsley gives off an acrid, foetid, mouse-like odour.
Leaf form: fennel leaves are thread-fine hair-like filaments; fool's-parsley leaves are flatter, darker green and parsley-like, not thread-fine.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Look under a secondary flower cluster for the three to four long, narrow, downward-drooping bracteoles (the beard) - diagnostic for fool's parsley and absent in fennel. Combine with crush-and-smell (sweet anise = fennel; acrid/foetid = fool's parsley). If a drooping bracteole beard is present or the smell is foetid, do not eat it.

constituents.name: Anthraquinones (including galiprenylin and other prenylated anthraquinones)

botanical_description.text: Sprawling to erect perennial herb with slender, square-sectioned stems and whorls of narrow, lance-shaped leaves typical of the bedstraw genus. Numerous small, four-petalled, white star-shaped flowers are borne in loose, branched clusters, giving a frothy appearance.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, in whorls of 6-8
botanical_description.flowers: Small, four-petalled, white, star-shaped, in loose branched clusters
botanical_description.stem: Slender, square-sectioned, smooth (not bristly like cleavers)
botanical_description.fruit: Small, smooth (non-hooked) twin nutlet, distinguishing it from bristly-fruited cleavers

habitat.text: A common plant of grassland, meadows, hedgerows, roadsides and waste ground on a wide range of soils; native to Europe and temperate Asia.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts are cut in summer while in flower and dried.
harvesting.season: Summer, at flowering

traditional_uses.text: White bedstraw shares the broader bedstraw genus's folk reputation as a mild diuretic and topical remedy for minor skin and wound complaints, though species-specific clinical or traditional-use documentation is sparse; most available research on this species is phytochemical and antioxidant in nature rather than confirming a specific traditional-use record.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water, following the general bedstraw-genus tradition; species-specific use documentation is limited.

preparations.name: Fresh herb (topical)
preparations.text: Fresh crushed herb applied topically for minor skin irritation, per general genus tradition.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: No species-specific dosing data exist; if used, follow conservative genus-typical dosing (around 2-4 g dried herb per cup) with professional guidance. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Aerial-part and flower extracts of Galium album / G. mollugo show free-radical-scavenging and reactive-oxygen-species-reducing activity attributed to their polyphenol, flavonoid and iridoid content.

contraindications.note: White bedstraw (Galium album) is a widespread grassland plant that shares the bedstraw genus's folk-medicinal reputation as a mild diuretic and as a topical remedy for minor skin and wound complaints. Modern evidence is limited to phytochemical and antioxidant studies; no clinical trials exist. It is generally regarded as low in toxicity at culinary or tea amounts, but it has not been formally evaluated for safety in pregnancy, in lactation, or alongside medication, so caution is advised.

botanical_description.text: Scrambling, sprawling annual herb covered in tiny hooked bristles on its square stems, leaves and seed capsules, allowing it to cling to clothing, fur and neighbouring plants as it climbs ('stickyweed'). The leaves are narrow and lance-shaped, borne in whorls of six to eight. Tiny white, four-petalled flowers give way to small, round, bristly-hooked twin seed capsules ('goosegrass burrs').
botanical_description.height: Scrambling stems to 1-2 m, supported by other vegetation
botanical_description.habit: Scrambling, sprawling annual herb, climbing by hooked bristles
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, in whorls of 6-8, with hooked bristles on the margins
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, white, four-petalled
botanical_description.stem: Square-sectioned, weak, covered in tiny hooked bristles
botanical_description.fruit: Small, round, bristly-hooked twin nutlet, clinging to fur and clothing

habitat.text: A common scrambling weed of hedgerows, waste ground, gardens and field margins, climbing over other vegetation for support; native to Europe, Asia and North Africa and widely naturalised elsewhere.

harvesting.text: The whole aerial plant (leaf, stem and seed) is cut in spring to early summer, before the fruit fully hardens, and used fresh (as juice) or dried.
harvesting.season: Spring to early summer

traditional_uses.text: Cleavers has a long European folk history as a lymphatic and diuretic 'spring tonic' herb, traditionally used to support the urinary system and lymphatic drainage, and topically as a soothing, emollient wash or poultice for skin irritation, wounds and minor swellings.

preparations.name: Fresh juice
preparations.text: Fresh aerial parts crushed or juiced, traditionally taken as a spring tonic or applied topically to skin irritation and wounds.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried or fresh herb infused in hot (or cold) water as a traditional diuretic and lymphatic-support tea.

preparations.name: Poultice/wash
preparations.text: Crushed fresh herb or a strained infusion applied topically as a wash or poultice for skin irritation and minor wounds.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-4 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (asperuloside)
constituents.note: Characteristic Rubiaceae iridoids shared with related bedstraws, contributing to the plant's traditional activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents supporting the plant's antimicrobial and wound-healing activity.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Astringent constituents contributing to the traditional topical use for skin and wounds.

contraindications.note: Duke (2002) describes cleavers as a diuretic, lymph tonic, and depurative with experimental support (score 1). The plant has been used in traditional medicine for swollen lymph nodes, urinary complaints, and skin conditions. Diuretic and anti-inflammatory activities are supported experimentally. No Commission E approval. Safety rating is ++ (relatively safe), with no known significant contraindications at usual herbal doses (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Bracket (shelf) fungus (not a true plant) that grows on the trunks and stumps of deciduous trees. It develops a hard, kidney- or fan-shaped cap with a glossy, varnished, red-brown to mahogany crust and concentric growth rings, often on a lateral woody stalk; the pale underside is covered in fine pores that release rusty-brown spores. The mycelium spreads through the wood substrate before fruiting.
botanical_description.leaves: Not applicable (fungus)
botanical_description.flowers: Not applicable (fungus); reproduces by spores
botanical_description.stem: Hard, glossy, varnished kidney- or fan-shaped cap, often on a lateral stalk
botanical_description.root: Mycelium spreading through the wood substrate
botanical_description.fruit: Pale, finely pored underside releasing rusty-brown spores
botanical_description.flowering_period: Fruiting body develops over weeks to months on the host wood

habitat.text: Grows as a wood-decay fungus on the stumps and trunks of deciduous trees (notably maple and other hardwoods) in East Asian forests; also widely cultivated commercially on hardwood logs or sawdust substrate.

harvesting.text: Wild fruiting bodies are collected once mature; cultivated material is harvested from logs or substrate at maturity, then dried and processed into slices, powder or extract.

traditional_uses.text: Reishi/lingzhi, the 'mushroom of immortality', is one of the most revered tonic fungi in traditional Chinese medicine, used for centuries to support vitality, calm the spirit, strengthen immunity and promote longevity; this traditional tonic reputation is now studied for immunomodulatory, anticancer-adjunct and metabolic effects.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried, sliced fruiting body simmered in water for an extended period, the traditional preparation to extract polysaccharides.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Extract standardised to polysaccharide (beta-glucan) or triterpene content, taken as capsules or powder; the form used in most modern studies.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcohol/water dual extraction, used to capture both water-soluble polysaccharides and alcohol-soluble triterpenes.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Traditional and clinical references commonly cite around 1-3 g of dried fruiting body extract daily, or higher doses of raw powder/decoction. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Beta-glucan polysaccharides
constituents.note: Principal immunomodulatory constituents, the main focus of anticancer-adjunct and immune research.

constituents.name: Triterpenes (ganoderic acids)
constituents.note: Bitter triterpenes associated with anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective and adaptogenic activity.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated at standard doses. May cause mild digestive upset, dry mouth, or dizziness in some individuals. May enhance the effects of anticoagulant and antihypertensive medications. Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Extended use beyond 6 months is not well studied in humans.

Duke (2002) rates reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) as + and notes immunostimulant, hepatoprotective, antioxidant, antitumor, and hypoglycemic activities at the experimental level (score 1). It is a key adaptogen in traditional Chinese medicine, valued for its polysaccharide (beta-glucan) content. Duke notes antiviral (score 1) and anti-aggregant activities. No strong clinical trials existed at time of publication, but lentinan and polysaccharide fractions from related species show immunomodulatory potential. Duke suggests caution in bleeding disorders due to anti-aggregant activity (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Reishi is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Wild-collected fruit bodies gathered as reishi/lingzhi for medicinal tea; the immature red, antler-like fruit bodies of poison fire coral can be taken for young reishi.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Reishi (Ganoderma lingzhi / lucidum) is a hard, varnished bracket fungus collected and brewed as a health tonic. Poison fire coral (Podostroma cornu-damae), one of the few deadly-poisonous fungi, is documented to resemble Ganoderma lucidum in its immature stage. A peer-reviewed case report (Ahn et al., 2013, Yonsei Med J) describes two people who collected and boiled wild fungus as tea - one died of pancytopenia and multiple organ failure - and states that in its immature period poison fire coral resembles Ganoderma lucidum, 'well known as a health-food.' Its trichothecene mycotoxins (satratoxins) are frequently fatal. Because reishi is wild-collected for medicinal tea in East Asia, this is a genuinely lethal confusion.
distinguishing_features: Colour and form: poison fire coral is bright blood-red to orange-red and grows as erect, often branched antler- or coral-like clubs rising from the ground or buried wood. True reishi is a flat kidney- or fan-shaped bracket (conk) with a hard, glossy, varnished red-brown to mahogany crust and a pale pored underside, growing on wood.
Growth pattern: reishi forms a shelf/bracket with a distinct cap and often a lateral stalk; poison fire coral forms finger-like or antler-like red spikes with no cap.
Underside: reishi has a white-to-brown pored underside that drops rusty-brown spores; poison fire coral has no pores at all.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Look at the shape. Reishi is a hard, flat, varnished shelf/bracket with a pale pored underside, growing on wood. Any bright red, erect antler- or coral-like club with NO cap and NO pores is NOT reishi - it may be poison fire coral (Podostroma cornu-damae), which can kill. Never brew an unfamiliar red club or coral fungus; if unsure, do not use it and confirm with an expert mycologist.

constituents.name: Secoiridoid bitter glycosides (gentiopicroside, amarogentin, swertiamarin)
constituents.note: Amarogentin is one of the bitterest natural compounds known; these stimulate digestive secretions. Genus-level reviews identify these secoiridoids, together with xanthones and flavonoids, as the main bioactives and report gastrointestinal, hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Xanthones (gentisin) and flavonoids
constituents.note: Pigment and antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Sugars (gentiobiose)
constituents.note: Carbohydrate constituents of the root.

botanical_description.text: Tall, robust perennial herb with a thick, fleshy taproot and large, broadly oval, strongly veined opposite leaves (a distinguishing feature from the deadly Veratrum album, which has alternate leaves). Bright golden-yellow, star-shaped flowers are borne in dense whorls in the leaf axils along the upper stem.
botanical_description.leaves: Large, broadly oval, strongly veined, arranged in opposite pairs
botanical_description.flowers: Bright golden-yellow, star-shaped, in dense axillary whorls
botanical_description.fruit: Capsule with numerous winged seeds

habitat.text: Grows in subalpine and alpine meadows and pastures of central and southern Europe, typically at moderate to high elevations on calcareous soils.

harvesting.text: The root is dug in autumn from plants at least several years old, when bitter secoiridoid content is highest, then cleaned and dried; careful identification against the deadly Veratrum album (white hellebore), which shares similar habitat and, before flowering, a superficially similar rosette, is essential before wild-harvesting.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Gentian root is one of the classic bitter digestive herbs of European tradition, used to stimulate appetite and digestive secretions for temporary loss of appetite and mild dyspeptic complaints, and has long been a base ingredient of bitter tonics, aperitifs and digestifs.

preparations.name: Cold infusion (maceration)
preparations.text: Dried root macerated in cold water for several hours to extract the bitter secoiridoids, a traditional preparation method for bitters.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root, often taken as a few drops before meals.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried root infused briefly in hot water and taken shortly before meals as a bitter digestive tonic.

dosage.name: Infusion/tincture
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 0.5-2 g of dried root per dose, taken shortly before meals, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Aromatic bitter for temporary loss of appetite
Digestive aid for mild dyspeptic complaints - indigestion, bloating and fullness (stimulates gastric acid, enzyme and bile secretion)

indications.note: Aromatic bitter for temporary loss of appetite
indications.label: Loss of appetite

indications.note: Digestive aid for mild dyspeptic complaints - indigestion, bloating and fullness (stimulates gastric acid, enzyme and bile secretion)
indications.label: Bloating

indications.note: Digestive aid for mild dyspeptic complaints - indigestion, bloating and fullness (stimulates gastric acid, enzyme and bile secretion)
indications.label: Indigestion

indications.note: Eases nausea linked to sluggish digestion (traditional)
indications.label: Nausea

contraindications.note: Because bitters increase stomach-acid secretion, gentian is contraindicated in peptic (stomach or duodenal) ulcer disease and hyperacidity.
Traditional use only; appetite loss or dyspepsia that persists needs medical assessment, and use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Non-flowering basal leaf rosette and root — the parts dug to make gentian wine, bitters, schnapps or tea, before either plant flowers.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: One of the best-documented lethal foraging mix-ups in Europe. White hellebore (Veratrum album) and yellow gentian grow side by side in the same subalpine meadows, and before flowering their large ribbed basal leaves and thick roots look almost identical to a non-botanist. Foragers digging gentian for home-made gentian wine, bitters or herbal tea have repeatedly harvested Veratrum by mistake; poisoning begins within 30 minutes to a few hours with vomiting, then a dangerously slow heartbeat and a collapse in blood pressure. Documented in multiple European case reports.
distinguishing_features: Leaf arrangement is decisive and works even before flowering: yellow gentian has OPPOSITE leaves — a matching pair growing directly across from each other at each stem node. Veratrum album has ALTERNATE leaves, attached singly and spiralling up the stem, each clasping/sheathing it.

Yellow gentian leaves are smooth and hairless with a few (about 5–7) bold arching parallel veins. Veratrum album leaves are strongly pleated and corrugated, with many prominent parallel veins and a finely hairy underside.

If in flower only: yellow gentian has bright yellow star-shaped flowers in whorls; Veratrum album has whitish-green flowers in a branched spike. Do NOT rely on flower colour when foraging the non-flowering rosette or root.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Check how the leaves attach to the stem. A matching PAIR directly opposite each other at each node = yellow gentian. Leaves attached one at a time, spiralling and sheathing the stem = Veratrum album — do not harvest. If you cannot confirm opposite leaf pairs, do not forage.

botanical_description.text: A perennial herb of the rose family, 20-60 cm tall, growing from a thick, reddish, aromatic rhizome that smells of cloves when broken (from its eugenol content) - the source of the name 'chocolate root'. The basal leaves are pinnate with a large terminal lobe, forming a rosette. Its distinctive flowers are nodding (drooping) bells with dull purple-pink to orange sepals and cream-to-pink petals, quite unlike the erect open yellow flowers of its relative wood avens (Geum urbanum). The fruit is a rounded head of achenes, each with a hooked, feathery style that clings to fur and clothing.
botanical_description.habit: Erect perennial herb from a thick aromatic rhizome
botanical_description.leaves: Basal rosette of pinnate leaves with a large terminal lobe; stem leaves smaller, trifoliolate
botanical_description.flowers: Nodding, bell-shaped, dull purple-pink to orange sepals with cream-to-pink petals (distinct from the erect yellow flowers of wood avens)
botanical_description.root: Thick reddish rhizome smelling of cloves (eugenol) - the 'chocolate root'
botanical_description.fruit: A rounded head of achenes, each with a hooked, feathery style

habitat.text: Damp ground: wet meadows, marshes, streamsides, ditches, fens and damp woodland across Europe, western Asia and North America. It favours moist, base-rich soils. Unlike wood avens (Geum urbanum), a plant of dry hedgerows and shade, water avens grows in wet, open places.

harvesting.text: The reddish rhizome/root is the main medicinal part, dug in spring or autumn and dried carefully to preserve its clove-scented, eugenol-bearing, tannin-rich qualities; the aerial flowering parts are gathered in summer. The root has historically been used both as an astringent medicine and as a flavouring (a coffee/chocolate substitute and to flavour ale).
harvesting.season: Root/rhizome in spring or autumn; aerial parts in flower (summer)

traditional_uses.text: Water avens shares the astringent, tannin-based traditional medicine of the avens (Geum) genus. The clove-scented root has been used as an astringent for diarrhoea, dysentery and other bowel complaints, as a gargle and mouth wash for sore throat, mouth ulcers and spongy gums, and as a wash for wounds and skin inflammation. It was also valued as an aromatic bitter tonic and, historically, a febrifuge, and the fragrant root was used to flavour ale and as a coffee/chocolate substitute (hence 'chocolate root').

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: The dried root/rhizome simmered in water as an astringent for diarrhoea and as a gargle for sore throat and inflamed gums.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: The aerial parts or root infused as a milder astringent and aromatic tonic tea.

preparations.name: Gargle / mouthwash
preparations.text: A cooled root decoction used as a gargle/mouthwash for sore throat, mouth ulcers and spongy gums.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the root (about 1:5).

dosage.name: Root decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal use: roughly 1-4 g of dried root per cup as a decoction, up to three times daily. Educational reference only.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 1-4 mL of a 1:5 root tincture up to three times daily in adults. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Ellagitannins (pedunculagin, galloyl-bis-HHDP-hexose)
constituents.note: Hydrolysable ellagitannins - the dominant astringent constituents of water avens.

constituents.name: Gallotannins and condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins)
constituents.note: Additional hydrolysable and condensed tannins contributing to the astringent, antidiarrhoeal and elastase-inhibiting activity.

constituents.name: Ellagic acid and derivatives
constituents.note: Ellagic acid and ellagic-acid glycosides (antioxidant; precursors of anti-inflammatory urolithins in the gut).

constituents.name: Gallic acid and derivatives
constituents.note: Gallic acid and galloyl derivatives (antioxidant, antimicrobial).

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin glycosides)
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonol glycosides.

constituents.name: Triterpenoid saponins (niga-ichigoside)
constituents.note: Triterpenoid saponins and aglycones.

constituents.name: Essential oil - eugenol
constituents.note: The root essential oil is dominated by eugenol, giving the clove-like scent (shared with clove) and antiseptic, mild-anaesthetic character.

actions.note: Tannin-rich root, the basis of the traditional antidiarrhoeal and gargle uses.
Astringent hydrolysable tannins; traditional remedy for diarrhoea and dysentery.
Inhibits prostaglandin biosynthesis; ellagitannin-derived urolithins are anti-inflammatory.
Geum (avens) extracts show antineoplastic and skin antineoplastic activity in vitro (preclinical).
Antiviral activity of avens (Geum) extract in vitro (congener).
Neuroprotective activity of water avens polyphenol fractions.
Avens root extract showed antidiabetic (glucose-lowering) activity in vivo (congener G. urbanum).
Astringent/antimicrobial root traditionally applied to wounds and inflamed skin; skin antineoplastic activity shown for avens.

indications.note: Astringent (tannin) antidiarrhoeal; traditional remedy for diarrhoea and dysentery.
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Root decoction used traditionally as a gargle/mouthwash for mouth ulcers and spongy gums; antimicrobial.
indications.label: Oral & throat health

indications.note: inferred from astringent/antimicrobial action and traditional wound-wash use
indications.label: Wounds

indications.note: Avens root showed antidiabetic activity in vivo (congener).
indications.label: Blood sugar / diabetes support

contraindications.note: The root and herb are rich in tannins; large or prolonged doses may cause digestive upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines - take apart from iron supplements and other drugs.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding has not been established; avoid medicinal doses during pregnancy and lactation.
In-vivo toxicity testing of avens (Geum) extract showed a favourable profile at the doses studied, but high-dose or long-term safety is not established.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Avens (Geum) root showed antidiabetic (glucose-lowering) activity; combined with antidiabetic medicines it could theoretically add to blood-sugar-lowering effects.

pairings.note: Classic Rosaceae astringent pairing: both are tannin-rich roots traditionally combined for diarrhoea and as mouth/throat gargles.
Rosaceae astringents rich in hydrolysable tannins, traditionally used together for diarrhoea, sore throat and as gargles.
Both are Rosaceae rich in ellagitannins and phenolics with overlapping antioxidant and anti-inflammatory profiles; water avens is astringent, meadowsweet more salicylate-based.

botanical_description.text: Ancient deciduous tree, a 'living fossil' with no close living relatives, bearing distinctive fan-shaped leaves with parallel forking veins that turn brilliant gold in autumn. The tree is dioecious (separate male and female trees); female trees bear fleshy, foul-smelling seed coats surrounding an edible inner nut (ginkgo nut) once the outer flesh is removed.
botanical_description.leaves: Distinctive fan-shaped, with parallel forking veins, turning gold in autumn
botanical_description.flowers: Dioecious; wind-pollinated, inconspicuous
botanical_description.stem: Tall, sturdy trunk with furrowed grey bark
botanical_description.fruit: Fleshy, foul-smelling seed coat (female trees) surrounding an edible inner nut

habitat.text: Native to China, where a small number of wild populations survive; now cultivated worldwide as a resilient, pollution-tolerant ornamental and medicinal tree.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked in late summer to autumn, when flavonoid and terpenoid content is highest, and processed into standardised extracts; the inner seed is collected in autumn from fallen fruit, with the malodorous outer flesh removed (handled with gloves, as it can irritate skin).
harvesting.season: Leaf in late summer/autumn; seed in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Ginkgo leaf and seed have a long East Asian tradition: the seed used in traditional Chinese medicine (in small, cooked amounts) for respiratory complaints, and the leaf, though a more recent addition to Western herbalism, now one of the most extensively studied botanicals for cognitive and peripheral circulatory support, reflecting decades of standardised-extract clinical research.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Leaf extract standardised to flavone glycosides and terpene lactones (commonly the EGb 761 standard), taken as tablets or capsules; the best-studied clinical form.

preparations.name: Cooked seed (traditional, East Asian)
preparations.text: Small numbers of cooked seeds used traditionally in East Asian cuisine and medicine; raw seeds are toxic (ginkgotoxin) and never eaten raw.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for cognitive and circulatory support commonly use around 120-240 mg of standardised leaf extract daily, in divided doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Flavone glycosides (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin glycosides)
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoids of the leaf, standardised in clinical extracts.

constituents.name: Terpene lactones (ginkgolides, bilobalide)
constituents.note: Unique diterpene and sesquiterpene lactones responsible for platelet-activating-factor antagonism and much of the neuroprotective and circulatory activity; also the basis of the bleeding-risk caution.

constituents.name: Ginkgotoxin (4'-O-methylpyridoxine)
constituents.note: A neurotoxic compound present in the raw seed; the reason only cooked seed in small amounts, or standardised leaf extract, is used medicinally.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated at standard doses but notable interactions exist. Ginkgo has antiplatelet and anticoagulant effects and should not be combined with warfarin, aspirin, or other blood-thinning medications without medical supervision. May lower the seizure threshold — avoid in epilepsy. Possible side effects include headache, dizziness, and GI upset. The raw seeds contain ginkgotoxin and are toxic; only processed leaf extracts are used medicinally.

Duke (2002) rates ginkgo as + (downgraded from higher rating for safety concerns) while noting clinical evidence (score 2) for anti-aggregant (platelet-inhibitory), anti-Alzheimer, and antioxidant activities. Commission E approves ginkgo leaf extract for cognitive deficits, peripheral circulatory disorders, and vertigo. However, Duke emphasizes significant safety concerns: ginkgo substantially increases bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin, NSAIDs) and may cause spontaneous bleeding at high doses alone. Dose: 120–240 mg standardized extract (EGb 761) daily. Contraindicated with anticoagulants without medical supervision (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo has antiplatelet activity (ginkgolide PAF antagonism) and may add to the bleeding risk of anticoagulant or antiplatelet drugs; a systematic review of case reports links ginkgo to spontaneous bleeding (including intracranial bleeds) with elevated bleeding times. Stop before surgery.
Ginkgo's antiplatelet effect may add to the bleeding risk of NSAIDs such as ibuprofen; case reports of bleeding exist.
Ginkgo can contain ginkgotoxin, which may lower the seizure threshold; use caution alongside anti-seizure medication.

Ginkgo has been reported to interact with the blood-pressure medicine nifedipine (a calcium-channel blocker), raising its levels and side effects such as dizziness; combined use with blood-pressure medicines warrants monitoring.

Ginkgo can change how some diabetes medicines are handled by the body (for example altering levels of tolbutamide), which may affect blood-sugar control; monitor blood sugar if combined with antidiabetic medicines.

pairings.note: Ginkgo and garlic both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginkgo and ginger both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginkgo and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginkgo and ginseng both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

botanical_description.text: Herbaceous perennial legume with pinnately compound leaves of small, oval, slightly sticky leaflets. Pale blue-violet to lilac pea-like flowers are borne in loose spikes, followed by small, flattened, oblong pods. The sweet-tasting rhizome and root system, brownish-grey outside and bright yellow inside, is the medicinal part, spreading extensively underground via stolons.
botanical_description.habit: Herbaceous perennial legume, spreading by underground stolons
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnately compound, small oval leaflets, slightly sticky
botanical_description.flowers: Pale blue-violet to lilac, pea-like, in loose spikes
botanical_description.root: Extensive, sweet-tasting rhizome and root system, brownish-grey outside, bright yellow inside
botanical_description.fruit: Small, flattened, oblong pod

habitat.text: Grows in dry, sunny, deep-soiled sites, riverbanks and open scrub; native to the Mediterranean region and Western to Central Asia, and cultivated widely for its root.

harvesting.text: The root and rhizome are dug in autumn from plants at least three to four years old, when glycyrrhizin content is highest, then cleaned and dried; sold whole, cut, or as processed extract.
harvesting.season: Autumn, from mature (3-4+ year) plants

traditional_uses.text: Licorice root is one of the oldest and most widely used herbs in the world, with a documented history across Chinese, Ayurvedic, Greek and European traditional medicine as a soothing demulcent and expectorant for coughs and sore throat, a gastroprotective remedy for stomach discomfort and ulcers, and a harmonising, sweetening addition to herbal formulas.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried root simmered in water as a traditional soothing, expectorant and gastroprotective tea.

preparations.name: Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL)
preparations.text: Extract with most of the glycyrrhizin removed, used for prolonged gastric support without the blood-pressure/potassium risk of whole root.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Root extract standardised to glycyrrhizin content, taken as capsules or lozenges.

dosage.name: Whole root/decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references limit regular whole-root use to under about 100 mg glycyrrhizin (roughly under 10-15 g dried root) daily, and for no more than 4-6 weeks continuously, given the pseudoaldosteronism risk; DGL is preferred for prolonged use. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Avoid prolonged use of whole licorice root in large doses. May cause pseudoaldosteronism (hypertension, oedema, hypokalaemia). Contraindicated in hypertension, renal failure, liver disease, hypokalaemia, and pregnancy. DGL form is safer for prolonged gastric use. Interactions with antihypertensives, diuretics, and corticosteroids.

Duke (2002) rates licorice as ++ and documents extensive activities. Glycyrrhizin (the key compound) has anti-inflammatory, antiulcer, antiviral, and adrenal-stimulant properties. Duke highlights an important safety concern: chronic use of licorice can cause pseudoaldosteronism — sodium retention, potassium loss, edema, and hypertension — due to glycyrrhizin's inhibition of cortisol metabolism. This effect is typically seen with >50 g licorice/day for more than 6 weeks. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) avoids this side effect. Dose: 5–15 g dried root daily. Contraindicated in hypertension, kidney disease, low potassium, liver cirrhosis, and in combination with diuretics or corticosteroids (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Licorice (glycyrrhizic acid) can cause sodium retention, potassium loss and raised blood pressure (pseudoaldosteronism), which can oppose blood-pressure medicines. Meta-analyses of randomised trials confirm that glycyrrhizic acid raises systolic and diastolic blood pressure, and a crossover trial found this even at a low daily dose (100 mg). Avoid regular or high licorice intake if you take blood-pressure medicines.

Licorice-induced potassium loss can increase the risk of digoxin toxicity; a meta-analysis confirms licorice lowers plasma potassium, and low potassium makes the heart more sensitive to cardiac glycosides. Avoid regular or high intake.

Licorice (glycyrrhizic acid) causes the body to lose potassium; combined with potassium-wasting water tablets (thiazide or loop diuretics such as hydrochlorothiazide or furosemide) this can add up to dangerously low potassium, which a meta-analysis confirms licorice lowers. Licorice also works against potassium-sparing diuretics such as spironolactone by mimicking aldosterone. Avoid regular or high licorice intake with any diuretic.

constituents.name: Triterpenoid saponins (glycyrrhizin/glycyrrhizic acid)
constituents.note: The principal sweet-tasting compound, about 50 times sweeter than sucrose; responsible for the anti-inflammatory, antiviral and gastroprotective activity and also for the pseudoaldosteronism risk on prolonged high-dose use.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and isoflavones (liquiritin, glabridin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and antimicrobial polyphenols, including the skin-lightening compound glabridin.

constituents.name: Coumarins and sterols
constituents.note: Minor supporting constituents of the root.

constituents.name: Gymnemic acids (triterpene saponins)
constituents.note: Main bioactives; suppress sweet taste and are linked to reduced intestinal glucose absorption and improved glycaemic control.

constituents.name: Gurmarin
constituents.note: A peptide that transiently blunts sweet-taste perception.

botanical_description.text: Woody climbing vine with velvety, opposite, oval leaves. Small, inconspicuous, yellowish flowers are borne in umbel-like clusters, followed by slender, boat-shaped seed pods. Chewing the leaf temporarily and selectively blocks the perception of sweetness, giving the plant its Hindi name 'gurmar' (sugar destroyer).
botanical_description.height: Woody climbing vine, several metres over support
botanical_description.leaves: Velvety, opposite, oval; chewing temporarily blocks sweet-taste perception
botanical_description.flowers: Small, inconspicuous, yellowish, in umbel-like clusters
botanical_description.fruit: Slender, boat-shaped seed pod

habitat.text: Native to the tropical forests of India, Sri Lanka and other parts of South and Southeast Asia and tropical Africa, growing as a climber in forest margins and scrub.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked through the growing season and dried for use in decoctions or extracts.

traditional_uses.text: Gymnema leaf (gurmar) has a long Ayurvedic tradition as a remedy for 'madhumeha' (sweet urine, diabetes), chewed or taken as a decoction to reduce sugar cravings and support healthy blood sugar; this traditional antidiabetic reputation is now supported by clinical trials of standardised leaf extracts in type 2 diabetes.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Leaf extract standardised to gymnemic acid content, taken as capsules; the form used in most clinical glycaemic-control studies.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried leaf simmered in water as a traditional Ayurvedic preparation.

preparations.name: Chewed fresh leaf
preparations.text: Fresh leaf chewed directly, the traditional way of demonstrating and using its sweet-taste-blocking effect.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials in type 2 diabetes commonly use around 400-800 mg of standardised leaf extract daily, in divided doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Leaf extracts lower fasting and post-prandial blood glucose and glycated haemoglobin in type 2 diabetics, partly via reduced intestinal glucose absorption (alpha-glucosidase/amylase inhibition) and support of insulin secretion.
Supplementation has been associated with a favourable shift in the lipid profile alongside glucose lowering.

contraindications.note: Gymnema lowers blood sugar and can add to the effect of diabetes medicines or insulin, risking hypoglycaemia; people on such treatment should monitor blood glucose closely and may need a dose adjustment supervised by their clinician. Rare drug-induced liver injury has been reported. Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding (insufficient safety data).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Gymnema lowers blood glucose and can add to the effect of diabetes medicines (metformin, sulfonylureas, insulin and others), so combined use may cause blood sugar to fall too low; monitor closely and adjust medication doses under medical supervision.

pairings.note: Gymnema and fenugreek both lower blood sugar; taking them together may add up and push blood glucose too low, especially alongside diabetes medication, so monitor and use caution.
Gymnema and bitter melon both lower blood sugar; combined use may add up and risk hypoglycaemia, particularly with diabetes drugs, so monitor blood glucose.
Gymnema and cassia cinnamon are both used to lower blood sugar; together their effects may add up, so watch for low blood sugar when combining them or using them with diabetes medication.

parts_used: Bark, leaf and distilled witch-hazel water

constituents.name: Tannins (4-10%, including hamamelitannin and gallotannins)
constituents.note: The principal astringent and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Flavonoids, catechins, proanthocyanidins and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents of the bark and leaf.

botanical_description.text: Large, multi-stemmed deciduous shrub or small tree with broad, oval, wavy-toothed leaves, asymmetrical at the base. Unusually, it flowers in late autumn to winter after the leaves have fallen, bearing clusters of small, spidery, bright yellow flowers with narrow, crinkled, ribbon-like petals. The woody seed capsule matures the following autumn and explosively ejects its seeds ('snapping hazelnut').
botanical_description.habit: Large, multi-stemmed deciduous shrub or small tree
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, oval, wavy-toothed, asymmetrical at the base
botanical_description.flowers: Small, spidery, bright yellow, with narrow crinkled ribbon-like petals, blooming in late autumn/winter
botanical_description.stem: Multiple woody stems with smooth, grey-brown bark
botanical_description.fruit: Woody capsule maturing the following autumn, explosively ejecting seeds

habitat.text: Native to woodland understorey and forest margins of eastern North America; widely cultivated as an ornamental and medicinal shrub.

harvesting.text: Bark is stripped from branches (not the main trunk, to avoid harming the plant) and leaves are picked in summer; both are dried for use, or steam-distilled together with twigs to produce distilled witch-hazel water, the most common commercial form.
parts: Bark, leaf and distilled witch-hazel water
harvesting.season: Bark and leaf in summer

traditional_uses.text: Witch hazel has a long Native American and, after adoption by European settlers, worldwide reputation as a topical astringent and anti-inflammatory remedy for skin irritation, minor wounds, bruises, insect bites and haemorrhoids, and as a soothing wash for irritated skin and mucous membranes.

preparations.name: Distilled witch-hazel water
preparations.text: Steam-distilled preparation of the bark and twigs, applied topically as a soothing, astringent skin toner and wound wash; the most widely used commercial form.

preparations.name: Decoction (bark/leaf)
preparations.text: Dried bark or leaf simmered in water and used as a cooled topical wash or compress.

preparations.name: Cream/ointment
preparations.text: Standardised extract formulated into creams or ointments, particularly for haemorrhoid relief.

dosage.name: Topical preparations
dosage.text: Applied to the affected skin area as needed, several times daily; for topical use only, not for internal use except in professionally prepared, standardised products. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant (supports irritated/atopic skin); Relief of haemorrhoid symptoms (astringent and anti-inflammatory)
Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant (supports irritated/atopic skin)
Astringent for skin irritation and minor inflammation of the skin and oral mucosa (tannins), applied topically; Relief of haemorrhoid symptoms (astringent and anti-inflammatory)
Vulnerary for minor wounds, bruises and insect bites

indications.note: Vulnerary for minor wounds, bruises and insect bites
indications.label: Bruising

indications.note: Relief of haemorrhoid symptoms (astringent and anti-inflammatory)
indications.label: Haemorrhoids

indications.note: Astringent for skin irritation and minor inflammation of the skin and oral mucosa (tannins), applied topically
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Astringent for skin irritation and minor inflammation of the skin and oral mucosa (tannins), applied topically
indications.label: Oral & throat health

indications.note: Astringent for skin irritation and minor inflammation of the skin and oral mucosa (tannins), applied topically
indications.label: Skin irritation

indications.note: Vulnerary for minor wounds, bruises and insect bites
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: For topical use. Do not apply to deep wounds, serious burns or severe rectal bleeding - these need medical care.

Oral use is not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding without professional guidance, and swallowing tannin-rich preparations can upset the stomach. Mild skin irritation or contact allergy is possible.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (harpagoside, harpagide, procumbide)
constituents.note: Harpagoside is the main marker compound and anti-inflammatory principle; effective extracts are standardised to it, with the better-quality clinical evidence using preparations delivering roughly 50-60 mg harpagoside per day.

constituents.name: Phenylethanoids (acteoside/verbascoside) and flavonoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant supporting constituents.

botanical_description.text: Low-growing, sprawling perennial herb with trailing stems and deeply lobed, greyish-green leaves arising from a large, fleshy primary taproot bearing smaller secondary storage tubers (the part used medicinally). Striking funnel-shaped, purplish-pink to violet flowers give way to a distinctive woody fruit covered in long, hooked spines - the origin of the common name.
botanical_description.leaves: Deeply lobed, greyish-green
botanical_description.flowers: Funnel-shaped, purplish-pink to violet
botanical_description.root: Large primary taproot with smaller secondary storage tubers (the medicinal part)
botanical_description.fruit: Woody capsule covered in long, hooked spines ('devil's claw')

habitat.text: Native to the dry savannas and semi-desert regions of southern Africa, particularly the Kalahari Desert region of Namibia, Botswana and South Africa.

harvesting.text: The secondary root tubers are dug by hand, sliced and dried in the sun, traditionally by local harvesters in southern Africa; sustainable wild-harvesting practices are important given the plant's slow growth and habitat sensitivity.

traditional_uses.text: Devil's claw has a long southern African traditional-medicine history as a remedy for fever, pain and digestive complaints, and is now one of the most clinically studied herbal analgesics for osteoarthritis and chronic low back pain, with standardised extracts showing efficacy comparable to some conventional analgesics in trials.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Root tuber extract standardised to harpagoside content, taken as capsules or tablets; the best-studied clinical form for osteoarthritis and back pain.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried root tuber simmered in water, a traditional preparation.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root tuber.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for osteoarthritis and back pain commonly use extracts providing around 50-60 mg harpagoside daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic for osteoarthritis (hip and knee) pain
Anti-inflammatory and analgesic for osteoarthritis (hip and knee) pain

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic for osteoarthritis (hip and knee) pain; General joint pain and arthritis support
indications.label: Arthritis / joint pain

indications.note: Effective at standardised doses (~50-60 mg harpagoside/day); an aqueous extract was non-inferior to rofecoxib for short-term chronic low back pain; Relief of chronic non-specific low back pain
indications.label: Back pain

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic for osteoarthritis (hip and knee) pain; Effective at standardised doses (~50-60 mg harpagoside/day); an aqueous extract was non-inferior to rofecoxib for short-term chronic low back pain; General joint pain and arthritis support; Relief of chronic non-specific low back pain
indications.label: Pain (general)

contraindications.note: Because it is a bitter that increases stomach-acid and bile secretion, it is contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, gastritis and (without medical supervision) gallstones.
May add to the effect of anticoagulants (e.g. warfarin) and may influence blood sugar, blood pressure and heart rhythm; use caution with these medicines.
Avoid during pregnancy (oxytocic/uterine effect) and while breastfeeding.

constituents.name: Triterpene saponins (hederacoside C, alpha-hederin)
constituents.note: The secretolytic and antispasmodic active constituents; extracts are standardised to them.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant supporting constituents.

botanical_description.text: Woody, evergreen climbing or trailing vine that attaches to surfaces by small aerial rootlets.
