Shiitake dermatitis (flagellate erythema) is a rare but recognized adverse effect from eating raw or undercooked shiitake — cooking prevents this (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Tall, upright perennial herb with deeply palmately lobed lower leaves (giving a lion's-paw appearance, the source of the genus name) and progressively less-divided upper leaves. Small, pink to pale purple, spiny-toothed, two-lipped flowers are borne in dense whorls up the square stem.
botanical_description.leaves: Deeply palmately lobed lower leaves; less-divided, three-lobed upper leaves
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pink to pale purple, spiny-toothed, two-lipped, in dense whorls up the stem
botanical_description.stem: Square (typical of the mint family), erect, often reddish
botanical_description.fruit: Small nutlet (typical of Lamiaceae)

habitat.text: Grows on waste ground, roadsides, hedgerows and near old dwellings; native to Central Asia and southeastern Europe and long naturalised across Europe and North America.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaf, stem and flower) are cut in summer as the plant comes into flower, and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Summer, at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Motherwort has a long European and Asian herbal tradition, reflected in its name, as a gynaecological and cardiac tonic, traditionally used for heart palpitations and nervous cardiac complaints, menstrual irregularities and cramps, and as a calming nervine for anxiety and to ease childbirth and postpartum recovery.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water as a traditional cardiac-support and calming tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried flowering herb, typically around 1:5.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-4 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (leonurine)
constituents.note: Leonurine and related iridoid/alkaloid-type constituents are considered the principal cardioactive and uterotonic compounds.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents contributing to the plant's anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Diterpenes (marrubiin-type)
constituents.note: Bitter diterpenes, shared with related Lamiaceae bitters, contributing to the traditional digestive and cardiac reputation.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Contraindicated in pregnancy (uterine stimulant). Avoid in heavy menstrual bleeding. May enhance anticoagulant and antihypertensive drug effects. Avoid in thyroid disorders. Generally well tolerated in normal doses for short-term use.

Duke (2002) rates motherwort as ++ and notes hypotensive, cardiotonic (negative chronotropic), anti-aggregant, and sedative activities at the experimental level (score 1). The plant is traditionally used for heart palpitations, hypertension, and menstrual regulation in European herbalism. Duke emphasizes strong safety caution: motherwort has documented oxytocic (uterine-stimulating) activity and is strictly contraindicated in pregnancy. It may also enhance the effects of cardiac medications and sedatives (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Motherwort is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Erect annual herb, slender and often branched near the top, growing 30-80 (up to 100) cm tall from a fine taproot. Leaves are alternate, sessile, narrowly lanceolate to linear, blue-green and entire-margined. Flowers are pale blue (occasionally white or pink), five-petalled and borne on slender stalks in loose terminal cymes. The fruit is a small globular capsule ('boll') that splits into ten compartments, each containing one flattened, glossy, red-brown seed.
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, sessile, narrowly lanceolate to linear, blue-green, entire
botanical_description.flowers: Pale blue (occasionally white or pink), five-petalled, in loose terminal cymes
botanical_description.fruit: Globular capsule ('boll') splitting into ten compartments, each with one flattened glossy red-brown seed

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean region and the Fertile Crescent, cultivated since prehistory for fibre and oil and now grown worldwide in temperate regions with well-drained, fertile soils and a cool, moist growing season; occasionally found as a naturalised escape on waste and cultivated ground.

harvesting.text: Grown as an annual crop, seed is harvested once the capsules ('bolls') have dried and turned brown, typically in late summer, by cutting or combining the whole plant and threshing out the seed. Whole seed stores well; it should only be ground shortly before use, since the oil in ground seed oxidises and turns rancid quickly.
harvesting.season: Late summer, once bolls have dried

traditional_uses.text: Flaxseed has a long history of traditional use as a bulk-forming laxative and demulcent for constipation and irritated gut mucosa, and as a poultice for boils and inflamed skin. Modern interest centres on its lignan (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside) and alpha-linolenic acid content, studied for cardiovascular, metabolic and hormone-related outcomes.

preparations.name: Ground seed ('flax meal')
preparations.text: Whole seed freshly ground (milled) and taken with plenty of liquid, or stirred into food; grinding is needed to make the oil and lignans bioavailable, as whole seeds largely pass through the gut undigested.

preparations.name: Flaxseed oil
preparations.text: Cold-pressed oil taken as a concentrated source of alpha-linolenic acid (omega-3); lacks the fibre and lignan content of the whole ground seed.

preparations.name: Poultice
preparations.text: Crushed or ground seed made into a warm paste and applied externally to boils and inflamed skin - a traditional demulcent, soothing application.

dosage.name: Ground seed
dosage.text: Traditional guidance and Duke (2002) suggest 1-2 tablespoons of ground seed with ample water, once or twice daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Flaxseed oil
dosage.text: Typical study doses for omega-3 intake have used roughly 1-2 tablespoons (about 10-15 mL) daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Lignans (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside, SDG)
constituents.note: Flax is the richest dietary source of SDG-type lignans, converted by gut bacteria to the mammalian lignans enterodiol and enterolactone; studied for antioxidant and hormone-modulating activity.

constituents.name: Alpha-linolenic acid (omega-3 fatty acid)
constituents.note: The dominant fatty acid of the seed oil (~50-55% of total oil), a plant-based omega-3 studied for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory effects.

constituents.name: Mucilage polysaccharides
constituents.note: Concentrated in the seed coat; gives flaxseed its demulcent, laxative, bulk-forming action.

constituents.name: Cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin, linustatin)
constituents.note: Present at low levels; cooking/roasting reduces them to negligible amounts in normal food use - a theoretical concern only at high raw intakes.

contraindications.note: Flaxseeds are safe for most people at recommended food amounts (1–2 tablespoons ground/day). They must be ground or milled to be bioavailable — whole seeds largely pass through the gut undigested. High fibre content may cause bloating, flatulence, and loose stools, especially if fibre intake is increased rapidly without adequate hydration. Flaxseeds contain cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin, linustatin); cooking or roasting reduces these to negligible levels in normal food amounts. Raw seeds in very large quantities are a theoretical concern, but typical use is safe (EFSA, 2017).

Medication interactions: Flaxseed may reduce absorption of oral medications if taken simultaneously — space flaxseed supplementation 1–2 hours from medications. Omega-3 ALA and lignans may mildly enhance anticoagulant/antiplatelet effects; use caution with blood thinners. Some sources suggest caution with hormone-sensitive conditions due to phytoestrogen (lignan) content, though dietary amounts are unlikely to pose risk (Adolphe et al., 2010).
Pregnancy: Food amounts are generally considered safe; high-dose supplements should be discussed with a clinician due to limited safety data.

Duke (2002) rates flaxseed as +++ with clinical evidence (score 2) for antiatherogenic, hypocholesterolemic, laxative, lipolytic (fat-dissolving), and demulcent activities. Flaxseed's omega-3 fatty acids (ALA) and lignans are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk. Commission E approves flaxseed as a laxative and for mucilaginous protection of inflamed gastrointestinal mucosa. Dose: 1–2 tablespoons ground seed with ample water, twice daily. Whole seeds should be chewed or ground for full medicinal effect. Cyanogenic glycosides are present but at safe levels in normal dietary amounts (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: A tall, erect perennial herb of the primrose family, 60-150 cm high, spreading by creeping rhizomes to form patches. The stems are softly downy and bear lance-shaped leaves in whorls of three or four (or opposite pairs), often gland-dotted. Bright yellow, five-petalled star-shaped flowers, about 15 mm across and often with reddish margins to the sepals, are carried in leafy branching clusters (panicles) at the top of the stem in summer. Despite the shared name 'loosestrife', it is unrelated to purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria).
botanical_description.habit: Tall erect rhizomatous perennial, spreading in patches
botanical_description.leaves: Lance-shaped, in whorls of 3-4 or opposite, often gland-dotted, softly hairy beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Bright yellow, 5-petalled, star-shaped (about 15 mm), in leafy terminal panicles; sepals often red-margined
botanical_description.stem: Erect, softly downy, little-branched below the flower cluster
botanical_description.fruit: A small globular capsule

habitat.text: Wet ground: margins of rivers, lakes, ponds and ditches, fens, marshes, wet meadows and damp woodland, across Europe and temperate Asia (and naturalised in North America). It favours moist to waterlogged, fertile soils in sun or light shade and can form dense stands.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaves, stems, flowers) are gathered in summer while in flower and dried; the root/rhizome is also used. The plant is best cut from established patches in wet ground.
harvesting.season: Aerial parts in flower (June-August); root in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Yellow loosestrife has a European folk-medicine reputation as an astringent and styptic: the flavonoid- and tannin-rich herb was used to stop bleeding (nosebleeds, wounds), for diarrhoea and dysentery, as a gargle and wash for mouth ulcers and sore throat, and to soothe inflammation. The dried herb was also burned to repel insects. More broadly, the genus Lysimachia is important in East Asian medicine - the closely related L. vulgaris var. davurica is used for liver and digestive complaints, while other congeners such as L. christinae (jin qian cao) are classic remedies for urinary and gallstones - reflecting a shared saponin and flavonoid chemistry.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: The dried flowering herb infused as an astringent tea for diarrhoea and as a gargle/wash for mouth and throat inflammation.

preparations.name: Poultice / wash
preparations.text: The fresh or dried herb applied as a styptic poultice or wash to stop bleeding and soothe wounds (traditional).

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the aerial parts (about 1:5).

dosage.name: Dried herb infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal use: roughly 2-4 g of dried flowering herb per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily. Educational reference only.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 tincture up to three times daily in adults. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (rutin, quercetin, kaempferol, myricitrin glycosides)
constituents.note: Flavonol glycosides - notably rutin - are the principal antioxidant constituents of yellow loosestrife.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids
constituents.note: Caffeic-acid derivatives and chlorogenic acid (antioxidant).

constituents.name: Triterpenoid saponins
constituents.note: Oleanane-type triterpenoid saponins characteristic of the genus Lysimachia.

constituents.name: Isocoumarins and benzoquinones
constituents.note: Root isocoumarins and benzoquinones with PCSK9-inhibitory (lipid-lowering) activity.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Astringent tannins underlying the traditional styptic/antidiarrhoeal use.

constituents.name: Lignans and loliolide
constituents.note: The lignan pinoresinol and loliolide, which reduce hepatic lipogenesis.

actions.note: Antibacterial activity of the phenolic-rich extract.
Antitumor activity of L. vulgaris extract; genus congeners (L. christinae, L. capillipes saponins) show anticancer activity.
Ameliorates liver fibrosis and NASH in mice (L. vulgaris var. davurica).
Root isocoumarins/benzoquinones inhibit PCSK9 expression and loliolide/pinoresinol reduce hepatic lipogenesis via liver-X-receptor degradation.
Improves diarrhoea-predominant IBS (L. vulgaris var. davurica); traditional astringent antidiarrhoeal.
Flavonoid- and tannin-rich herb; the basis of the traditional styptic and antidiarrhoeal use.

indications.note: Ameliorates liver fibrosis/NASH in animal models.
indications.label: Liver support

indications.note: PCSK9 inhibition and reduced hepatic lipogenesis (lipid-lowering).
indications.label: High cholesterol

indications.note: Improves NASH/metabolic and lipid parameters in animal models.
indications.label: Metabolic support

indications.note: Improves diarrhoea-predominant IBS; traditional astringent antidiarrhoeal.
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Improved diarrhoea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (var. davurica).
indications.label: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)

contraindications.note: Safety data are limited and there are no human clinical trials; avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Contains astringent tannins and flavonoids; large or prolonged doses may cause digestive upset and can reduce absorption of iron and some medicines.

Much of the pharmacological data are for Lysimachia vulgaris var. davurica and closely related Lysimachia species (e.g. L. christinae, L. foenum-graecum); apply to the plain species with appropriate caution.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Yellow loosestrife root constituents inhibit PCSK9 expression and reduce hepatic lipogenesis; taken with statins or other lipid-lowering drugs they could theoretically add to cholesterol-lowering effects.

pairings.note: Both are Primulaceae rich in triterpenoid saponins and flavonoids; related family chemistry rather than a specific therapeutic combination.

constituents.name: Isoquinoline alkaloids (berberine, berbamine, palmatine, jatrorrhizine, magnoflorine)
constituents.note: Berberine is the main anti-psoriatic and antimicrobial constituent.

botanical_description.text: Evergreen shrub, 0.5-2 m tall, spreading by underground rhizomes to form thickets. The pinnate leaves resemble holly, with 5-9 glossy, dark green, spiny-toothed leaflets that often turn bronze-red in winter. Small, bright yellow, six-petalled flowers are borne in dense, upright terminal racemes in early spring, followed by clusters of blue-black, grape-like berries with a waxy bloom. The wood and inner bark of the stem and root are bright yellow when cut, owing to their high content of berberine-type alkaloids.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnate, holly-like, 5-9 glossy dark green spiny-toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Small, bright yellow, six-petalled, in dense upright terminal racemes
botanical_description.stem: Woody; wood and inner bark bright yellow when cut
botanical_description.root: Woody, rhizomatous, bright yellow within (berberine-type alkaloids)
botanical_description.fruit: Clusters of blue-black, grape-like berries with a waxy bloom

habitat.text: Native to the Pacific Northwest of North America, growing in coniferous woodland understorey, forest margins and rocky slopes; widely introduced and naturalised as an ornamental and hedging shrub in temperate regions elsewhere.

harvesting.text: The root and root bark, the medicinal parts, are dug from established plants - traditionally in autumn - and dried. Because wild populations are slow-growing, cultivated or nursery-sourced material is preferred over wild-harvesting.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Oregon grape root has a traditional Native American and Eclectic-medicine history as a bitter tonic and alterative (blood-purifying) remedy and for skin conditions. Its main modern use, supported by clinical trials, is topical application of a standardised bark extract for mild-to-moderate psoriasis and atopic dermatitis (eczema), where its berberine-type alkaloids give anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative effects on skin cells.

preparations.name: Topical cream/ointment
preparations.text: A standardised 10% Mahonia aquifolium bark extract cream, the best-studied form, applied to affected skin up to three times daily for psoriasis or eczema.

preparations.name: Tincture/decoction of root bark
preparations.text: Traditional internal preparation of the dried root bark as a bitter tonic; far less studied than the topical form.

dosage.name: Topical 10% extract cream
dosage.text: Clinical trials in psoriasis and eczema have used a 10% Mahonia aquifolium bark extract cream applied to affected areas up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription; internal use is not well studied and should be supervised.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative (slows the skin-cell overgrowth of psoriasis)
Anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative (slows the skin-cell overgrowth of psoriasis)
Antimicrobial (berberine) - supports skin and mucosal infection

indications.note: Topical for atopic dermatitis (eczema) and skin irritation
indications.label: Eczema

indications.note: Antimicrobial (berberine) - supports skin and mucosal infection
indications.label: Infection (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative (slows the skin-cell overgrowth of psoriasis); Topical treatment of mild-to-moderate psoriasis (reduces plaque severity) - a double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT showed significant improvement in PASI and quality-of-life scores
indications.label: Psoriasis

indications.note: Topical for atopic dermatitis (eczema) and skin irritation
indications.label: Skin irritation

contraindications.note: Avoid internal use in pregnancy and breastfeeding: like goldenseal, the berberine it contains can cross the placenta and into milk and worsen newborn jaundice (kernicterus risk).

Berberine impairs the metabolism of some medicines - for example it raises blood levels of cyclosporine, risking toxicity - so use caution with prescription drugs. It is used mainly topically for skin conditions.

constituents.name: Mucilage polysaccharides
constituents.note: The high mucilage content gives the demulcent, soothing action.

constituents.name: Anthocyanins (malvin) and flavonoids
constituents.note: The blue-purple pigments and antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Coumarins and other phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Phenolic constituents associated with antioxidant and reported antiproliferative (anticancer) activity in cell studies.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Mildly astringent constituents.

botanical_description.text: Biennial or short-lived perennial herb, 30-120 cm tall, with erect or sprawling, branched, often reddish stems. Leaves are alternate, long-stalked and palmately lobed with 5-7 shallow rounded lobes and toothed margins. Flowers are pink-purple with darker veining, with five notch-tipped petals borne in small axillary clusters. The fruit is a flattened, disc-shaped schizocarp ('cheese') that splits into a ring of small single-seeded nutlets.
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, long-stalked, palmately 5-7-lobed with toothed margins
botanical_description.flowers: Pink-purple with darker veins, five notched petals, in small axillary clusters
botanical_description.stem: Erect or sprawling, branched, often reddish
botanical_description.fruit: Flattened disc-shaped schizocarp ('cheese') of small nutlets

habitat.text: Common on roadsides, waste ground, hedgebanks and field margins across Europe, North Africa and temperate Asia, and widely naturalised elsewhere; tolerates a wide range of soils, favouring sunny, disturbed, nitrogen-rich sites.

harvesting.text: Leaves and flowers are gathered during the flowering period (summer) and dried quickly out of direct sun to preserve their mucilage content; fresh material can also be used directly for infusions or poultices.
harvesting.season: Summer, during flowering

traditional_uses.text: Mallow leaf and flower have a long ethnobotanical history across Europe as a demulcent for irritated mucous membranes - soothing coughs, sore throat and mouth irritation - and for occasional constipation and skin irritation, reflecting the mucilage-rich leaf and flower. Modern phytochemical and pharmacological work confirms antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial and wound-healing activity of its extracts.

preparations.name: Infusion (hot)
preparations.text: Dried leaf and/or flower steeped in hot water as a traditional soothing tea for cough, sore throat and mild digestive irritation.

preparations.name: Cold macerate
preparations.text: Leaf/flower soaked in cold water for several hours, a traditional technique that preserves more of the heat-sensitive mucilage than a hot infusion.

preparations.name: Poultice/compress
preparations.text: Crushed fresh or softened dried leaf applied externally to soothe irritated or inflamed skin.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: EMA herbal-monograph guidance suggests roughly 1.5-2 g of dried leaf or flower per cup, taken as an infusion up to 3 times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory, analgesic and wound-healing (preclinical)
Anti-inflammatory, analgesic and wound-healing (preclinical)
Antibacterial / antimicrobial (methanolic leaf extracts; the naphthoquinone Malvone A)
Antioxidant (phenolic compounds and anthocyanins)
Demulcent for irritation of the mouth or throat and associated dry cough (soothing mucilage); Mild laxative for occasional constipation (mucilage; traditional)
Mild laxative for occasional constipation (mucilage; traditional)
Anti-inflammatory, analgesic and wound-healing (preclinical)

indications.note: Mild laxative for occasional constipation (mucilage; traditional)
indications.label: Constipation

indications.note: Demulcent for irritation of the mouth or throat and associated dry cough (soothing mucilage)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Soothes irritated and inflamed skin and mucous membranes
indications.label: Skin irritation

contraindications.note: Generally very well tolerated. Irritation of the mouth/throat or a cough that persists beyond about a week, or is accompanied by fever or breathlessness, needs medical assessment.
Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of adequate data.

constituents.name: Labdane diterpene marrubiin
constituents.note: The characteristic bitter principle (pharmacopoeial material contains at least 0.7%); drives the expectorant and digestive actions. A phytochemical/pharmacological overview identifies marrubiin and the phenolic fraction as the basis for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenylethanoid esters (e.g. verbascoside)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Tannins and volatile oil
constituents.note: Astringent and aromatic constituents of the herb.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb, 20-60 cm tall, with square, densely white-woolly (tomentose) stems characteristic of the mint family. Leaves are opposite, ovate-rounded, crinkled and coarsely toothed, grey-green and woolly beneath, with a strong bitter, slightly musky smell when crushed. Small white, two-lipped flowers are clustered in dense whorls (verticillasters) in the leaf axils up the stem. The fruit splits into four small nutlets typical of the Lamiaceae.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, ovate-rounded, crinkled, coarsely toothed, woolly beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, two-lipped, in dense axillary whorls (verticillasters)
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to Europe, North Africa and temperate Asia and widely naturalised elsewhere, growing on dry, disturbed, sunny ground - roadsides, waste places, pastures and rocky or sandy soils.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts are cut during the flowering season (summer), when bitter marrubiin content is highest, and dried promptly out of direct sunlight; harvest site and season measurably affect the marrubiin and antioxidant content of the dried herb.
harvesting.season: Summer, during flowering

traditional_uses.text: White horehound has a long European and Mediterranean folk-medicine history as a bitter expectorant for coughs, bronchitis and catarrh, and as a digestive bitter for poor appetite and dyspepsia. It is one of the classic ingredients of traditional cough syrups and horehound candy. A modern critical review found reasonable, though not conclusive, scientific support for these traditional respiratory and digestive uses.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried aerial parts steeped in hot water as a bitter digestive and expectorant tea.

preparations.name: Decoction/syrup
preparations.text: A stronger decoction, traditionally sweetened into a syrup (or made into horehound candy), for cough and sore throat.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts, taken as a bitter digestive.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: EMA/ESCOP herbal-monograph guidance suggests about 1.5-2 g of dried aerial parts per cup, as an infusion up to 3 times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (traditional use in whooping cough)
Bitter digestive for dyspeptic complaints (indigestion, bloating, flatulence) and loss of appetite
Bitter digestive for dyspeptic complaints (indigestion, bloating, flatulence) and loss of appetite
Expectorant for productive cough and catarrh of the upper respiratory tract / bronchitis

indications.note: Bitter digestive for dyspeptic complaints (indigestion, bloating, flatulence) and loss of appetite
indications.label: Loss of appetite

indications.note: Bitter digestive for dyspeptic complaints (indigestion, bloating, flatulence) and loss of appetite
indications.label: Bloating

indications.note: Expectorant for productive cough and catarrh of the upper respiratory tract / bronchitis
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Antispasmodic (traditional use in whooping cough); Expectorant for productive cough and catarrh of the upper respiratory tract / bronchitis
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Bitter digestive for dyspeptic complaints (indigestion, bloating, flatulence) and loss of appetite
indications.label: Indigestion

contraindications.note: As a strong bitter it may aggravate hyperacidity; theoretical caution in peptic ulcer. Large amounts have shown effects on heart rhythm and blood pressure in studies, so avoid excessive intake.
Traditional use only; a cough or digestive complaint that persists needs medical assessment. Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic annual herb, 15-60 cm tall, with an erect, much-branched, hairless stem. Leaves are alternate and finely bi- to tripinnately divided into thread-like segments, giving a feathery appearance. Flower heads are small daisy-like composites, 1-2.5 cm across, with white ray florets that reflex sharply downward as the flower matures around a conical, hollow, yellow disc of tubular florets - the hollow, conical receptacle is a key feature distinguishing German chamomile from similar-looking daisies. The fruit is a tiny ribbed achene without a pappus.
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, finely 2-3-pinnately divided into thread-like segments
botanical_description.flowers: Small daisy-like heads with downward-reflexed white ray florets around a conical hollow yellow disc
botanical_description.fruit: Tiny ribbed achene, no pappus

habitat.text: Native to Europe and western Asia and now naturalised worldwide, growing on disturbed, sunny ground - arable field margins, waste places, roadsides and sandy or loamy soils; widely cultivated as a medicinal crop.

harvesting.text: Flower heads are hand- or machine-harvested at full bloom, when the ray florets are horizontal and just beginning to reflex, then dried quickly at low temperature and out of direct light to preserve the volatile oil.
harvesting.season: Late spring to summer, at full bloom

traditional_uses.text: Chamomile is one of the most widely used medicinal herbs in the world, with a long traditional history as a mild digestive, calming and anti-inflammatory remedy - taken as a tea for indigestion, cramping, and restlessness or poor sleep, and applied topically for skin and mucous-membrane inflammation and wound healing. Modern reviews confirm anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, sedative/anxiolytic and antimicrobial pharmacological activity supporting these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried flower heads steeped in hot water, the classic way of taking chamomile for digestive complaints, mild anxiety and sleep support.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the flower heads; a concentrated source of alpha-bisabolol and chamazulene, used mainly in topical and aromatherapy preparations.

preparations.name: Topical cream/compress
preparations.text: Concentrated extract or infused oil applied to skin, or as a compress - a randomised controlled trial found a topical chamomile oil preparation effective for knee osteoarthritis pain.

preparations.name: Mouthwash/gargle
preparations.text: Infusion used as a mouthwash or gargle, traditionally for mouth and throat irritation.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional guidance and Duke (2002) suggest about 3 g of dried flower heads per cup of water, taken up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (alpha-bisabolol, chamazulene)
constituents.note: The volatile oil, concentrated in the flower head, gives chamomile its characteristic blue colour (chamazulene) and much of its anti-inflammatory activity (alpha-bisabolol).

constituents.name: Flavonoids (apigenin, quercetin, luteolin, rutin)
constituents.note: Apigenin is considered a key contributor to chamomile's sedative/anxiolytic activity via GABA-A receptor binding.

constituents.name: Coumarins
constituents.note: Minor constituents (e.g. herniarin, umbelliferone) contributing to the mild antispasmodic action.

constituents.name: Mucilage polysaccharides
constituents.note: Contribute to the soothing, demulcent effect on irritated mucous membranes.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Generally considered very safe when used as tea or topical preparations.Allergy risk: People allergic to ragweed, daisies, or other Asteraceae plants may react (itching, rash).Blood thinners: Chamomile may slightly enhance effects of anticoagulants—use large amounts cautiously.Pregnancy: Normal tea amounts are usually considered safe, but concentrated extracts should be used with caution.Topical use: Rare skin irritation in sensitive individuals.

Duke (2002) rates German chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) highly for anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and wound-healing activities. The essential oil contains the potent anti-inflammatory compound alpha-bisabolol. Chamomile is Commission E approved for digestive complaints and topical wound care. Dose: 3 g dried flower heads per cup of water, three times daily. Alpha-bisabolol is noted as one of the most active natural anti-inflammatory compounds known. Caution: chamomile belongs to the Asteraceae family and can cause allergic reactions in individuals sensitive to ragweed, chrysanthemums, or related plants (Duke, 2002).

Generally very safe. Rare allergic reactions in individuals sensitive to the Asteraceae family (ragweed, chrysanthemums). May cause contact dermatitis with topical essential oil use. Avoid very high doses in pregnancy. Well tolerated by children in appropriate amounts.

Scented mayweed (Matricaria chamomilla) shares the same species as German chamomile (see German Chamomile entry above). Duke (2002) provides the same clinical support: Commission E approves it for dyspeptic complaints (orally) and skin and mucous membrane inflammation (topically). Anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and wound-healing activities are well-established (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Chamomile contains natural coumarin constituents and has been linked in a case report to increased bleeding when taken with warfarin, so caution is advised when combining it with anticoagulant or antiplatelet drugs.

Chamomile has calming, sedative effects (a randomised controlled trial found it reduces generalised-anxiety symptoms); taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.
Chamomile improved blood-sugar control (lower HbA1c and insulin resistance) in a trial in type 2 diabetes; combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering, so monitor blood sugar.

pairings.note: Chamomile and valerian are both traditional mild sedatives for restlessness and poor sleep; taken together they may enhance calming and sleep effects but also add to drowsiness.
Chamomile and lemon balm are both gentle calming herbs commonly combined for stress and sleep; used together their mild sedative effects may add up.
Chamomile and passionflower are both gentle calming, sleep-supporting herbs traditionally combined; used together they may reinforce each other's soothing, sleep-promoting effect.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb, 30-70 (up to 150) cm tall, with square, branched, hairy stems typical of the mint family. Leaves are opposite, ovate to heart-shaped with a toothed margin, wrinkled and softly hairy, releasing a strong lemon scent when crushed. Small, pale yellow to white, two-lipped flowers are borne in loose whorls in the leaf axils. The fruit splits into four small nutlets.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, ovate to heart-shaped, toothed, wrinkled, lemon-scented when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale yellow to white, two-lipped, in loose axillary whorls
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to southern Europe, the Mediterranean and western Asia, widely cultivated and naturalised in gardens, hedgebanks and waste ground across temperate regions, favouring sunny to lightly shaded, well-drained soils.

harvesting.text: Leaves and flowering tops are cut just before or at the start of flowering (summer), when the volatile-oil content is highest, and dried quickly at low temperature away from direct light, since the aromatic oil is heat- and light-sensitive.
harvesting.season: Summer, just before or at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Lemon balm has a long European tradition as a calming, 'cheering' herb for nervous tension, poor sleep and mild anxiety, and as a carminative for indigestion, bloating and cramping; topically it has traditionally been applied to cold sores. Modern clinical trials support anxiolytic and mood-related benefits and antiviral activity against herpes simplex virus, consistent with these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water, the classic way of taking lemon balm for calming and digestive support.

preparations.name: Standardised extract capsule
preparations.text: Encapsulated dry extract, the form used in clinical trials studying lemon balm for anxiety and mood; follow product-specific dosing.

preparations.name: Topical cream
preparations.text: Concentrated leaf extract cream applied to cold sores (herpes labialis) at the earliest sign of an outbreak.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the fresh or dried leaf.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional herbal guidance suggests roughly 1.5-4.5 g dried leaf per cup, as an infusion up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription; Duke (2002) has no dedicated dosing entry for lemon balm.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for anxiety and mood have used standardised dry-extract capsules; dosing varies by product concentration, so follow the manufacturer's guidance. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Rosmarinic acid and phenolic acids
constituents.note: The principal phenolic acid of lemon balm, associated with much of its antioxidant and antiviral activity.

constituents.name: Essential oil (citral, citronellal)
constituents.note: The volatile oil (geranial and neral, together called citral, plus citronellal) gives the characteristic lemon scent and has demonstrated anxiolytic activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Contribute to the antioxidant activity of the leaf.

constituents.name: Triterpenic acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid)
constituents.note: Minor constituents studied for anti-inflammatory activity.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Generally very safe for adults. Theoretically may interact with thyroid medications and sedatives. Avoid high therapeutic doses during pregnancy. Long-term safety data beyond a few months is limited.
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry under 'Lemon Balm' or 'Melissa' in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Lemon balm has calming, sedative effects (a randomised controlled trial found it reduces anxiety, stress and sleep disturbance); taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

pairings.note: Lemon balm and passionflower are both gentle, GABA-acting calming herbs often blended together in anti-anxiety and sleep formulas; combined use may deepen relaxation but also add to drowsiness.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb, 30-90 cm tall, a sterile natural hybrid (Mentha aquatica x M. spicata) that spreads by rhizomes and surface stolons rather than viable seed. Stems are square, often purple-tinged. Leaves are opposite, lance-shaped to ovate with a sharply toothed margin, and release a strong menthol scent when crushed. Small, pale purple flowers are borne in dense, interrupted terminal spikes.
botanical_description.habit: Rhizomatous, mat-forming perennial herb, sterile hybrid
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, lance-shaped to ovate, sharply toothed, strongly menthol-scented
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pale purple, in dense interrupted terminal spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Rarely sets seed (sterile hybrid); propagated vegetatively

habitat.text: A cultivated hybrid grown worldwide as a crop and garden herb, propagated vegetatively since it rarely sets viable seed; naturalised in damp ground - streambanks, ditches and waste places - in temperate regions.

harvesting.text: Aerial parts are cut at or just before full flowering (mid- to late summer), when the essential-oil (menthol) content peaks, then dried quickly out of direct sun, or distilled fresh for essential oil.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer, at or just before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Peppermint has one of the best-documented traditional uses of any herb - as a carminative and antispasmodic for indigestion, bloating and colic. Enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules are among the most clinically validated herbal treatments for irritable bowel syndrome, and topical or inhaled peppermint oil is traditionally and clinically used for tension-type headache and muscular aches.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water, the classic digestive tea.

preparations.name: Enteric-coated oil capsules
preparations.text: Standardised peppermint oil in enteric-coated capsules, the clinically studied form for irritable bowel syndrome, releasing the oil in the intestine rather than the stomach.

preparations.name: Essential oil (topical)
preparations.text: Diluted essential oil applied to the temples and neck for tension-type headache.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the leaf.

dosage.name: Enteric-coated oil capsules
dosage.text: Duke (2002) and Commission E guidance: about 0.6 mL enteric-coated oil two to three times daily between meals for IBS-type digestive complaints. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Dried leaf infusion
dosage.text: Traditional guidance: about 1.5-3 g dried leaf as a tea, up to three times daily for digestive complaints. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (menthol, menthone)
constituents.note: Menthol is the dominant constituent of the leaf oil, responsible for the cooling sensation and much of the antispasmodic and analgesic activity.

constituents.name: Rosmarinic acid and flavonoids (eriocitrin, luteolin)
constituents.note: Phenolic antioxidants of the leaf.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Contribute mild astringency.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Generally very safe. Essential oil should not be applied to the face or chest of infants or children — menthol can cause respiratory depression. Avoid enteric-coated capsules if taking antacids or acid-suppressing drugs. May worsen GERD by relaxing the lower oesophageal sphincter. Avoid in bile duct obstruction and gallstones.

Duke (2002) rates peppermint as +++ with clinical evidence (score 2) for antispasmodic activity, consistent with Commission E approval for IBS (irritable bowel syndrome) using enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules. Enteric-coated peppermint oil is one of the most clinically validated herbal treatments for IBS, reducing abdominal pain and spasm. Dose: 0.6 ml enteric-coated oil two to three times daily between meals (for IBS); 1.5–3 g dried leaf as tea three times daily for digestive complaints. Peppermint should not be applied near the face or nostrils of infants and small children (risk of laryngospasm from menthol). Contraindicated in cholelithiasis (gallstones) (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Cucurbitane-type triterpenoids (momordicosides)
constituents.note: Now regarded as the main antidiabetic principles.

constituents.name: Charantin
constituents.note: A mixture of two sterol glucosides, historically credited with the hypoglycaemic effect.

constituents.name: Polypeptide-p ('plant insulin')
constituents.note: An insulin-like polypeptide of the fruit and seed.

constituents.name: Vicine (in the seeds)
constituents.note: A pyrimidine glycoside that can trigger favism in G6PD-deficient people.

botanical_description.text: Climbing or trailing annual vine (Cucurbitaceae) that climbs by simple tendrils, typically 2-5 m long. Leaves are alternate, long-stalked and palmately lobed with 5-7 deep, toothed lobes. Flowers are small, yellow and five-petalled, male and female flowers separate on the same plant. The fruit is an oblong, warty-skinned gourd that ripens from green to orange-yellow and splits open to reveal seeds coated in a bright red aril; the unripe green fruit is the part used medicinally and as food.
botanical_description.habit: Climbing or trailing annual vine, tendril-bearing
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, long-stalked, palmately 5-7-lobed with toothed margins
botanical_description.flowers: Small, yellow, five-petalled, male and female separate on the same plant
botanical_description.fruit: Oblong, warty-skinned gourd, green ripening to orange-yellow, splitting to reveal red-arilled seeds
botanical_description.flowering_period: Summer, through the growing season in warm climates

habitat.text: Likely native to tropical Asia (possibly the Indian subcontinent), now widely cultivated across tropical and subtropical Asia, Africa, the Caribbean and increasingly the Americas as a vegetable and medicinal crop; needs warmth, full sun and a trellis or support to climb.

harvesting.text: The fruit is harvested unripe, while still green and firm, before it yellows and splits, for use as a vegetable and in traditional antidiabetic preparations; leaves are also traditionally gathered.
harvesting.season: Unripe fruit throughout the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Bitter melon has a long history in Asian, African and Caribbean traditional medicine as a bitter digestive tonic and mild laxative, and most notably as a remedy for diabetes and its complications; this traditional antidiabetic use is now the most scientifically supported of its properties, with numerous studies on its blood-glucose- and lipid-lowering effects.

preparations.name: Fresh fruit / juice
preparations.text: The unripe fruit eaten cooked as a vegetable or juiced fresh - the traditional food-medicine form used for blood-sugar support.

preparations.name: Dried fruit tea
preparations.text: Dried fruit slices infused in hot water.

preparations.name: Standardised extract capsule
preparations.text: Encapsulated dry fruit extract, the form used in clinical trials for blood-sugar support.

dosage.name: Fruit extract / juice
dosage.text: Clinical trials studying bitter melon for blood-sugar support have used standardised fruit extract or juice preparations taken daily over several weeks; dosing varies by product. Because it can lower blood glucose, monitor levels closely if combining with diabetes medication. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Antidiabetic / blood-sugar lowering (improves glucose uptake and GLUT4 translocation)
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Bitter digestive / stomachic (traditional use for poor digestion); mild laxative
Bitter digestive / stomachic (traditional use for poor digestion); mild laxative
Bitter digestive / stomachic (traditional use for poor digestion); mild laxative

indications.note: Antidiabetic / blood-sugar lowering (improves glucose uptake and GLUT4 translocation)
indications.label: Blood sugar / diabetes support

contraindications.note: Avoid in pregnancy: bitter melon can stimulate the uterus and has been associated with miscarriage; also avoid during breastfeeding.
Strong blood-sugar-lowering action: used with insulin or other diabetes medicines it can cause hypoglycaemia - monitor blood glucose and seek medical advice.

People with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency should avoid the seeds, which contain vicine and can cause favism (haemolytic anaemia). Excessive intake may cause abdominal pain and diarrhoea.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Bitter melon has a blood-sugar-lowering effect (a meta-analysis of randomised trials in type 2 diabetes found reduced fasting glucose and HbA1c), so combining it with diabetes medicines may increase the risk of hypoglycaemia; monitor blood glucose.

botanical_description.text: Small evergreen tree or shrub (Rubiaceae), 3-10 m tall, with large, glossy, dark green, elliptical leaves marked by prominent parallel veins. Small, white, tubular, fragrant flowers are borne in dense rounded heads, developing into a compound fleshy fruit (syncarp) that is warty, semi-translucent and yellow-white when ripe, with a strong, pungent, cheese-like odour.
botanical_description.leaves: Large, glossy, dark green, elliptical, prominently veined
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, tubular, fragrant, in dense rounded heads
botanical_description.fruit: Compound fleshy syncarp, warty and semi-translucent, yellow-white when ripe, pungent cheese-like odour
botanical_description.flowering_period: Year-round in tropical climates, with peak flushes seasonally

habitat.text: Native to Southeast Asia and Australasia, and dispersed across the Pacific islands and into India and the Caribbean by early Polynesian voyagers and subsequent cultivation; grows in coastal areas, open and disturbed forest, and even on bare lava flows, tolerating poor, saline and drought-prone soils.

harvesting.text: Ripe fruit is hand-picked as it softens and turns yellow-white, then traditionally left to further ripen or ferment for juice production; leaves and root are also traditionally gathered.
harvesting.season: Fruit harvested as it ripens, largely year-round in the tropics

traditional_uses.text: Noni has an extensive Polynesian, South Asian and Southeast Asian traditional-medicine history, used as a broad-spectrum tonic for pain, inflammation, infection and immune support, and traditionally for diabetes and skin complaints. The fermented fruit juice is the most widely used modern preparation, now studied for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory and metabolic effects supporting several of these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Fermented fruit juice
preparations.text: The classic Polynesian preparation: ripe fruit fermented in sealed containers for several weeks, then strained; the most-studied modern form.

preparations.name: Fresh fruit
preparations.text: Ripe or unripe fruit eaten as food (and historically as a famine food) in some Pacific cultures.

preparations.name: Leaf preparation
preparations.text: Leaves used traditionally as a poultice for wounds and skin complaints, or infused as a tea.

preparations.name: Root preparation
preparations.text: Root traditionally used as a source of yellow/red dye and in folk medicine.

dosage.name: Fermented fruit juice
dosage.text: Commercial noni juice is typically taken in modest daily amounts (a few tablespoons); given rare reports of hepatotoxicity with excessive or prolonged intake, avoid high doses, especially with pre-existing liver disease. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Iridoids (asperulosidic acid and related iridoid glycosides)
constituents.note: A major phytochemical class of noni fruit and leaf, studied for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Anthraquinones (damnacanthal and related compounds)
constituents.note: Found mainly in the root and, at lower levels, the fruit; studied for anticancer and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Polysaccharides
constituents.note: Fruit polysaccharides studied for gut-microbiota-modulating and anti-inflammatory effects in animal models of inflammatory bowel disease.

constituents.name: Scopoletin (coumarin)
constituents.note: A phenolic coumarin found in the fruit, studied for anti-inflammatory and vascular effects.

contraindications.note: Generally safe in moderate amounts. High potassium content — use caution in renal disease. Rare cases of hepatotoxicity reported with excessive consumption. May interact with warfarin (due to vitamin K content). Avoid during pregnancy.

Duke (2002) rates noni as +++ and notes analgesic, antirheumatic, antiarthritic, antitumor, antispasmodic, and hypotensive activities at the experimental level (score 1). Traditional Polynesian and South Asian medicine values it as a broad-spectrum tonic. The plant contains morindine, proxeronine, and damnacanthal, which are under investigation for immunomodulatory and anti-tumor effects. Duke notes that noni fruit can be consumed as food and is generally safe, though the juice has a strong unpleasant odor and taste. No significant toxicity has been reported with regular food consumption (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb, 30-100 cm tall, with greyish-green, downy, square stems typical of the mint family. Leaves are opposite, triangular-ovate with scalloped or toothed margins, grey-green above and softly felted (whitish) beneath, releasing a minty-lemony aroma when crushed. Small white flowers, spotted with purple or pink, are borne in dense terminal and axillary spikes. Nepetalactone, the volatile compound famous for its effect on cats, is concentrated in glandular hairs on the leaves and stems.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, triangular-ovate, scalloped/toothed, felted white beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white with purple/pink spotting, in dense terminal and axillary spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to Europe and temperate Asia and widely naturalised in North America; grows on disturbed, sunny ground - roadsides, waste places, hedgebanks and dry, calcareous or well-drained soils.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts (leaf, stem and flower) are cut at or just before flowering (summer), when volatile nepetalactone content is highest, and dried quickly out of direct light.
harvesting.season: Summer, at or just before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Catnip has a long North American and European folk-medicine history as a mild sedative/nervine, diaphoretic (fever-reducing, sweat-promoting) remedy, carminative digestive and traditional emmenagogue; it was also given as a children's tea for colic and restlessness, though large amounts are not advised for young children and it is traditionally avoided in pregnancy.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried aerial parts steeped in hot water as a traditional calming, digestive tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the aerial parts; used mainly as a natural insect repellent (nepetalactone) rather than taken internally.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional herbal guidance suggests a mild infusion of the dried aerial parts, up to a few cups daily for adults. Keep away from toddlers/young children beyond very small amounts and avoid medicinal-dose use in pregnancy. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Iridoid monoterpenes (nepetalactone)
constituents.note: The characteristic volatile iridoid responsible for the cat-attractant effect and studied for sedative, antimicrobial and insect-repellent activity.

constituents.name: Essential oil
constituents.note: Rich in nepetalactone isomers along with other monoterpenes; studied for antimicrobial and antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Contribute to the antioxidant activity of the aerial parts.

contraindications.note: Pregnancy: Do not use (traditional concern around stimulating menstruation/uterus) (Health Canada, n.d.). Breastfeeding: Health Canada advises talk to a healthcare professional first (Health Canada, n.d.). Kids: It’s often described as a “gentle” traditional tea, but large amounts are not a good idea—there’s a published case where a toddler became very sleepy/“slowed down” after consuming a lot. Keep catnip tea away from toddlers unless a clinician advises it (Osterhoudt et al., 1997). Sedatives / alcohol: Catnip can be calming, so don’t stack it with other sedating herbs/sleep meds/alcohol (you could get extra drowsy) (WebMD, n.d.a). Essential oil caution: Catnip essential oil is concentrated. Research notes it may cause mild skin irritation for some people—always dilute, patch test, and never treat it like “tea.” (Reichert et al., 2019)

Duke (2002) describes catnip primarily as a sedative, diaphoretic, and emmenagogue with experimental (score 1) support. It has demonstrated uterotonic and oxytocic activity, making it contraindicated in pregnancy. The active compounds include nepetalactone and iridoids. Catnip is classified as a mild nervine and antispasmodic in traditional North American herbal medicine, though clinical trials are lacking. Due to its emmenagogue and uterotonic properties, medicinal-dose use should be avoided during pregnancy (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Catnip is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Annual herb (Ranunculaceae), 20-90 cm tall, with an erect, branched stem and finely divided, pinnately dissected, fern-like leaves. Delicate pale blue-white (occasionally pale purple) flowers, each with 5-10 petal-like sepals, are surrounded by a feathery bract collar. The fruit is an inflated, many-celled capsule that dries and splits to release numerous small, black, angular seeds - the medicinal part.
botanical_description.leaves: Finely pinnately dissected, fern-like
botanical_description.flowers: Pale blue-white (occasionally pale purple), 5-10 petal-like sepals, feathery bract collar
botanical_description.fruit: Inflated capsule splitting to release small black angular seeds

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean basin, southern Europe, western Asia and North Africa, and widely cultivated as a spice and medicinal crop across the Middle East, South Asia and North Africa; grows in cultivated fields and on disturbed ground.

harvesting.text: Seed capsules are harvested once dried and brown (late summer), then threshed to release the small black seeds. Seed is stored whole and either used directly or cold-pressed for oil shortly before use to preserve the volatile thymoquinone-rich fraction.
harvesting.season: Late summer, once capsules have dried

traditional_uses.text: One of the most celebrated medicinal seeds in Middle Eastern and Islamic traditional medicine - known as 'Habbatul Barakah' (the blessed seed) - black seed has traditionally been used for digestive complaints, respiratory conditions (asthma, cough), pain, inflammation, skin conditions and as a general tonic. Modern research on its principal constituent thymoquinone supports broad anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antimicrobial, antidiabetic and immunomodulatory activity.

preparations.name: Whole or ground seed
preparations.text: Seed eaten directly, sprinkled on food, or ground into a paste - the traditional culinary-medicinal form.

preparations.name: Cold-pressed seed oil
preparations.text: The form most used in clinical trials, taken orally in capsules or as a liquid.

preparations.name: Infusion/decoction
preparations.text: Crushed seed steeped or simmered in hot water.

preparations.name: Topical oil
preparations.text: Seed oil applied to skin for irritation or infection.

dosage.name: Seed oil
dosage.text: Clinical trials have generally been well tolerated at doses up to about 200 mg/day of oil taken for up to 90 days. Educational reference only, not a prescription; avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy.

constituents.name: Thymoquinone
constituents.note: The principal bioactive constituent of the seed oil, responsible for much of its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Volatile oil (nigellone, thymol, carvacrol)
constituents.note: The aromatic fraction of the seed, contributing antimicrobial and antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Fixed oil (linoleic acid, oleic acid)
constituents.note: The bulk lipid fraction of the seed (roughly a third of seed weight by weight), the carrier for the fat-soluble thymoquinone.

constituents.name: Alkaloids (nigellicine, nigellidine)
constituents.note: Minor isoquinoline-type alkaloids studied for antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Saponins (alpha-hederin)
constituents.note: Triterpene saponin studied for anticancer and antiparasitic activity.

contraindications.note: Generally well-tolerated in clinical trials at doses up to 200 mg/day of oil for 90 days. Potential side effects may include mild gastrointestinal discomfort. Contraindicated during pregnancy due to potential uterine stimulant effects. May interact with immunosuppressants and antidiabetic medications. Allergic reactions possible in sensitive individuals. Consult healthcare provider before use, especially with concurrent medications.

Duke (2002) describes black cumin (Nigella sativa) as one of the most multi-active medicinal spices, with experimental evidence for bronchodilation, immunostimulation, hepatoprotection, and antihistaminic activity. The prophet Muhammad reportedly described it as 'a cure for all things except death.' Key bioactive compounds include thymoquinone and nigellone. Antihistaminic and bronchodilatory activity supports its traditional use in asthma and allergic conditions. Duke notes it is generally safe at culinary amounts but recommends caution with medicinal doses in pregnancy due to potential uterotonic effects (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Black seed (Nigella sativa) lowers fasting blood glucose and HbA1c in trials in people with prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (confirmed by meta-analysis); combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering, so monitor blood sugar.

Black seed (Nigella sativa) modestly lowers blood pressure - two meta-analyses of randomised trials found reductions of roughly 3 mmHg systolic and 3 mmHg diastolic. Combined with blood-pressure medicines it may add to their effect, so monitor your blood pressure.

constituents.name: Volatile oil rich in eugenol
constituents.note: The principal aromatic constituent, with anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Ursolic acid, rosmarinic acid, apigenin and luteolin
constituents.note: Antioxidant and adaptogenic constituents.

botanical_description.text: Aromatic annual or short-lived perennial subshrub (Lamiaceae), 30-75 cm tall, with erect, much-branched, hairy stems often tinged purple. Leaves are opposite, ovate-oblong with a toothed margin, and release a strong, clove-like (eugenol) scent when crushed. Small purplish-pink, two-lipped flowers are borne in elongated terminal racemes. The plant is held sacred in Hindu tradition (Tulsi).
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, ovate-oblong, toothed, clove-scented when crushed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, purplish-pink, two-lipped, in elongated terminal racemes
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)
botanical_description.flowering_period: Most of the year in tropical climates

habitat.text: Native to the Indian subcontinent and tropical Asia, now cultivated and naturalised throughout the tropics and subtropics worldwide; commonly grown in home gardens and temple courtyards as well as cultivated as a medicinal and aromatic crop.

harvesting.text: Leaves are harvested year-round from actively growing plants, ideally picked once morning dew has dried; whole flowering tops are also gathered.
harvesting.season: Year-round in warm climates

traditional_uses.text: Tulsi is one of the most revered plants in Ayurvedic medicine, traditionally used as an adaptogen for stress, a remedy for respiratory complaints (colds, cough, asthma), and a general immune tonic. Modern clinical trials support adaptogenic/anti-stress, blood-sugar-lowering and immunomodulatory effects consistent with these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf
preparations.text: A few fresh leaves chewed daily, or added to food - a traditional daily practice.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried or fresh leaf steeped in hot water, alone or blended with other herbs/spices.

preparations.name: Standardised extract capsule
preparations.text: Encapsulated dry-leaf extract, the form used in most clinical trials for stress, mood and sleep.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the leaf; used mainly topically or aromatically rather than taken internally at concentrated strength.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for stress and mood have used standardised dry-extract capsules taken over several weeks; dosing varies by product concentration, so follow the manufacturer's guidance. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests a small handful of fresh leaves, or about 1-2 g dried leaf, per cup, up to a few times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Adaptogen for stress and anxiety (improves stress scores and well-being in trials)
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Immunomodulatory / immune support
Cognitive support - a placebo-controlled study in healthy adults found improved reaction time and accuracy and shorter P300 latency

indications.note: Adaptogen for stress and anxiety (improves stress scores and well-being in trials)
indications.label: Anxiety

indications.note: Supports blood-sugar control (lowers fasting and post-meal glucose)
indications.label: Blood sugar / diabetes support

indications.note: Adaptogen for stress and anxiety (improves stress scores and well-being in trials)
indications.label: Stress

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; mild nausea has been reported. Its blood-sugar-lowering effect means caution alongside antidiabetic medicines.
Animal studies suggest mild anti-fertility and antiplatelet effects, so avoid medicinal doses when pregnant or trying to conceive, and before surgery.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Holy basil (tulsi) can lower fasting and after-meal blood glucose in people with type 2 diabetes; combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering, so monitor blood sugar.

constituents.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) and linoleic acid
constituents.note: Omega-6 essential fatty acids of the seed oil; GLA is the constituent the supplement is taken for.

constituents.name: Other seed-oil fatty acids and plant sterols
constituents.note: Minor lipid constituents.

botanical_description.text: Biennial herb (Onagraceae): a basal leaf rosette in the first year, followed in the second year by a stout, often reddish, erect flowering stem 30-150 cm tall. Leaves are alternate, lanceolate, with a prominent pale (often red-tinged) midrib and a wavy, untoothed margin. Large, fragrant, lemon-scented, four-petalled yellow flowers open in the evening and typically wilt by the following midday, followed by a narrow, cylindrical seed capsule.
botanical_description.habit: Biennial: first-year basal rosette, second-year erect flowering stem
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, lanceolate, wavy (untoothed) margin, prominent pale midrib
botanical_description.flowers: Large, fragrant, lemon-scented, yellow, four-petalled, opening in the evening
botanical_description.fruit: Narrow cylindrical seed capsule

habitat.text: Native to eastern North America, widely naturalised across Europe, Asia and elsewhere on disturbed, sunny ground - waste places, roadsides, dunes and railway banks - as a hardy coloniser of poor, sandy or gravelly soils.

harvesting.text: Mature seed capsules are harvested in late summer to autumn once dry and brown, and the tiny seeds are cold-pressed for their oil, the medicinal part. The first-year rosette leaves and young shoots have traditionally been used as food, but must never be gathered without certain identification given the deadly foxglove look-alike risk documented for this plant.
harvesting.season: Late summer to autumn

traditional_uses.text: Evening primrose has a Native American tradition as a food and poultice plant, but its modern use is almost entirely as a dietary source of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), an omega-6 fatty acid marketed for skin, hormonal and inflammatory complaints. Clinical trial evidence for these specific uses (eczema, PMS, menopause) is mixed to weak, and a Cochrane review found oral evening primrose oil is not effective for eczema.

preparations.name: Cold-pressed seed oil (capsules)
preparations.text: The standard commercial form, standardised for GLA content - the form used in most clinical trials.

preparations.name: Seed oil (liquid)
preparations.text: Taken directly by the spoonful or added to food.

dosage.name: Seed oil
dosage.text: Clinical trials evaluating evening primrose oil have most often used around 2-4 g of oil daily, taken for at least several weeks; evidence of benefit is inconsistent across most studied conditions. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Skin hydration and barrier function in healthy skin

indications.note: Eczema / atopic dermatitis - popularly used, but a Cochrane review found oral EPO is NOT effective
indications.label: Eczema

indications.note: Menopausal hot flushes - some trial benefit reported
indications.label: Hot flashes

indications.note: Premenstrual syndrome and cyclical breast pain - some positive but inconsistent trial results (a recent RCT found evening primrose oil reduced cyclical breast-pain severity versus placebo, with a combination of EPO and vitamin E most effective)
indications.label: Pain (general)

indications.note: Premenstrual syndrome and cyclical breast pain - some positive but inconsistent trial results (a recent RCT found evening primrose oil reduced cyclical breast-pain severity versus placebo, with a combination of EPO and vitamin E most effective)
indications.label: PMS / premenstrual syndrome

contraindications.note: Evidence is weak and largely negative: a Cochrane review concluded that oral evening primrose oil is not an effective treatment for eczema, and the broader trial literature is heterogeneous. Adverse effects are mild and mainly gastrointestinal.
May increase bleeding risk and has been reported to interact with warfarin; avoid concentrated/medicinal use in pregnancy unless advised by a clinician.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: First-year basal rosette leaves gathered as an edible/medicinal; deadly foxglove forms a very similar first-year rosette.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Evening primrose (Oenothera biennis) is a biennial whose first-year rosette leaves (and later shoots and flowers) are eaten and used. As Totally Wild UK warns, a beginner could confuse the first-year rosette with deadly foxglove (Digitalis purpurea), because they share a similar first-year 'tick list' - both form rosettes of soft, pointed, sometimes pink-tinged leaves. Foxglove is potentially fatal: its leaves contain cardiac glycosides that disturb the heart rhythm. Because the rosettes are alike before either plant flowers, this is a genuine and potentially deadly confusion.
distinguishing_features: Leaf hairiness and margin: foxglove leaves are softly hairy and much wider with a clearly serrated (toothed) margin and a net of sunken veins. Evening primrose leaves are narrower, far less hairy, with only a wavy (not toothed) margin.
Midrib: evening primrose has a distinctive prominent white/pale midrib, often red-tinged; foxglove has a greyer, downier leaf with net veins.

Best practice: do not eat any rosette leaf unless you have confirmed evening primrose (ideally seeing the plant's later stem or flowers); if the leaf is broad, soft-hairy and toothed, treat it as foxglove.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Compare the leaves. Narrow, nearly hairless leaves with a wavy margin and a bold white midrib = evening primrose. Broad, softly hairy leaves with a toothed (serrated) margin and net veins = foxglove - deadly, do not eat. If in any doubt, do not gather the rosette.

botanical_description.text: Evergreen tree (Oleaceae), typically 3-15 m tall, often gnarled and long-lived, with some specimens surviving for centuries. Leaves are narrow, leathery, lance-shaped, dark green above and silvery-grey beneath. Small, fragrant, creamy-white four-lobed flowers are borne in axillary clusters, followed by a fleshy drupe (the olive) that ripens from green to purple-black.
botanical_description.habit: Evergreen tree, often gnarled and long-lived
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, leathery, lance-shaped, dark green above, silvery-grey beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, fragrant, creamy-white, four-lobed, in axillary clusters
botanical_description.fruit: Fleshy drupe (the olive), ripening from green to purple-black

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean basin, the Near East and parts of Africa, and cultivated for millennia across Mediterranean-type climates worldwide (warm, dry summers and mild winters); tolerates poor, rocky, calcareous soils and drought.

harvesting.text: Leaves can be harvested year-round from pruned or fallen branches, generally at their highest oleuropein content in late autumn and winter; fruit is hand- or machine-harvested from early autumn (green olives) through winter (fully ripe black olives), depending on the intended product.
harvesting.season: Leaf year-round (peak late autumn/winter); fruit autumn to winter

traditional_uses.text: Olive leaf and fruit have a long Mediterranean tradition as a tonic, antipyretic and mild antihypertensive remedy, and olive oil is the foundation fat of the traditional Mediterranean diet, long associated with cardiovascular health. Modern research on oleuropein and other leaf polyphenols supports antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, blood-pressure-lowering and insulin-sensitising effects consistent with these traditional and dietary uses.

preparations.name: Leaf infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water, a traditional mild antihypertensive and tonic tea.

preparations.name: Standardised leaf extract
preparations.text: Capsule or liquid extract standardised for oleuropein content - the form used in most clinical trials.

preparations.name: Olive oil (culinary)
preparations.text: Extra-virgin, cold-pressed oil, the everyday dietary form and the basis of the Mediterranean diet.

preparations.name: Table olives (fruit)
preparations.text: Cured or fermented ripe or unripe fruit eaten as food.

dosage.name: Standardised leaf extract
dosage.text: A meta-analysis of trials for blood pressure used around 500 mg/day of standardised olive leaf extract. Educational reference only, not a prescription; culinary olive oil and leaf tea are considered food-safe at normal dietary amounts.

constituents.name: Oleuropein and hydroxytyrosol (secoiridoid phenols)
constituents.note: The principal bioactive phenolics of the leaf, responsible for most of its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and antihypertensive activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, rutin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoids of the leaf.

constituents.name: Triterpenes (oleanolic acid, maslinic acid)
constituents.note: Pentacyclic triterpenes of the leaf cuticle and fruit skin, studied for anti-inflammatory and metabolic effects.

constituents.name: Squalene and monounsaturated fatty acids (oleic acid)
constituents.note: The dominant fatty acid of the fruit oil, a key component of the Mediterranean-diet lipid profile.

contraindications.note: Olive leaf extract may lower blood pressure; use caution if already on antihypertensive medication. May have mild hypoglycaemic effects. Olive oil is safe for dietary use. Rare olive pollen allergy. Well tolerated in normal culinary and supplement doses.

Duke (2002) includes olive leaf as ++ and notes hypotensive, hypoglycemic, ACE inhibitor-like, and antioxidant activities at the experimental level (score 1). Olive leaf extract (containing oleuropein) has demonstrated blood pressure-lowering and antiarrhythmic effects consistent with clinical support (PHR). Duke notes that traditional Mediterranean use for both the leaf and fruit is food-grade and safe. Olive leaf preparations are used as mild antihypertensives in European phytotherapy. No significant adverse effects are reported at standard herbal doses (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Olive leaf improves insulin sensitivity and glucose handling; combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering, so monitor blood sugar.

Olive leaf can modestly lower blood pressure - a meta-analysis found about a 6 mmHg fall in systolic pressure at 500 mg/day, though the evidence base is small. Combined with blood-pressure medicines it may add to their effect, so monitor your blood pressure.

pairings.note: Olive leaf and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

botanical_description.text: A tough, deep-rooted perennial herb or low subshrub of the pea family, 30-80 cm tall, sometimes bearing soft spines. It has a very long, woody, tenacious taproot - so strong it was said to stop (arrest) the harrow, giving 'rest-harrow' its name. The stems are hairy and sticky, often reddish, and clothed in small trifoliolate (clover-like) leaves with toothed leaflets. The flowers are typical pea flowers, pink to purplish-pink (occasionally white), borne singly or in pairs in the leaf axils. Field restharrow (O. arvensis) is generally less spiny than its close relative spiny restharrow (O. spinosa).
botanical_description.habit: Deep-rooted perennial herb or low subshrub, sometimes softly spiny
botanical_description.leaves: Trifoliolate (clover-like), with finely toothed leaflets; stems hairy and glandular-sticky
botanical_description.flowers: Pea-type, pink to purplish-pink (rarely white), solitary or paired in the leaf axils
botanical_description.stem: Erect to ascending, hairy and sticky, often reddish; less spiny than O. spinosa
botanical_description.root: Very long, woody, tenacious taproot (the medicinal Ononidis radix)
botanical_description.fruit: A small, hairy, few-seeded pea pod

habitat.text: Rough grassland, field margins, roadsides, dunes and dry, sandy or calcareous waste ground across Europe, western Asia and parts of North Africa. As a deep-rooted, nitrogen-fixing legume it colonises poor, disturbed soils and was historically a troublesome weed of arable fields (hence 'restharrow').

harvesting.text: The root (Ononidis radix) is the principal medicinal part, dug in autumn from the second year onward and dried; the aerial flowering parts are also gathered in summer. The long woody taproot is cleaned and cut before drying for decoctions and infusions.
harvesting.season: Root in autumn; aerial parts in flower (summer)

traditional_uses.text: Restharrow root has a long place in European herbal medicine as a gentle aquaretic diuretic used for 'irrigation therapy' of the urinary tract - to increase urine flow in cystitis, urinary gravel and stones, and to flush the bladder. Field restharrow (Ononis arvensis) is used interchangeably with the officinal spiny restharrow (O. spinosa, Ononidis radix).
