The root and flowering herb have also been used as a mild anti-inflammatory, for skin complaints and eczema, for rheumatic conditions, and (as a decoction or gargle) for the mouth and throat. The isoflavone-rich root is a folk remedy in central and eastern European traditions.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: The dried root simmered in water and taken as an aquaretic diuretic for irrigation of the urinary tract; the classic restharrow preparation.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: The flowering aerial parts infused as a milder diuretic and anti-inflammatory tea.

preparations.name: Gargle / wash
preparations.text: A root decoction used traditionally as a mouth/throat gargle and as a wash for skin complaints.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the root (about 1:5).

dosage.name: Root decoction
dosage.text: Traditional herbal use: roughly 6-12 g of dried root daily as a decoction, taken with plenty of fluid for irrigation of the urinary tract. Educational reference only.

dosage.name: Tincture (1:5)
dosage.text: About 2-4 mL of a 1:5 root tincture up to three times daily in adults. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Isoflavonoids (formononetin, ononin and derivatives)
constituents.note: Formononetin and its 7-O-glucoside ononin, plus related isoflavones - the signature restharrow constituents (also phytoestrogens).

constituents.name: Pterocarpans and isoflavanones (medicarpin, maackiain, onogenin, sativanone)
constituents.note: Pterocarpans and isoflavanones and their glucosides, contributing to antimicrobial and wound-healing activity.

constituents.name: alpha-Onocerin
constituents.note: alpha-Onocerin (onocerin), a characteristic triterpene of the genus Ononis.

constituents.name: Coumarins (scopoletin, scopolin)
constituents.note: Coumarins detected in the phytochemical profile.

constituents.name: Hydroxycinnamic / phenolic acids
constituents.note: Caffeic, chlorogenic and p-coumaric acids (antioxidant).

constituents.name: Essential (volatile) oil
constituents.note: A volatile oil present in the root and aerial parts.

actions.note: Aquaretic diuretic; the classic irrigation-therapy action of restharrow root (Ononidis radix), used interchangeably for O. arvensis and O. spinosa.
Aqueous root extract reduced adhesion of uropathogenic E. coli to bladder cells (anti-adhesive).
Inhibits IL-8 release via TLR4/LPS and cytosolic phospholipase A2alpha (restharrow root).
Isoflavonoids from restharrow root promote wound healing in vivo.
Analgesic activity of Ononis extract in animals (congener O. spinosa).
Protective against ethanol-induced gastric mucosal injury (congener O. spinosa).
Ononis isoflavones showed chemopreventive/antiproliferative activity against breast cancer cells (preclinical).

indications.note: Aquaretic diuretic for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract.
indications.label: Urinary support

indications.note: Anti-adhesive activity against uropathogenic E. coli.
indications.label: Urinary tract infection (UTI)

indications.note: inferred from diuretic / urinary 'gravel' traditional use
indications.label: Kidney support

indications.note: Isoflavonoid wound-healing agents from restharrow root.
indications.label: Wounds

indications.note: inferred from anti-inflammatory and traditional skin/eczema use
indications.label: Skin irritation

contraindications.note: A safety signal exists in the genus: an Ononis spinosa root extract showed hepatotoxic effects at higher doses in animals (alongside analgesic activity). Avoid excessive or prolonged use, and use caution in liver disease.

Used as an aquaretic for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract, restharrow should be taken with a generous fluid intake; it should NOT be used for 'flushing' when there is oedema due to impaired heart or kidney function (standard irrigation-therapy caution).
The root is rich in isoflavone phytoestrogens (formononetin); avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy and breastfeeding and use caution in hormone-sensitive conditions.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Restharrow is an aquaretic diuretic; taken with pharmaceutical diuretics it may add to fluid and electrolyte loss.
The root is rich in the isoflavone phytoestrogen formononetin; it could theoretically interact with hormonal contraceptives, hormone replacement or hormone-sensitive conditions.

pairings.note: Classic urinary irrigation-therapy pairing: both are aquaretic diuretics used to increase urine flow in cystitis and urinary gravel.
Both are traditional diuretic 'flushing' herbs for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract.
Both are Fabaceae rich in the isoflavone phytoestrogen formononetin; combined use adds to phytoestrogen exposure - use caution in hormone-sensitive conditions.

botanical_description.text: Tender perennial subshrub (Lamiaceae), often grown as an annual in cold climates, 20-60 cm tall, with square, downy, much-branched stems. Leaves are opposite, small, grey-green and ovate. Tiny white to pale pink, two-lipped flowers are borne in distinctive knot-like clustered spikes, the origin of the common name 'knotted marjoram'.
botanical_description.habit: Bushy, downy perennial subshrub (often grown as an annual)
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, small, grey-green, ovate
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, white to pale pink, two-lipped, in knot-like clustered spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to Cyprus, Turkey and the eastern Mediterranean, and widely cultivated as a culinary and medicinal herb in gardens worldwide; prefers full sun and well-drained, often calcareous soils.

harvesting.text: The aerial flowering parts are cut just before or at flowering (summer), when essential-oil content is highest, and dried quickly in a warm, shaded, well-ventilated place.
harvesting.season: Summer, just before or at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Marjoram has a long Mediterranean culinary-medicinal tradition as a digestive carminative and mild sedative for indigestion, cramping and poor sleep, and as an antiseptic for minor wounds and infections. Modern research confirms antispasmodic, antimicrobial, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity from its essential oil and phenolic constituents.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried herb steeped in hot water as a digestive, calming tea.

preparations.name: Essential oil (topical)
preparations.text: Diluted essential oil applied for muscular aches or minor skin infections.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb.

preparations.name: Culinary use
preparations.text: Fresh or dried herb used as a seasoning, providing a mild, ongoing dietary digestive benefit.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Duke (2002) suggests about 1-2 g dried herb as tea, three times daily, or equivalent preparations. Educational reference only, not a prescription; avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy.

constituents.name: Essential oil (terpinen-4-ol, sabinene hydrate, thymol, carvacrol)
constituents.note: The volatile oil, dominated by terpinen-4-ol and cis-sabinene hydrate in true sweet marjoram, gives most of the antimicrobial and antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Rosmarinic acid and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Minor antioxidant constituents of the leaf.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Generally very safe as a culinary herb. Essential oil should not be ingested in large amounts and avoided in pregnancy. Theoretically may interact with anticoagulants. Avoid medicinal doses during pregnancy.

Duke (2002) rates marjoram (Origanum majorana) as +++ and notes antiviral, antispasmodic, COX-2 inhibitory, and antiseptic activities at the experimental level (score 1). It demonstrates antiherpetic activity in vitro. The plant contains rosmarinic acid and thymol, which contribute to antioxidant and antimicrobial effects. Dose: 1–2 g dried herb as tea three times daily or equivalent preparations. Duke notes the plant is generally well-tolerated with no significant contraindications at usual herbal doses (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Marjoram is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

constituents.name: Essential oil rich in carvacrol and thymol
constituents.note: Phenolic monoterpenes responsible for the strong antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids (rosmarinic acid) and flavonoids
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb/subshrub (Lamiaceae), 20-80 cm tall, with square, often reddish, downy stems. Leaves are opposite, ovate and aromatic. Small purplish-pink (occasionally white) flowers are borne in dense, flattened, corymb-like clusters at the branch tips.
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, ovate, aromatic
botanical_description.flowers: Small, purplish-pink (occasionally white), in dense corymb-like terminal clusters
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean, Europe and temperate Asia and widely naturalised elsewhere, growing on dry, sunny grassland, banks and scrubby hillsides, typically on calcareous soils.

harvesting.text: Aerial parts are cut at or just before flowering (mid- to late summer), when essential-oil (carvacrol/thymol) content peaks, then dried quickly in the shade.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer, at or just before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Oregano has a long Mediterranean culinary-medicinal tradition as an aromatic digestive bitter and antiseptic remedy for coughs, colds and infections. Modern research on its carvacrol- and thymol-rich essential oil strongly supports broad antibacterial, antifungal and antioxidant activity, consistent with these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried herb steeped in hot water as a digestive, aromatic tea.

preparations.name: Essential oil (diluted, topical/aromatic)
preparations.text: The most-studied form; concentrated oil is a strong irritant and must be well diluted before topical use and never taken undiluted internally.

preparations.name: Emulsified oil (oral, supervised)
preparations.text: A small clinical study used an emulsified oregano oil preparation to clear enteric parasites and improve gut symptoms; oral use of concentrated oil should be supervised.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried herb.

dosage.name: Culinary/infusion use
dosage.text: Culinary amounts (as a food seasoning or mild tea) are considered safe; concentrated essential oil should only be used well diluted and is not intended for undiluted internal use without professional guidance. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory

Antimicrobial (antibacterial and antifungal via carvacrol and thymol) - supports respiratory and gastrointestinal infections; a small clinical study found emulsified oregano oil cleared enteric parasites (incl. Blastocystis hominis) and improved gut symptoms

Antimicrobial (antibacterial and antifungal via carvacrol and thymol) - supports respiratory and gastrointestinal infections; a small clinical study found emulsified oregano oil cleared enteric parasites (incl. Blastocystis hominis) and improved gut symptoms
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Carminative digestive for indigestion (traditional bitter aromatic)
Carminative digestive for indigestion (traditional bitter aromatic)

indications.note: Aromatic support for coughs and colds (traditional)
indications.label: Cold & flu

indications.note: Aromatic support for coughs and colds (traditional)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Carminative digestive for indigestion (traditional bitter aromatic)
indications.label: Indigestion

indications.note: Antimicrobial (antibacterial and antifungal via carvacrol and thymol) - supports respiratory and gastrointestinal infections; a small clinical study found emulsified oregano oil cleared enteric parasites (incl. Blastocystis hominis) and improved gut symptoms
indications.label: Infection (general)

contraindications.note: Culinary amounts are safe. Concentrated oregano essential oil is a strong irritant - dilute well before any topical use and do not take undiluted oil internally.

May lower blood sugar and has a mild antiplatelet effect, so use caution with antidiabetic or anticoagulant medicines; avoid medicinal/oil doses in pregnancy. Allergy is possible in people sensitive to the mint family (Lamiaceae).

botanical_description.text: A small, sour-tasting herbaceous annual to short-lived perennial of the woodsorrel family, 10-40 cm tall. Its bright green leaves are clover-like, palmately divided into three heart-shaped (notched) leaflets that fold down at night and in strong sun. The small five-petalled flowers are bright yellow, borne in little clusters on axillary stalks. The whole plant tastes sharply sour from its oxalic-acid content. It is very similar to the creeping yellow woodsorrel (Oxalis corniculata) but has more upright stems that do not root at the nodes, and generally green (not purplish) foliage.
botanical_description.habit: Upright to ascending annual or short-lived perennial; stems not rooting at the nodes (unlike creeping O. corniculata)
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, long-stalked, trifoliolate (clover-like) with three heart-shaped leaflets that fold at night; sour to taste
botanical_description.flowers: Small, 5-petalled, bright yellow, in axillary clusters
botanical_description.stem: Slender, erect to ascending, branched, often finely hairy, green
botanical_description.root: Slender fibrous roots, sometimes with thin rhizomes
botanical_description.fruit: An erect, cylindrical, five-angled capsule that splits explosively to scatter the seeds

habitat.text: A common weed of disturbed, open and cultivated ground: gardens, lawns, arable fields, roadsides, footpaths, waste places and greenhouses. It favours moist, fertile, well-lit soils and is excluded by dense established vegetation. Native to North America but now naturalised almost worldwide across temperate and warm regions.

harvesting.text: The sour leaves and aerial parts are gathered when actively growing and flowering, used fresh or dried. Because the plant accumulates oxalic acid, harvesting for food or medicine should favour young growth in modest quantities. As a low weed it is easily hand-gathered.
harvesting.season: Spring to autumn during active growth and flowering

traditional_uses.text: Yellow woodsorrel (Oxalis stricta and its near-identical relative O. corniculata) has a long folk-medicine history, deepest in South and East Asian traditions. The cooling, sour whole plant has been used as a refrigerant and thirst-quencher, as a sour leafy vegetable/condiment, and as a source of vitamin C against scurvy. Traditional medicinal uses (chiefly documented for O. corniculata) include dysentery and diarrhoea and other digestive complaints, fever, liver complaints, and topical application of the crushed leaves or juice to wounds, insect bites, warts, corns, mouth sores and inflamed skin, as well as use as a mild diuretic. O. stricta shares these uses where the two woodsorrels are not distinguished.

preparations.name: Fresh juice / poultice
preparations.text: Crushed fresh leaves or expressed juice applied topically to wounds, insect bites, warts and inflamed skin, or taken in small amounts for its cooling sour effect.

preparations.name: Infusion / decoction
preparations.text: A whole-plant or leaf infusion/decoction used traditionally for fevers, diarrhoea and as a mild diuretic.

preparations.name: Sour culinary green
preparations.text: Young leaves eaten raw in small quantities as a tart salad green or to add sourness to food (edible 'sourgrass').

dosage.name: Fresh herb (culinary/traditional)
dosage.text: No standardised medicinal dose is established. Traditionally used as small quantities of the fresh sour herb or as leaf infusions. Because of the high oxalate content, keep amounts modest. Educational reference only.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.note: Flavonoid glycosides (e.g. isovitexin, vitexin, quercetin and apigenin derivatives), the main antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids
constituents.note: Caffeic, ferulic and related phenolic acids (antioxidant).

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Tannins contributing to astringent/antidiarrhoeal activity.

constituents.name: Oxalic acid and soluble oxalates
constituents.note: The defining sour constituent - oxalic acid with potassium and calcium oxalates - biosynthesised in part from vitamin C. Responsible for the sour taste and for the kidney-stone/oxalate caution.

constituents.name: Vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid)
constituents.note: High ascorbate content (basis of the traditional antiscorbutic use); also a metabolic precursor of the plant's oxalic acid.

constituents.name: Acidic polysaccharide
constituents.note: An acidic polysaccharide with antioxidant activity.

actions.note: Antibacterial and antiparasitic (amoebicidal/giardicidal) activity of woodsorrel extracts/constituents.
Protective against paracetamol-induced liver injury (congener O. corniculata).
Protective against experimental gastric ulceration (congener O. corniculata).
Woodsorrel phytoconstituents (and a green-synthesised nanoparticle made with O. stricta extract) induced apoptosis in breast, colon and Hep2 cancer cells (preclinical).
Alpha-glucosidase and alpha-amylase inhibition by woodsorrel extract (congener O. corniculata).
Anti-Alzheimer's activity of woodsorrel extract in rats (congener O. corniculata).
Promotes wound and fracture healing; traditional topical use for wounds and skin (congener O. corniculata).
Sour, tannin-containing herb; the basis of traditional antidiarrhoeal use.
Traditional astringent remedy for diarrhoea and dysentery (congener O. corniculata).

indications.note: Traditional astringent remedy for diarrhoea and dysentery.
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Promotes wound/fracture healing; traditional topical use.
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Yellow woodsorrel is RICH IN OXALIC ACID and soluble oxalates (the source of its sour taste). Avoid large or frequent quantities, and especially avoid it if you are prone to calcium-oxalate kidney stones, have gout, or have kidney disease. Oxalates also bind calcium and iron, reducing their absorption. Use only modest amounts, and cooking reduces soluble oxalate.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding has not been established; avoid medicinal doses during pregnancy and lactation.

Most modern pharmacological data come from the near-identical congener Oxalis corniculata; the two yellow woodsorrels are used interchangeably in folk medicine, but human clinical trials are lacking for either species.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Woodsorrel extract inhibits carbohydrate-digesting enzymes and shows antidiabetic activity; combined with antidiabetic medicines it could theoretically add to blood-sugar-lowering effects.

botanical_description.text: Low, creeping, evergreen dwarf shrub (Ericaceae) with slender, wiry stems that root at the nodes. Leaves are tiny, alternate, leathery and ovate with in-rolled margins, dark green above and whitish beneath. Flowers are small, pink and four-petalled, sharply reflexed backward so that the protruding stamens and style resemble a crane's head and neck - the origin of the name 'craneberry', later shortened to 'cranberry'. The fruit is a small, round, tart red berry.
botanical_description.height: Prostrate, mat-forming (stems trailing, rarely more than 10-20 cm above the moss)
botanical_description.leaves: Tiny, alternate, leathery, ovate, in-rolled margins, whitish beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, pink, four-petalled, sharply reflexed (crane's-head shape)
botanical_description.stem: Slender, wiry, trailing, rooting at the nodes
botanical_description.root: Shallow fibrous roots adapted to waterlogged, acidic peat
botanical_description.fruit: Small, round, tart red berry

habitat.text: Native to boggy, acidic wetlands - sphagnum bogs and marshes - across northern and central Europe and northern Asia, requiring waterlogged, nutrient-poor, acidic peat soils.

harvesting.text: Berries are hand-picked or wet-harvested (traditionally by hand-raking the bog) in autumn once fully ripe and deep red.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Cranberry has a long northern-European and Native American tradition as a food and folk remedy for urinary complaints, valued for its tart, astringent, vitamin-C-rich fruit. Modern research on its proanthocyanidins supports a role in reducing bacterial adhesion in the urinary tract, consistent with the traditional use for cystitis and recurrent urinary tract infections.

preparations.name: Fresh or dried berries
preparations.text: Eaten whole, fresh or dried, or added to food.

preparations.name: Juice
preparations.text: Unsweetened juice, the traditional preparation used for urinary support.

preparations.name: Standardised extract capsule
preparations.text: Proanthocyanidin-standardised extract, a concentrated form used in clinical trials for UTI prevention.

dosage.name: Juice / extract
dosage.text: Food amounts (juice, sauces, dried fruit) are considered safe; standardised extract products vary in proanthocyanidin content, so follow product-specific dosing for urinary support. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Proanthocyanidins (A-type PACs)
constituents.note: The A-type proanthocyanidins are considered responsible for inhibiting bacterial adhesion to the urinary tract lining.

constituents.name: Anthocyanins
constituents.note: Red pigments and antioxidants of the fruit.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, myricetin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant flavonoids of the fruit.

constituents.name: Triterpenoids and phytosterols
constituents.note: Minor lipophilic constituents of the fruit skin.

constituents.name: Organic acids (quinic, citric, malic acid)
constituents.note: Give the fruit its characteristic tartness and are traditionally credited with a mild urinary-acidifying effect.

contraindications.note: Food amounts are generally considered safe (berries in meals, drinks, sauces). (Williams et al., 2023).

Duke (2002) includes cranberry primarily at the experimental level for its astringent, diuretic, and antioxidant properties. Commission E has not approved cranberry for specific indications, though there is evidence supporting its role in preventing urinary tract infections (UTIs) by inhibiting bacterial adhesion to uroepithelial cells (proanthocyanidins). Duke notes the high vitamin C and organic acid content. Food-grade consumption is considered safe and beneficial as a urinary acidifier (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Slow-growing perennial herb (Araliaceae), 30-60 cm tall, harvested at 4 years of age or older. A single erect stem bears a whorl of palmately compound leaves, each with 3-5 toothed leaflets, at its top. Small greenish-white flowers are borne in a single terminal umbel, followed by small red berries. The fleshy, often forked or human-shaped taproot - the origin of the name 'ginseng' ('renshen', man-root) - is the medicinal part.
botanical_description.habit: Slow-growing perennial herb, single erect stem
botanical_description.leaves: Palmately compound, whorled at the stem top, 3-5 toothed leaflets per leaf
botanical_description.flowers: Small, greenish-white, in a single terminal umbel
botanical_description.root: Fleshy, often forked, human-shaped taproot - the medicinal part
botanical_description.fruit: Small red berries

habitat.text: Native to the cool-temperate deciduous mountain forests of Manchuria, Korea and the Russian Far East. Historically wild-harvested, it is now predominantly cultivated, mainly in Korea and China, under artificial shade for several years before harvest.

harvesting.text: The root is harvested after at least 4-6 years of growth, once its ginsenoside content is fully developed. Roots are either air-dried as 'white ginseng' or steamed and then dried as 'red ginseng', a processing step that alters the ginsenoside profile.
harvesting.season: After 4-6+ years of cultivation

traditional_uses.text: Ginseng is one of the most celebrated tonic herbs of East Asian traditional medicine, used for millennia as a general adaptogen to restore vitality, support recovery from illness and improve stamina and cognitive performance. Modern clinical research supports adaptogenic, immunomodulatory, antioxidant, neuroprotective and mild antidiabetic effects consistent with this traditional use.

preparations.name: Standardised root extract (capsule)
preparations.text: Commission E-recognised standardised extract (4-7% ginsenosides), the most clinically studied preparation.

preparations.name: Dried root (sliced/whole)
preparations.text: Sliced or whole dried root, chewed or decocted.

preparations.name: Red ginseng (steamed and dried root)
preparations.text: Traditional processed form (steamed then dried), which alters and concentrates the ginsenoside profile compared with white (unprocessed) ginseng.

preparations.name: Tea/decoction
preparations.text: Sliced root simmered in water.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Duke (2002) and Commission E guidance: 200-600 mg of standardised extract (4-7% ginsenosides) daily, used cyclically (2-3 weeks on, 2 weeks off) rather than continuously. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Ginsenosides (panaxosides)
constituents.note: The principal bioactive triterpene saponins of the root, considered responsible for most of ginseng's adaptogenic, neuroprotective and immunomodulatory activity; the ratio of protopanaxadiol- to protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides differs between fresh, white and red ginseng.

constituents.name: Polysaccharides (panaxans)
constituents.note: Contribute to the immunomodulatory activity of the root.

constituents.name: Volatile oil and phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Minor constituents contributing to the aroma and antioxidant activity.

actions.note: Physical performance / strength improvement

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated in short-term use (up to 3 months). May cause insomnia, headache, or gastrointestinal upset at high doses. Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding. May interact with warfarin, MAOIs, and diabetic medications. Not recommended for continuous use without breaks.

Duke (2002) rates Korean/Oriental ginseng (Panax ginseng) as highly active with clinical support for adaptogenic, immunostimulant, and performance-enhancing effects. Commission E approves standardized ginseng root extract (4–7% ginsenosides) as a tonic for fatigue and during convalescence. Dose: 200–600 mg standardized extract daily. Duke cautions about 'Ginseng Abuse Syndrome' (GAS) with overdose — symptoms include hypertension, insomnia, edema, and nervousness. Drug interactions are notable: ginseng potentiates MAOIs and may interact with warfarin, digoxin, and stimulants. Cycle use (2–3 weeks on, 2 weeks off) is recommended (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Ginseng may reduce the anticoagulant effect of warfarin (lower INR); a randomized trial of American ginseng showed a reduced INR. Monitor anticoagulation closely.
Case reports link ginseng with MAOI antidepressants (e.g. phenelzine) to headache, tremor and manic-like symptoms.

Ginseng can lower blood glucose - a controlled trial in type 2 diabetes reduced fasting glucose and HbA1c, and ginseng also lowers post-meal glucose. Combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering and risk hypoglycaemia, so monitor blood sugar.

pairings.note: Ginseng and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginseng and ginger both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin, chrysin)
constituents.note: The principal calming constituents, thought to act on GABA pathways.

constituents.name: Trace harman-type alkaloids
constituents.note: Minor constituents that may contribute to the sedative effect.

botanical_description.text: Fast-growing, climbing or trailing perennial vine (Passifloraceae) that climbs by axillary tendrils, reaching several metres. Leaves are alternate and deeply 3-lobed. The large, intricate flowers have a fringed corona of lavender-white filaments around a central structure whose parts were likened by early missionaries to instruments of the Passion of Christ, giving the plant its name. The fruit is an edible, egg-shaped, yellow-orange berry known as a 'maypop'.
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, deeply 3-lobed
botanical_description.flowers: Large, intricate, lavender-white with a fringed corona
botanical_description.fruit: Edible, egg-shaped, yellow-orange berry ('maypop')

habitat.text: Native to the south-eastern United States, growing in thickets, fencerows, roadsides and disturbed sunny ground; widely cultivated and naturalised as an ornamental and medicinal crop elsewhere in warm-temperate and subtropical regions.

harvesting.text: The flowering and fruiting aerial parts (herb) are cut during the growing season, typically as flowering begins, and dried for use.
harvesting.season: Growing season, at the start of flowering

traditional_uses.text: Passionflower has a traditional use, recognised by European regulatory herbal monographs, as a mild sedative and anxiolytic for nervous tension, mild anxiety and difficulty sleeping. Modern clinical trials support anxiolytic and sleep-supporting effects, including reduced preoperative anxiety without sedation or psychomotor impairment.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried herb steeped in hot water as a calming, sleep-supporting tea.

preparations.name: Standardised extract/tincture
preparations.text: Liquid or encapsulated extract, the form used in most clinical trials.

preparations.name: Tea blend
preparations.text: Traditionally combined with other calming herbs such as chamomile or lemon balm.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: EMA herbal-monograph guidance suggests around 0.5-2 g dried herb per cup as an infusion, up to several times daily, or an equivalent liquid/dry extract. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anxiolytic for anxiety and nervous tension (reduces anxiety in clinical trials)
Sleep support for insomnia - improves subjective sleep quality and, on polysomnography, increases total sleep time

indications.note: Anxiolytic for anxiety and nervous tension (reduces anxiety in clinical trials); Reduces preoperative / situational anxiety without sedation or psychomotor impairment (RCT in ambulatory surgery patients)
indications.label: Anxiety

indications.note: Sleep support for insomnia - improves subjective sleep quality and, on polysomnography, increases total sleep time
indications.label: Insomnia / sleeplessness

indications.note: Anxiolytic for anxiety and nervous tension (reduces anxiety in clinical trials)
indications.label: Nervous tension

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated, with no serious adverse effects reported in trials. Its sedative effect can add to that of alcohol, sedatives and sleep medicines, so avoid combining and do not use before driving.
Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Passionflower has calming, sedative effects (a systematic review of clinical trials supports an anxiety-reducing effect); taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Woody climbing shrub or liana (Sapindaceae) in its native forest habitat, though often cultivated and pruned as a shrub. Leaves are alternate and pinnately compound with 5 leaflets. Small greenish-white flowers are borne in axillary panicles, followed by a red-orange, three-lobed capsule that splits open to reveal black seeds partly covered by a white aril - a fruit likened in Indigenous Amazonian folklore to a staring eye.
botanical_description.height: Climbing shrub/liana (cultivated as a shrub of a few metres)
botanical_description.leaves: Alternate, pinnately compound with 5 leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Small, greenish-white, in axillary panicles
botanical_description.fruit: Red-orange, three-lobed capsule splitting to reveal black, white-arilled seeds

habitat.text: Native to the Amazon basin, particularly Brazil, where it has long been cultivated by Indigenous peoples (notably the Sateré-Mawé); grows in tropical, humid lowland forest conditions.

harvesting.text: Ripe fruit capsules are hand-harvested as they split open; seeds are removed, roasted, and traditionally ground into a paste with water and shaped into sticks for storage, or dried and powdered.
harvesting.season: As fruit capsules ripen and split

traditional_uses.text: Guarana seed has a long Amazonian Indigenous tradition as a stimulant tonic for fatigue and stamina and as a ceremonial and social beverage. Its very high caffeine content (greater by weight than coffee beans) underlies its modern use for cognitive performance, physical endurance and metabolic support, supported by clinical trials.

preparations.name: Powdered seed/paste
preparations.text: Traditional roasted, ground seed, taken in water or food - the classic Amazonian Indigenous preparation.

preparations.name: Standardised extract capsule
preparations.text: The form used in most clinical trials for cognition, fatigue and physical performance.

preparations.name: Guarana beverage/soft drink
preparations.text: Seed extract used as a flavouring and stimulant ingredient in commercial beverages.

dosage.name: Extract/powder
dosage.text: Guarana products vary widely in caffeine content (the seed is naturally 2-4 times more caffeine-dense than coffee by weight); follow product-specific dosing and apply the same cautions as for a strong caffeine source. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Caffeine (guaranine)
constituents.note: The dominant bioactive constituent, present at 2-4 times the concentration found in coffee beans by weight; responsible for guarana's stimulant, ergogenic and thermogenic effects.

constituents.name: Tannins (catechins, epicatechins)
constituents.note: Polyphenols thought to slow caffeine absorption and to contribute antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Saponins
constituents.note: Minor constituents of the seed.

constituents.name: Theobromine and theophylline (minor methylxanthines)
constituents.note: Minor stimulant alkaloids alongside caffeine.

actions.note: Physical performance / strength improvement

contraindications.note: Guarana contains very high levels of caffeine (2–4 times that of coffee by weight). All the cautions of caffeine apply — avoid in cardiac arrhythmia, hypertension, anxiety disorders, insomnia, and during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Should not be combined with other stimulants, MAOIs, or medications affected by caffeine. Risk of dependence and withdrawal symptoms with prolonged high-dose use.

Duke (2002) rates guarana as ++ and notes CNS-stimulant, anti-aggregant, diuretic, and thermogenic activities, primarily due to its high caffeine content (3–5%). It has positive chronotropic and inotropic effects on the heart. Duke flags guarana as genotoxic and mutagenic at the experimental level (score 1), which warrants caution with high-dose supplementation. Contraindicated in cardiovascular disease, anxiety disorders, peptic ulcers, and during pregnancy. Interactions include potentiation of other stimulants and anticoagulants (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Highly oxygenated coumarins (umckalin)
constituents.note: Characteristic root constituents with antibacterial activity.

constituents.name: Polyphenols and proanthocyanidins (gallic acid derivatives)
constituents.note: Contribute to the antiviral and immunomodulatory effects.

botanical_description.text: Low-growing herbaceous perennial (Geraniaceae) with a thick, dark, tuberous rootstock. Long-stalked, heart- to kidney-shaped, greyish-green, softly hairy leaves form a basal rosette. Slender flowering stems bear small, dark red to almost black, five-petalled flowers. The thick tuberous root is the medicinal part, used as the standardised extract EPs 7630.
botanical_description.leaves: Long-stalked, heart- to kidney-shaped, greyish-green, softly hairy
botanical_description.flowers: Small, dark red to almost black, five-petalled
botanical_description.stem: Slender flowering stems above a basal leaf rosette
botanical_description.root: Thick, dark, tuberous rootstock - the medicinal part
botanical_description.fruit: Small beaked capsule (typical Geraniaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native to the grasslands of the Eastern Cape and Free State regions of South Africa and Lesotho, growing on rocky, well-drained grassland slopes at moderate to high altitude.

harvesting.text: The thick tuberous root is dug from mature plants - wild-harvested and increasingly cultivated - then cleaned and dried before being processed into the standardised ethanolic extract (EPs 7630) used commercially.
harvesting.season: Harvested from mature plants; specific season not standardised in the bucket

traditional_uses.text: Known locally as 'Umckaloabo', Pelargonium sidoides root has a long history of traditional use by South African indigenous peoples for respiratory and gastrointestinal complaints, and was popularised in Europe in the early 20th century as a tuberculosis remedy. Its modern use, now well supported by clinical trials, is as a standardised extract (EPs 7630) for acute bronchitis, the common cold and other upper respiratory tract infections.

preparations.name: Standardised liquid extract (EPs 7630)
preparations.text: The clinically studied commercial form, taken as drops or syrup for acute bronchitis and upper respiratory tract infections.

preparations.name: Tablets/lozenges
preparations.text: Encapsulated standardised extract.

dosage.name: Standardised extract (EPs 7630)
dosage.text: Clinical trials have used the standardised commercial extract per product labelling for acute bronchitis and respiratory infections in both adults and children; dosing (drops or syrup) differs by age, so follow product-specific instructions. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antiviral and immunomodulatory (mechanism of action)
Antiviral and immunomodulatory (mechanism of action)

indications.note: Acute bronchitis - reduces symptom severity and shortens illness
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Common cold and acute respiratory tract infection
indications.label: Cold & flu

indications.note: Common cold and acute respiratory tract infection
indications.label: Infection (general)

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; the main effects are mild gastrointestinal upset or, rarely, an allergic skin reaction. Rare reports of liver injury mean you should stop and seek advice if signs of liver problems occur.
The coumarin content gives a theoretical bleeding risk, so use caution with anticoagulant medicines; use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

constituents.name: Sesquiterpenes (petasin and isopetasin)
constituents.note: The anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic active principles; standardised in commercial extracts.

constituents.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs)
constituents.note: Naturally present but hepatotoxic and carcinogenic; must be removed to make a safe product.

botanical_description.text: Robust, rhizomatous perennial herb (Asteraceae) that flowers before the leaves emerge: thick, pinkish, scaly flower spikes push up from the ground in early spring, followed by enormous rounded to heart-shaped leaves - up to about 1 m across at maturity - on stout stalks, giving rise to the common name 'umbrella plant'.
botanical_description.height: Flower spikes low in early spring; leaves later up to ~1 m across
botanical_description.leaves: Enormous, rounded to heart-shaped, up to ~1 m across, on stout stalks
botanical_description.flowers: Thick, pinkish, scaly flower spikes, appearing before the leaves
botanical_description.stem: Thick underground rhizome; scaly flowering stalks in early spring
botanical_description.fruit: Small ribbed achenes with a pappus (typical Asteraceae)

habitat.text: Native to damp, marshy ground across Europe and western Asia - riverbanks, ditches, wet meadows and damp woodland margins - favouring moist, nutrient-rich soils.

harvesting.text: Rhizome/root is dug in autumn or early spring; leaves are gathered during the growing season. Raw material naturally contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids, so commercial products are manufactured to remove them (PA-free standardised extracts); home-gathered material should never be used medicinally.
harvesting.season: Root/rhizome in autumn or early spring; leaf during the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Butterbur has a long European folk-medicine history as a fever remedy - the name 'pestilence wort' reflects its historic use during plague outbreaks - and for asthma, cough and migraine-type headache. Modern clinical trials of standardised, pyrrolizidine-alkaloid-free extracts support its use for migraine prevention and allergic rhinitis.

preparations.name: Standardised PA-free extract (tablet)
preparations.text: The only recommended form - e.g. the CO2-extracted root product studied for migraine prevention, or the Ze 339 leaf extract studied for allergic rhinitis. Raw root or leaf tea should never be used, as it retains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids.

dosage.name: Standardised PA-free extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials have used standardised PA-free extracts (for example around 50-75 mg twice daily of a CO2 root extract for migraine prevention). Always use a certified PA-free commercial product, never raw plant material. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Relief of allergic rhinitis / hay fever (antihistaminic and leukotriene-inhibiting; systematic review found it superior to placebo and comparable to non-sedating antihistamines for intermittent allergic rhinitis)
Antispasmodic support in bronchial asthma
Antispasmodic support in bronchial asthma

indications.note: Relief of allergic rhinitis / hay fever (antihistaminic and leukotriene-inhibiting; systematic review found it superior to placebo and comparable to non-sedating antihistamines for intermittent allergic rhinitis)
indications.label: Allergy / hay fever

indications.note: Relief of allergic rhinitis / hay fever (antihistaminic and leukotriene-inhibiting; systematic review found it superior to placebo and comparable to non-sedating antihistamines for intermittent allergic rhinitis)
indications.label: Fever

indications.note: Migraine prophylaxis (reduces attack frequency; up to ~60% reduction vs placebo in a randomised controlled trial of a standardised CO2 root extract)
indications.label: Migraine

contraindications.note: Use ONLY certified pyrrolizidine-alkaloid (PA)-free, standardised extracts. Raw or unprocessed butterbur contains PAs that are toxic to the liver and potentially carcinogenic.

Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding. As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae), it can cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to ragweed and related plants. Watch for and report any signs of liver problems.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Very large rounded leaves and rhizome gathered for use; alpendost has similar large rounded leaves and the same hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Butterbur (Petasites hybridus) has very large rounded leaves and is used medicinally (as pyrrolizidine-free standardised extracts). In the wild its foliage is confused with alpendost (Adenostyles alliariae) and coltsfoot, all large-leaved damp-ground plants. Alpendost contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids and, gathered in mistake for coltsfoot/butterbur and brewed as tea, has caused liver veno-occlusive disease (Sperl et al., 1995). Butterbur's own raw leaves also contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids, so wild butterbur should only be used as tested, PA-free products rather than home-gathered material.
distinguishing_features: Leaf: butterbur has enormous rounded leaves (up to about 1 m across when mature) on stout stalks; alpendost leaves are large but smaller and more coarsely toothed. Neither should be brewed from home-gathered material.
Flowers: butterbur pushes up thick pinkish flower spikes in early spring before the leaves; alpendost has fluffy whitish-pink flower clusters among its leaves in summer.
Best practice: do not brew wild butterbur or its large-leaved lookalikes at all - use only standardised, pyrrolizidine-free commercial butterbur preparations.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Do not gather large rounded 'butterbur' leaves for tea - butterbur, alpendost and coltsfoot all look alike and carry liver-damaging pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Use only standardised, pyrrolizidine-free commercial butterbur products rather than home-collected leaves.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (apigenin) and other phenolics
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

constituents.name: Essential oil (apiole and myristicin)
constituents.note: Aromatic constituents that drive the diuretic action but are toxic in large amounts.

constituents.name: Furanocoumarins (bergapten, imperatorin)
constituents.note: Photosensitising constituents; the basis for the mild phototoxicity caution.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and carotenoids
constituents.note: Nutritive antioxidant constituents.

botanical_description.text: Biennial herb (Apiaceae), usually grown as an annual, 20-60 cm tall, with a slender taproot. Leaves are bright green and finely divided, either flat or tightly curled ('crisped') depending on variety. In the second year, small yellow-green flowers are borne in compound umbels, followed by small, ridged fruit (the 'seed').
botanical_description.habit: Biennial herb (usually cultivated as an annual)
botanical_description.leaves: Bright green, finely divided, flat or curled ('crisped')
botanical_description.flowers: Small, yellow-green, in compound umbels (second year)
botanical_description.fruit: Small, ridged, aromatic fruit ("seed")

habitat.text: Native to the central Mediterranean (Sardinia and southern Italy), long cultivated worldwide as a culinary and medicinal herb in gardens and as a field crop.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked fresh throughout the growing season; the root (of root-parsley varieties) is lifted in autumn; the seed (fruit) is collected from second-year plants once ripe and dry - it is used mainly in medicinal/aromatic preparations rather than food, given its higher concentration of the toxic essential-oil constituents apiole and myristicin.
harvesting.season: Leaf throughout the growing season; root in autumn; seed once ripe (second year)

traditional_uses.text: Parsley has a long Mediterranean culinary-medicinal tradition as a carminative digestive tonic, breath freshener and mild diuretic traditionally used for urinary complaints and fluid retention. Modern research supports antioxidant, hepatoprotective, nephroprotective and diuretic activity.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf
preparations.text: Eaten fresh as a garnish or herb - a traditional daily digestive and breath-freshening support.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf or crushed seed steeped in hot water, a traditional diuretic and digestive preparation.

preparations.name: Root decoction
preparations.text: Root simmered in water, a traditional diuretic preparation.

dosage.name: Leaf infusion
dosage.text: Culinary amounts of leaf are food-safe; medicinal use should stick to modest infusion amounts (roughly 2-4 g dried leaf or root per cup, a few times daily). Avoid concentrated seed extract or essential oil, which are toxic and uterine-stimulant at higher doses, and avoid medicinal doses entirely in pregnancy. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antioxidant, nephroprotective and hepatoprotective
Carminative digestive and stomach tonic (bloating, indigestion)
Carminative digestive and stomach tonic (bloating, indigestion)

Diuretic - reduces fluid retention/swelling and supports urinary and kidney function (traditionally used for urinary stones); animal studies attribute the effect to inhibition of the renal Na+/K+ pump, raising urine flow
Antioxidant, nephroprotective and hepatoprotective
Antioxidant, nephroprotective and hepatoprotective

indications.note: Breath freshener (chlorophyll-rich; traditional for bad breath)
indications.label: Bad breath

indications.note: Carminative digestive and stomach tonic (bloating, indigestion)
indications.label: Bloating

indications.note: Carminative digestive and stomach tonic (bloating, indigestion)
indications.label: Indigestion

indications.note: Diuretic - reduces fluid retention/swelling and supports urinary and kidney function (traditionally used for urinary stones); animal studies attribute the effect to inhibition of the renal Na+/K+ pump, raising urine flow
indications.label: Swelling / fluid retention

contraindications.note: Culinary amounts are safe, but medicinal/seed doses and the essential oil (apiole, myristicin) can be toxic and stimulate the uterus - AVOID medicinal doses of parsley in pregnancy.
Its diuretic action means caution alongside diuretic medicines; the furanocoumarins can cause mild photosensitivity, and concentrated use should be avoided in active kidney disease.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Leaves and stems (foliage); the divided leaves resemble flat-leaf parsley, especially before flowering.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Poison hemlock is in the same family as parsley and its foliage is often described as parsley- or carrot-top-like; it can be confused with edible parsley-family plants. Its alkaloid coniine causes a paralysis that stops breathing, and eating even a small amount of any part can be fatal.
distinguishing_features: Poison hemlock stems are smooth, hairless and marked with purple or red spots and streaks; garden parsley stems have no purple blotching.
Crushed poison hemlock smells foul and musty ('mousy'); parsley smells clean and pleasantly herby.
Poison hemlock grows tall (often 1.5 to 3 m) with hollow stems; culinary parsley is a low herb.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Inspect the stem: hairless and smooth with purple or red spots and streaks = poison hemlock — do not eat. Confirm with the crushed-plant smell (foul and mousy versus parsley's pleasant herb scent).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Leaves and foliage; the finely divided glossy leaves closely resemble flat-leaf (Italian) parsley — the origin of the name 'fool's parsley'.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Named directly for the confusion with parsley, fool's parsley contains toxic polyacetylenes; poisoning causes burning in the mouth and throat and gastrointestinal and neurological effects, and it is especially a risk to children.
distinguishing_features: In flower, each little umbel of fool's parsley has three or four long, narrow bracteoles hanging downward beneath it (a 'beard'); true parsley has no such long drooping bracteoles.
Crushed fool's parsley smells foul, acrid and mousy; parsley smells clean and pleasantly herby.
Fool's parsley leaves are hairless, more finely divided, and darker and glossier than culinary parsley.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Look under the flower clusters for three or four long, downward-hanging bracteoles (a 'beard') — present in fool's parsley, absent in true parsley — and cross-check the foul mousy smell versus parsley's pleasant scent. If a parsley-like plant only smells 'off' and isn't flowering, don't use it without expert confirmation.

botanical_description.text: Annual herb (Apiaceae), 30-60 cm tall, with an erect, downy stem. Lower leaves are rounded and toothed or shallowly lobed, while upper leaves are finely feathery and pinnately divided. Small white flowers are borne in compound umbels, followed by small, ridged, downy fruit ('aniseed') with a strong, sweet, liquorice scent.
botanical_description.leaves: Lower leaves rounded/toothed; upper leaves finely feathery, pinnately divided
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, in compound umbels
botanical_description.fruit: Small, ridged, downy, sweetly liquorice-scented fruit ("aniseed")

habitat.text: Native to the eastern Mediterranean and south-west Asia, widely cultivated as a spice and medicinal crop in warm-temperate regions worldwide, needing a long, warm growing season and well-drained soil.

harvesting.text: Fruit ('seed') is harvested in late summer once the umbels have dried and turned brown, then threshed and dried further.
harvesting.season: Late summer, once umbels have dried

traditional_uses.text: Anise has a long Mediterranean and Middle Eastern tradition as a carminative digestive remedy for bloating and indigestion, an expectorant for coughs and catarrh, and a traditional galactagogue (milk-promoting) herb for nursing mothers. Commission E and WHO both approve its traditional use for catarrh of the upper respiratory tract and gastrointestinal spasms.

preparations.name: Crushed seed infusion (tea)
preparations.text: The classic carminative preparation: crushed seed steeped in hot water.

preparations.name: Essential oil
preparations.text: Concentrated aromatic fruit oil, used in small measured amounts in traditional formulations.

preparations.name: Chewed seed
preparations.text: Traditional after-meal digestive aid.

dosage.name: Crushed seed / essential oil
dosage.text: Duke (2002): about 0.5-1 g crushed seed three times daily in tea, or 0.05-0.2 mL of the fruit essential oil. Food amounts up to about 20 g/day are generally considered safe for adults. Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy and in children given limited safety data. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (trans-anethole)
constituents.note: Trans-anethole typically makes up the great majority of the volatile oil and gives anise its characteristic liquorice aroma and much of its digestive and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant activity.

constituents.name: Fixed oil and fatty acids
constituents.note: The fruit's lipid fraction.

contraindications.note: Generally safe when consumed in food amounts. Doses up to 20g daily considered safe for adults. May cause allergic reactions in some people. Caution advised during pregnancy and breastfeeding - stick to food amounts. Anethole can be toxic at high concentrations, particularly to the nervous system. Not enough safety data for medicinal use in children.

Duke (2002) provides clinical evidence (score 2) for anise as an antibacterial, antiseptic, antispasmodic, and expectorant. It has demonstrated estrogenic activity and is used as a clinically supported lactagogue to promote milk production. Commission E and WHO have both approved its use for catarrh and gastrointestinal spasms. Typical dosages are 0.5–1 g crushed seed three times per day in tea, or 0.05–0.2 ml of the fruit essential oil (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The plant's foliage - lower leaves broad and parsley-like, upper leaves fine and lace-like - and the umbels of small white flowers, plus the aromatic seeds, which resemble poison hemlock; and hemlock seedlings volunteering among planted anise.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Anise (Pimpinella anisum) is a parsley-family herb that poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) is documented to resemble. King County (Washington) lists anise among the edible parsley-family plants poison hemlock can be confused with, and the North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox lists Pimpinella anisum among the plants commonly confused with poison hemlock. Conium contains coniine and kills by ascending paralysis and respiratory failure - a few leaves or seeds can be fatal. Documented anise-specific poisonings are sparse, so the value of this record is the rock-solid tests below rather than a claim that this exact swap is common.
distinguishing_features: SMELL (decisive): crushed anise foliage and seed smell sweet and strongly of licorice/aniseed. Poison hemlock smells rank, musty and mouse-like, with no sweet licorice note.
Stem: poison hemlock has a hairless, hollow stem conspicuously blotched and streaked with red-purple, especially near the base. Anise stems are plain green and never carry purple blotches.
Stature: poison hemlock is a tall (often 1.5-3 m) branching biennial; cultivated anise is a small annual usually under 0.6 m. A large fern-leaved umbellifer with a purple-blotched stem is not anise.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Crush a leaf or seed and smell it, and look at the stem. A sweet licorice/aniseed scent with a plain green stem = anise. A musty/mousy smell and/or a hairless stem with red-purple blotches = poison hemlock - do not eat. If either warning sign is present, discard it.

botanical_description.text: Evergreen coniferous tree (Pinaceae), 15-35 m tall, with a long, straight trunk at maturity, distinctive orange-red flaking bark in the upper crown and grey-brown fissured bark at the base. Needles are blue-green, borne in pairs (fascicles of two) with a papery basal sheath. Small yellow male cones release pollen in spring; female cones are woody and mature over two years.
botanical_description.leaves: Needles blue-green, in pairs, with a papery basal sheath
botanical_description.flowers: Small yellow male cones (pollen); separate woody female cones
botanical_description.stem: Long, straight trunk; orange-red flaking bark in the upper crown, grey-brown fissured bark at the base
botanical_description.fruit: Woody cone, maturing over two years

habitat.text: Native across northern and central Europe and Siberia - the most widely distributed pine species in the world - forming extensive boreal and montane forests on poor, sandy or peaty, acidic soils; a pioneer species tolerant of dry, nutrient-poor ground.

harvesting.text: Young shoots ('candles') and needles are gathered in spring; bark is stripped from felled or cultivated trees; resin/sap is tapped from the trunk. Needle-bearing twigs can be cut for tea and for steam distillation of the essential oil year-round.
harvesting.season: Young shoots/needles in spring; bark and sap variable

traditional_uses.text: Scots pine has a long northern European tradition as a source of resinous, aromatic preparations for respiratory complaints (coughs, bronchitis, catarrh) and topical rheumatic/muscular remedies, and pine-needle tea has traditionally been valued as a vitamin-C-rich winter tonic. Modern research on the needle essential oil supports antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and expectorant activity.

preparations.name: Needle infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Fresh or dried young needles steeped in hot water - the traditional vitamin-C-rich tonic tea. Only use needles from a certainly identified pine, never yew (see dangerous look-alikes).

preparations.name: Essential oil (inhalation/topical)
preparations.text: Steam-distilled from the needles; used diluted in ointments, chest rubs or steam inhalation for respiratory complaints.

preparations.name: Bud/bark preparations
preparations.text: Traditional syrup or decoction of young buds or bark for bronchitis and sinus congestion.

dosage.name: Essential oil (topical)
dosage.text: Duke (2002): about 100-200 mg essential oil in an ointment or cream for topical use. Do not ingest the essential oil; avoid inhalation in severe asthma, whooping cough or laryngospasm. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Essential oil (alpha-pinene, beta-pinene, limonene)
constituents.note: The needle oil is dominated by monoterpene hydrocarbons, chiefly alpha-pinene, giving the characteristic resinous scent and much of its antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Resin acids (diterpene resin acids)
constituents.note: The resinous sap/pitch, traditionally used topically for wounds and skin complaints.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and flavonoids (needles)
constituents.note: The needle is traditionally valued as a source of vitamin C, particularly in winter.

contraindications.note: Generally safe in normal amounts. Pine essential oil should not be ingested; topical use only in diluted form. May irritate airways in high concentrations. Allergic reactions to pine pollen and resin are common. Turpentine products are irritant and potentially toxic if ingested.

Duke (2002) rates Scotch pine as + and notes antiseptic, antibacterial (score 1), and bronchospasmolytic activities. Commission E (KOM) and PhEur (PIP) approve pine needle preparations for topical use in rheumatic and neuralgic conditions and for inhalations in upper respiratory catarrh. Duke cautions that pine needle oil should not be inhaled by patients with severe asthma, whooping cough, or laryngospasm. Pine bud preparations are traditionally used for bronchitis and sinus congestion. Dose: 100–200 mg essential oil in ointment or cream for topical use (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Evergreen needles and twigs gathered to brew pine-needle tea; yew's flat evergreen needles and red berry-like arils are mistaken for edible conifer.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Pine needles (from Scots pine, Pinus sylvestris) are foraged to brew a vitamin-C-rich tea. Yew (Taxus baccata), one of the most poisonous trees in Europe, is a common evergreen in gardens, churchyards and hedges, and its flat evergreen needles are mistaken for edible conifer needles. The Missouri Poison Center explicitly warns to beware of a toxic pine look-alike, the yew tree or English yew (Taxus species), which holds cardiotoxic taxine alkaloids in all parts except the red flesh of the aril. Eating even a few yew leaves - or brewing them as 'pine' tea - can cause fatal heart-rhythm disturbances, seizures and cardiac arrest, with no antidote. Because pine-needle tea is popular and yew is widespread, this is a potentially fatal confusion.
distinguishing_features: Needle attachment (decisive): pine needles grow in bundles (Scots pine in pairs of two) wrapped at the base by a papery sheath. Yew needles grow SINGLY, attached directly to the twig in two flat rows. Bundled needles = pine; single flat needles in two rows = yew.
Fruit: pines bear woody cones. Yew bears no cones - instead soft red, berry-like arils each holding a single seed. A red berry-like fruit means yew, not pine.
Smell: crushed pine needles smell strongly resinous and piney. Yew needles have little or no pine scent.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Look at how the needles attach. Pine needles come in bundles (Scots pine in pairs) with a papery sheath at the base, and the tree bears woody cones and a strong piney scent. Yew needles are single, soft and flat in two rows, the tree bears red berry-like arils instead of cones and has little scent - yew is deadly, so never brew it. If the needles are single rather than bundled, or you see red berries, do not use it.

constituents.name: Kavalactones (kavain, dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, desmethoxyyangonin)
constituents.note: Lipophilic lactones concentrated in the root/rhizome; regarded as the main anxiolytic constituents, acting on GABA-A signalling and other CNS targets.

botanical_description.text: Robust, shrubby perennial with thick, jointed, cane-like stems and large, heart-shaped, glossy green leaves. The plant rarely if ever flowers or sets viable seed in cultivation and is propagated entirely by stem cuttings; the thick, knotty underground rhizome and root, the medicinal part, contains the characteristic kavalactone-rich resin.
botanical_description.habit: Robust, shrubby perennial with cane-like stems
botanical_description.leaves: Large, heart-shaped, glossy green
botanical_description.flowers: Rarely produced; the plant is propagated vegetatively by stem cuttings
botanical_description.root: Thick, knotty rhizome and root system, rich in kavalactone resin (the medicinal part)
botanical_description.fruit: Rarely if ever sets viable seed

habitat.text: Native to the islands of the South Pacific (Vanuatu, Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and neighbouring islands); cultivated in warm, humid, shaded tropical conditions.

harvesting.text: The root and rhizome are dug from plants at least three to five years old, when kavalactone content is highest, then cleaned, peeled and dried or processed fresh into a beverage.
harvesting.season: From mature (3-5+ year) plants

traditional_uses.text: Kava root has a centuries-long ceremonial and social tradition across Pacific Island cultures, prepared as a communal beverage for its calming, sociable and relaxing effects; this traditional anxiolytic reputation is now supported by clinical trials of standardised water-based extracts for anxiety.

preparations.name: Traditional beverage (water-based)
preparations.text: Peeled root pounded or ground and mixed with water, strained and drunk fresh, the traditional Pacific Island ceremonial and social preparation.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Water-based extract standardised to kavalactone content, taken as capsules; the form used in most clinical anxiety trials, and the safer choice given documented hepatotoxicity concerns with some solvent-extracted products.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for anxiety commonly use extracts providing up to around 250 mg kavalactones daily, from water-based extracts of peeled root of a noble cultivar, for short-term use only, given the rare but serious hepatotoxicity risk. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Standardised kava extracts significantly reduce anxiety versus placebo in controlled trials (Hamilton Anxiety scale).
Kavalactones have calming, sleep-supporting and muscle-relaxant effects and can add to the action of other CNS depressants.

contraindications.note: Rare but serious drug-induced liver injury (hepatitis, cirrhosis, liver failure) has been reported with kava; it is contraindicated in people with liver disease and should not be combined with alcohol or other medicines that can harm the liver. To reduce risk, use only water-based extracts of peeled root/rhizome from a noble cultivar, keep the dose at or below about 250 mg kavalactones per day, and use short-term only. Stop and seek care for signs of liver trouble (dark urine, jaundice, nausea, abdominal pain).

Kava is sedating and can add to the effects of alcohol, benzodiazepines and other central-nervous-system depressants, impairing alertness and driving; avoid such combinations. Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding, or before surgery.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Kava adds to central-nervous-system depression, so combining it with benzodiazepines, sleeping tablets, opioids, barbiturates or alcohol can cause excessive drowsiness and impaired coordination.

pairings.note: Kava and valerian are both calming, sedating herbs; taken together they may add up to cause excessive drowsiness, so use caution and avoid before driving or combining with other sedatives.
Kava and passionflower are both relaxing, sedating herbs; combined use may deepen drowsiness and sedation, so use caution.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (aucubin, catalpol)
constituents.note: Anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial constituents.

constituents.name: Mucilage polysaccharides (~2-3%)
constituents.note: Coat and protect inflamed mucous membranes and reduce cough-receptor sensitivity.

constituents.name: Phenylethanoids (acteoside/verbascoside, plantamajoside) and flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb (Plantaginaceae) forming a low rosette of narrow, lance-shaped leaves with prominent parallel ribs (hence 'ribwort'). Slender, furrowed, leafless flowering stalks rise from the rosette bearing a dense, short, dark brown-black ovoid to cylindrical flower spike ringed by a 'collar' of protruding cream-white stamens.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, prominently 3-5-ribbed, in a basal rosette
botanical_description.flowers: Dense, short, dark brown-black ovoid to cylindrical spike with a ring of cream-white stamens
botanical_description.fruit: Small capsule with 1-2 seeds

habitat.text: Native throughout Europe and temperate Asia and naturalised worldwide; common in grassland, meadows, lawns, roadsides and waste ground, tolerating trampling and a wide range of soils.

harvesting.text: Leaves and aerial parts are gathered during the growing season, ideally before or during flowering, and dried quickly.
harvesting.season: Growing season, before or during flowering

traditional_uses.text: Ribwort plantain has an official European herbal-monograph tradition for the temporary relief of dry cough and irritation of the mouth and throat, and for minor skin wounds, reflecting its long folk use as a demulcent respiratory remedy and wound herb. Modern research confirms mucilage-driven demulcent action alongside antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water for cough and sore throat.

preparations.name: Fresh pressed juice / syrup
preparations.text: Fresh leaf juice, traditionally made into a cough syrup, often combined with honey.

preparations.name: Poultice
preparations.text: Crushed fresh leaf applied to minor wounds and insect bites.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried leaf.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: EMA/ESCOP herbal-monograph guidance suggests roughly 2-4 g dried leaf per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily, or an equivalent liquid extract. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory for temporary mild inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat); in vitro the extract inhibits nitric oxide production and inducible NOS in macrophages, supporting its traditional respiratory anti-inflammatory use
Antibacterial, antiviral and immunostimulant (in vitro)
Antibacterial, antiviral and immunostimulant (in vitro)
Demulcent for catarrh of the respiratory tract and dry, irritating cough / bronchitis (mucilage soothes and protects irritated airways)
Antibacterial, antiviral and immunostimulant (in vitro)
Vulnerary for minor wounds and insect bites (topical)

indications.note: Demulcent for catarrh of the respiratory tract and dry, irritating cough / bronchitis (mucilage soothes and protects irritated airways)
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Demulcent for catarrh of the respiratory tract and dry, irritating cough / bronchitis (mucilage soothes and protects irritated airways)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory for temporary mild inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat); in vitro the extract inhibits nitric oxide production and inducible NOS in macrophages, supporting its traditional respiratory anti-inflammatory use
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory for temporary mild inflammation of the mouth and throat (sore throat); in vitro the extract inhibits nitric oxide production and inducible NOS in macrophages, supporting its traditional respiratory anti-inflammatory use
indications.label: Sore throat

indications.note: Vulnerary for minor wounds and insect bites (topical)
indications.label: Wounds

contraindications.note: Generally very well tolerated. A cough or throat irritation that persists beyond about a week, or is accompanied by fever or breathlessness, needs medical assessment.
Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of adequate data.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (aucubin, catalpol)
constituents.note: Characteristic iridoids of Plantago major.

constituents.name: Caffeic acid esters (plantamajoside, acteoside/verbascoside)
constituents.note: Polyphenolic esters contributing to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (baicalein, luteolin, hispidulin)
constituents.note: Flavonoid constituents of the leaf.

constituents.name: Allantoin
constituents.note: Cell-proliferative compound supporting wound healing.

constituents.name: Mucilage and triterpenes (ursolic and oleanolic acid)
constituents.note: Demulcent mucilage plus anti-inflammatory triterpenes.

botanical_description.text: Low, rosette-forming perennial herb (Plantaginaceae) with broad, oval to rounded leaves marked by 5-9 prominent parallel ribs, borne on long, channelled petioles. Slender, leafless flowering spikes rise from the rosette bearing tiny, densely packed greenish flowers, followed by small seed capsules.
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, oval to rounded, 5-9-ribbed, long-petioled
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, densely packed, greenish, in slender spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Small seed capsule

habitat.text: Native to Europe and temperate Asia and now one of the most widespread naturalised weeds worldwide (the name 'waybread', or historically 'white man's foot', reflects its spread along paths and trails); thrives on compacted, disturbed ground such as paths, lawns and roadsides.

harvesting.text: Leaves are gathered fresh throughout the growing season, especially for poultice use; seed is collected in autumn once ripe.
harvesting.season: Leaf throughout the growing season; seed in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Greater plantain is one of the most widely used wound herbs across world folk-medicine traditions, applied fresh as a poultice to cuts, stings and insect bites, and taken as a tea for cough, digestive upset and mild diarrhoea. Modern reviews confirm broad anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, antioxidant and wound-healing activity supporting these traditional uses.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf poultice
preparations.text: Crushed or chewed fresh leaf applied directly to wounds, stings and insect bites - the classic traditional first-aid use.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water for cough, digestive complaints and mild diarrhoea.

preparations.name: Seed (bulk fibre)
preparations.text: The mucilaginous seed, related to psyllium, used traditionally as a mild bulk laxative.

dosage.name: Leaf tea / poultice
dosage.text: Duke (2002): leaf taken as a tea, or used fresh as a poultice, for wounds, sore throat and bronchial conditions. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Astringent (diarrhoea)
Demulcent / expectorant (coughs, catarrh, irritated airways)
Demulcent / expectorant (coughs, catarrh, irritated airways)
Bulk laxative (mucilaginous seeds)
Vulnerary (wound healing) - fresh leaf poultice

contraindications.note: Extremely safe; one of the most widely used edible and medicinal plants in the world. Psyllium seeds (closely related) are well-studied as a bulk laxative but should always be taken with adequate water. Allergic reactions are rare but possible. No significant drug interactions documented at normal doses.

Duke (2002) rates plantain as ++ and notes anti-inflammatory, antitussive, antiatherosclerotic, and COX-2-inhibitory activities at the experimental level (score 1). The seeds (psyllium) of related species have clinical evidence for cholesterol reduction and as bulk laxatives, though entries here apply to the leaf. Dose: leaf as tea or poultice for wounds, sore throat, and bronchial conditions. Duke notes the plant is generally safe with anti-inflammatory topical use well-supported in traditional practice. Commission E approved plantain (ribwort species) for catarrh and wound healing (Duke, 2002).
Generally regarded as safe as food and in customary medicinal use; allergic reactions are uncommon, though the abundant wind-borne pollen can aggravate hay fever in sensitive people.
Correct identification is essential and material should be gathered away from roadsides, sprayed lawns and other contaminated ground; deep or infected wounds warrant proper medical care.
Additional omniasana.bio sources cited for Plantago major (identification, distribution, foraging, traditional use and supporting evidence; see bibliography).

botanical_description.text: Wood-decay fungus (not a true plant, Pleurotaceae) that grows in shelf-like, overlapping clusters on dead or dying hardwood trees and stumps. Fan- or oyster-shell-shaped caps, 5-25 cm across, are usually grey, tan or brown (occasionally pale or white), with white gills running down a short, off-centre stem. The mycelium is a fine white network that colonises and decomposes the wood substrate before fruiting.
botanical_description.leaves: Not applicable (fungus)
botanical_description.flowers: Not applicable (fungus); reproduces by spores
botanical_description.stem: Short, off-centre or lateral, white decurrent gills running down it
botanical_description.root: Mycelium spreading through the wood substrate
botanical_description.fruit: Pale gilled underside releasing a white to lilac-grey spore print
botanical_description.flowering_period: Fruiting bodies typically appear in cooler, moist seasons (autumn-spring in temperate climates)

habitat.text: Grows naturally on dead or dying broadleaf (hardwood) trees - beech, oak, poplar and others - in temperate forests worldwide; widely cultivated commercially on straw, sawdust and other lignocellulosic substrates.

harvesting.text: Wild fruiting bodies are picked as the caps mature but before they become tough or insect-damaged; cultivated mushrooms are harvested from substrate blocks or bags once the caps have expanded but before releasing significant spore load. Never forage a white shelf-fungus from wood without confirming the host tree, since the fatal Angel Wing look-alike is specific to conifer (softwood) wood.
harvesting.season: Cooler, moist seasons (autumn-spring in temperate climates)

traditional_uses.text: Oyster mushroom is a widely eaten culinary mushroom with a growing modern reputation as a medicinal fungus, valued for immune support and general wellbeing. Contemporary research on its beta-glucan polysaccharides supports immunomodulatory, antioxidant, anticancer and cardiometabolic activity.

preparations.name: Cooked fruiting body (culinary)
preparations.text: The common food form; always eaten cooked, never raw.

preparations.name: Dried mushroom / powder
preparations.text: Dried and powdered for use in soups, broths, or encapsulated supplements.

preparations.name: Standardised polysaccharide extract
preparations.text: Beta-glucan-rich extract, the form used in most bioactivity research.
