dosage.name: Polysaccharide extract / culinary use
dosage.text: Most research uses standardised polysaccharide (beta-glucan) extracts rather than a specific whole-food gram dose; as a food, oyster mushroom is eaten in normal culinary amounts, always well cooked. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Beta-glucan polysaccharides
constituents.note: The principal bioactive constituents, responsible for most of the mushroom's immunomodulatory and anticancer activity.

constituents.name: Glycoproteins (protein-bound polysaccharide complexes)
constituents.note: Contribute to immunomodulatory activity alongside the pure beta-glucans.

constituents.name: Phenolic antioxidants (including ergothioneine)
constituents.note: Contribute to the antioxidant activity of the fruiting body.

contraindications.note: Generally very safe as a food. Rare cases of occupational asthma and allergy reported among people working with mushroom cultivation. Safe for culinary use without significant known interactions.
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry for Oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus) in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The fruiting body — a white, shelf-like gilled mushroom on wood, which looks like a small white oyster mushroom.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Angel wing was eaten as a traditional food in Japan until an outbreak of acute brain inflammation (encephalopathy) in autumn 2004 killed several people; most victims were elderly and, crucially, had reduced kidney function. Because it can be fatal in anyone with impaired kidneys while looking and tasting like an ordinary edible, it is now regarded as unsafe.
distinguishing_features: Angel wing grows ONLY on dead conifer (softwood) wood — spruce, fir, pine; wild oyster mushrooms grow on hardwood (broadleaf) trees. The host tree is the single most reliable difference.
Angel wing is pure chalk-white all over; oysters are usually grey, tan or brown, and only rarely white.
Angel-wing flesh is very thin, floppy and almost translucent at the edge, and the caps are small; oyster flesh is thick, dense and meaty, often forming large shelves.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Identify the wood: angel wing grows on conifer (softwood) wood and is thin, small and pure white. If you cannot confirm the host is a hardwood (broadleaf) tree, do not eat a white oyster-like mushroom — anyone with reduced kidney function is at particular risk.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The whole cap-and-gilled fruiting body; jack-o'-lantern grows in oyster-like clusters on wood.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Jack-o'-lantern mushrooms grow in clusters on wood and have true gills, so they are mistaken for oyster mushrooms (and chanterelles). They contain illudin toxins and cause severe vomiting, cramps and diarrhoea within an hour or two — painful and sometimes needing hospital care, though rarely fatal in healthy adults.
distinguishing_features: Jack-o'-lantern is bright orange to yellow-orange all over — cap, gills AND flesh; oyster mushrooms are white, grey, tan or brown, never bright orange.
Its flesh is orange when cut; oyster flesh is white.
In the dark its gills may glow faintly green; oysters never glow.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Colour: a mushroom that is bright orange throughout (cap, gills and flesh) is not an oyster mushroom, which is always white, grey, tan or brown. Cut it — orange flesh means jack-o'-lantern, do not eat.

constituents.name: Tannins (ellagitannins such as agrimoniin, and catechin-type tannins)
constituents.note: The main astringent active constituents.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Antioxidant supporting constituents.

botanical_description.text: Low, sprawling to semi-erect perennial herb (Rosaceae) with wiry stems. Leaves are palmate, typically with 4-5 leaflets and large leaf-like stipules that give the impression of more leaflets. Flowers are small, yellow and four-petalled - unusual among Potentilla species, most of which have five petals. The thick, woody rhizome is reddish inside when cut, the origin of the old name 'bloodroot' for this European species.
botanical_description.height: Low, sprawling to semi-erect, stems to about 30-45 cm
botanical_description.habit: Low, sprawling to semi-erect perennial herb
botanical_description.leaves: Palmate, typically 4-5 leaflets, with large leaf-like stipules
botanical_description.flowers: Small, yellow, four-petalled (unusual among Potentilla species)
botanical_description.root: Thick, woody rhizome, reddish inside when cut
botanical_description.fruit: Small dry achenes (typical Rosaceae)

habitat.text: Native to Europe and western Asia, common in acidic grassland, heath, moorland, open woodland and pastures on poor, well-drained soils.

harvesting.text: The rhizome is dug in autumn or early spring, when tannin content is highest, then cleaned and dried.
harvesting.season: Autumn or early spring

traditional_uses.text: Tormentil rhizome has an official European herbal-monograph tradition for mild diarrhoea and inflammation of the mouth and throat, reflecting its long folk use as a powerful astringent for gut and mucous-membrane complaints. Modern trials confirm its ellagitannin-rich rhizome shortens the duration of infectious (rotavirus) diarrhoea.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried rhizome simmered in water, the standard astringent preparation for diarrhoea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract, taken diluted or used as a gargle/mouthwash.

preparations.name: Gargle/mouthwash
preparations.text: Diluted decoction or tincture used for sore throat and mouth inflammation.

dosage.name: Decoction/tincture
dosage.text: EMA/ESCOP guidance suggests around 4-6 g dried rhizome (or an equivalent decoction), divided into 2-3 doses daily, for diarrhoea, or a diluted tincture used as a gargle several times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and inflammation of the mouth and throat
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (ellagitannins tighten the gut lining) - in a paediatric RCT, tormentil root extract shortened rotavirus diarrhoea (3 vs 5 days) and reduced rehydration needs
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (ellagitannins tighten the gut lining) - in a paediatric RCT, tormentil root extract shortened rotavirus diarrhoea (3 vs 5 days) and reduced rehydration needs

indications.note: Astringent for mild diarrhoea (ellagitannins tighten the gut lining) - in a paediatric RCT, tormentil root extract shortened rotavirus diarrhoea (3 vs 5 days) and reduced rehydration needs
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Supportive in inflammatory bowel disease / ulcerative colitis (limited trial evidence)
indications.label: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and inflammation of the mouth and throat
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and inflammation of the mouth and throat
indications.label: Sore throat

contraindications.note: Tannin-rich: high or prolonged intake may cause stomach upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.
Diarrhoea persisting more than 3-4 days, or with fever or blood in the stool, needs medical care. Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of data.

botanical_description.text: Low perennial herb (Primulaceae) forming a basal rosette of wrinkled, oval, softly hairy leaves that taper abruptly to a winged stalk. A single leafless flowering stem rises from the rosette bearing a one-sided umbel of small, fragrant, deep yellow, funnel-shaped flowers with orange spots at the throat, nodding on slender pedicels.
botanical_description.leaves: Wrinkled, oval, softly hairy, tapering to a winged stalk
botanical_description.flowers: Small, fragrant, deep yellow, funnel-shaped with orange throat spots, in a one-sided nodding umbel
botanical_description.fruit: Small capsule

habitat.text: Native across Europe and western Asia, growing in unimproved, species-rich grassland, meadows, pastures, open woodland and hedge banks on calcareous or well-drained soils.

harvesting.text: Flowers are picked in spring as they open; the root/rhizome is dug in autumn.
harvesting.season: Flower in spring; root/rhizome in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Cowslip has a long European folk tradition as an expectorant remedy for coughs and bronchial catarrh, and the flowers have traditionally been used as a mild sedative and for headache. Modern research on its saponin-rich root supports secretolytic (mucus-thinning) expectorant activity, and its flowers are rich in antioxidant phenolics.

preparations.name: Root decoction/tea
preparations.text: Dried root simmered or infused, the traditional expectorant preparation for cough with mucus.

preparations.name: Flower infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried flower steeped in hot water, a milder traditional calming and headache remedy.

preparations.name: Standardised combination extract
preparations.text: Root is a component of some standardised multi-herb bronchial/cough products (e.g. combined with ivy and thyme).

dosage.name: Root/herb tea
dosage.text: Duke (2002): about 0.5-1 g root or 1-2 g herb as tea, three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription; large doses of the saponin-rich root can cause gastrointestinal upset.

constituents.name: Triterpene saponins (primulic acid)
constituents.note: Concentrated in the root; responsible for the traditional expectorant (secretolytic) action.

constituents.name: Phenolic glycosides and flavonoids
constituents.note: Found mainly in the flowers and leaves; contribute antioxidant activity.

contraindications.note: Allergy risk: avoid if you’re allergic to cowslip/other Primula species (European Medicines Agency, 2012). Stomach sensitivity: use caution if you have gastritis or a stomach ulcer—cowslip can upset the stomach in some people (European Medicines Agency, 2012). Possible side effects (especially high dose): nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; allergic reactions can happen (European Medicines Agency, 2012). Pregnancy/breastfeeding: not recommended due to lack of good safety data (European Medicines Agency, 2012). Kids: many preparations are not recommended under 12 (and some root extracts under 4) unless a labeled product specifically allows it (European Medicines Agency, 2012).

Duke (2002) provides clinical evidence (score 2) for cowslip's expectorant and secretolytic activities, consistent with Commission E approval for bronchitis and cough with mucus. Dose: 0.5–1 g root or 1–2 g herb as tea three times daily. Saponins in the root are responsible for the expectorant action. Duke warns that the plant may cause hemolysis in large doses due to saponin content, and caution is warranted in individuals with aspirin sensitivity due to phenolic acid content (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Cowslip is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

constituents.name: Triterpenoids and phenolic acids
constituents.note: Pentacyclic triterpenoids (ursolic, oleanolic and betulinic acids), the phenolic acid rosmarinic acid, coumarins (umbelliferone, scopoletin, esculetin) and the flavonoid luteolin are the main bioactives behind self-heal's antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity.

constituents.name: Sulphated and polyphenol-conjugated polysaccharides
constituents.note: Water-extracted polysaccharides — including a partially sulphated arabinogalactomannan (PSP-2B) and polyphenolic-protein-polysaccharide conjugates (PVG/PVE30) — are the principal antiviral and immunomodulatory constituents, blocking herpes simplex virus attachment and modulating innate immune signalling.

botanical_description.text: Low, creeping to semi-erect perennial herb (Lamiaceae), 5-30 cm tall, with square, often purplish stems. Leaves are opposite, ovate with slightly toothed margins. Small, two-lipped, violet-purple flowers are densely packed into a compact, club-shaped terminal spike subtended by purplish bracts.
botanical_description.habit: Low, creeping to semi-erect perennial herb
botanical_description.leaves: Opposite, ovate, slightly toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, two-lipped, violet-purple, in a compact club-shaped terminal spike
botanical_description.fruit: Four small nutlets (typical Lamiaceae schizocarp)

habitat.text: Native throughout Europe, Asia and North America (circumboreal), extremely common in lawns, meadows, woodland clearings, roadsides and waste ground, tolerating mowing and a wide range of soils.

harvesting.text: Whole flowering aerial parts (flower, leaf and stem) are cut during flowering (summer) and dried.
harvesting.season: Summer, during flowering

traditional_uses.text: Selfheal has an extremely broad folk-medicine reputation across European, North American and Traditional Chinese Medicine traditions - as its name suggests, used as an all-purpose wound herb, gargle for sore throat, and internal remedy for fevers and inflammation. Modern research on its triterpenoid, phenolic and polysaccharide constituents supports wide-ranging antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial and antiviral activity.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf poultice
preparations.text: Crushed fresh herb applied to wounds, cuts and bruises.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried aerial parts steeped in hot water, taken internally or used as a gargle or wash.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests roughly 2-4 g dried herb per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily, or an equivalent tincture; used topically as a wash or gargle at similar strength. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

contraindications.note: Generally very safe and well tolerated. No significant known toxicity or drug interactions. Safe for topical and internal use in normal medicinal doses. Suitable for most adults including children in appropriate doses.

Duke (2002) rates self-heal as +++ and notes antioxidant (score 2), anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and COX-2 inhibitory activities. The plant contains luteolin, rosmarinic acid, and hyperoside — all with well-documented bioactivities. Hepatoprotective activity has been experimentally demonstrated. Duke notes that self-heal has a broad antimicrobial spectrum and an impressive antioxidant profile, supporting its traditional reputation as a versatile wound-healing herb. No significant safety concerns at normal herbal doses (Duke, 2002).

constituents.name: Phytosterols (beta-sitosterol)
constituents.note: Sterols associated with the prostate-supporting activity.

constituents.name: Pentacyclic triterpenes (ursolic and oleanolic acid) and ferulic acid esters
constituents.note: Anti-inflammatory supporting constituents of the bark.

botanical_description.text: Evergreen tree (Rosaceae), 10-30 m tall, with dark, fissured, red-brown bark (the source of the common name 'red stinkwood', from its unpleasant smell when cut). Leaves are glossy dark green, leathery, oblong with finely toothed margins. Small white, five-petalled flowers are borne in axillary racemes, followed by small reddish-brown, two-lobed fruit.
botanical_description.leaves: Glossy dark green, leathery, oblong, finely toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, five-petalled, in axillary racemes
botanical_description.fruit: Small, reddish-brown, two-lobed fruit
botanical_description.flowering_period: Variable, often twice yearly in its native range

habitat.text: Native to montane forests of central and southern Africa (and Madagascar), growing at moderate to high altitude (roughly 900-3400 m). The species is CITES-listed and conservation-threatened owing to over-harvesting of bark for the pharmaceutical trade.

harvesting.text: Bark is traditionally stripped from mature trees. Unsustainable stripping - especially removing bark all the way round the trunk - kills the tree and has driven population decline, so sustainable, partial and rotational bark harvesting or cultivated sources are recommended.
harvesting.season: Not standardised; sustainable/rotational harvesting recommended

traditional_uses.text: Prunus africana bark has a traditional use in East and Central African ethnomedicine for urinary complaints, and its extract (marketed as Pygeum) became one of the most widely used European phytotherapy remedies for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) symptoms in the 20th century. Clinical evidence for symptom benefit is moderate but drawn from small, methodologically weak trials.

preparations.name: Standardised lipophilic bark extract (capsule)
preparations.text: The clinically studied commercial form, standardised for phytosterol content.

preparations.name: Traditional bark decoction
preparations.text: Dried bark simmered in water, a traditional East/Central African preparation.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials have most often used around 100-200 mg/day of standardised lipophilic bark extract, divided into two doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription; any prostate or urinary symptom should be medically assessed first.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory (prostate)

indications.note: Supports lower urinary tract symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) - a Cochrane meta-analysis of 18 RCTs (1562 men) found a moderate improvement in urinary symptoms and flow versus placebo, but the trials were small, short and methodologically weak, so the evidence remains uncertain
indications.label: Prostate support

indications.note: Supports lower urinary tract symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) - a Cochrane meta-analysis of 18 RCTs (1562 men) found a moderate improvement in urinary symptoms and flow versus placebo, but the trials were small, short and methodologically weak, so the evidence remains uncertain
indications.label: Urinary support

indications.note: Supports lower urinary tract symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) - a Cochrane meta-analysis of 18 RCTs (1562 men) found a moderate improvement in urinary symptoms and flow versus placebo, but the trials were small, short and methodologically weak, so the evidence remains uncertain
indications.label: Urinary tract infection (UTI)

contraindications.note: Lower urinary tract / prostate symptoms must be medically assessed first to exclude prostate cancer. The evidence is mixed and rests on small, short, methodologically weak trials, so men with moderate or severe BPH should not rely on it instead of proven treatment.
Generally well tolerated, with mild gastrointestinal effects the most common report.

constituents.name: Cyanogenic glycoside prunasin
constituents.note: Releases trace hydrogen cyanide that calms the cough reflex - the basis of both the antitussive action and the safety caution.

constituents.name: Tannins, coumarins (scopoletin) and benzaldehyde
constituents.note: Astringent and aromatic supporting constituents.

constituents.name: Flavanones (naringenin) and methoxylated benzene derivatives
constituents.note: Bio-guided fractionation of the bark identified naringenin and trimethoxy-benzene/benzoic-acid compounds; the bark extract also showed antiparasitic (cysticidal) activity in laboratory studies.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous tree (Rosaceae), 15-30 m tall, with dark, scaly, aromatic bark that has a distinctive bitter-almond smell when cut or crushed (from cyanogenic compounds). Leaves are glossy dark green, elliptical and finely toothed. Small white flowers are borne in drooping racemes, followed by small, dark purple-black cherries that are edible when fully ripe but otherwise bitter.
botanical_description.leaves: Glossy dark green, elliptical, finely toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, in drooping racemes
botanical_description.stem: Dark, scaly, aromatic bark with a bitter-almond smell when cut
botanical_description.fruit: Small, dark purple-black cherries, bitter unless fully ripe

habitat.text: Native to eastern North America, growing in a wide range of woodland, forest-edge and open habitats on well-drained soils; widely naturalised and, in parts of Europe, considered an invasive tree.

harvesting.text: Bark is stripped from the trunk and larger branches, traditionally in autumn, and must be thoroughly dried before use - fresh bark has a much higher cyanogenic-glycoside content and is more toxic.
harvesting.season: Traditionally autumn; must be dried before use

traditional_uses.text: Wild cherry bark has a long Native American and European settler tradition as a cough remedy, valued specifically for its unusual cough-suppressant action - from the cyanogenic glycoside prunasin, which releases a trace, sedating amount of cyanide that calms the cough reflex. It remains a classic ingredient of traditional cough syrups.

preparations.name: Decoction/infusion
preparations.text: Dried bark gently simmered or infused in hot water - the traditional cough preparation.

preparations.name: Syrup
preparations.text: Bark decoction sweetened - a classic cough-syrup base.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried bark.

dosage.name: Decoction
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests roughly 1-2 g dried bark per cup as a decoction, up to three times daily, using only properly dried bark; avoid large or prolonged doses given the cyanogenic-glycoside content, and avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antispasmodic for bronchial cough and bronchitis (eases spasm of the airways)
Antitussive - calms a persistent dry, irritating cough (prunasin quietens the cough reflex)

indications.note: Antispasmodic for bronchial cough and bronchitis (eases spasm of the airways)
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Antispasmodic for bronchial cough and bronchitis (eases spasm of the airways); Antitussive - calms a persistent dry, irritating cough (prunasin quietens the cough reflex)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Soothes sore throat and respiratory irritation (traditional)
indications.label: Sore throat

contraindications.note: Contains cyanogenic compounds: use only properly dried, prepared bark and avoid large or prolonged doses (fresh bark has a higher toxic content). It suppresses the cough reflex, so it suits dry, unproductive coughs - not productive coughs where mucus needs clearing.
Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding because of the cyanogenic glycosides.

botanical_description.text: Saprotrophic fungus (not a true plant, Hymenogastraceae) with a cap 2-8 cm across, convex to bell-shaped, golden-brown to caramel with a paler margin, fading as it dries. The whitish to yellowish stem often bears a thin ring (annulus) that darkens purple-black from falling spores; gills are grey to dark purple-brown, crowded and adnate. The mycelium colonises dung and dung-enriched, nutrient-rich soil. All parts stain blue-green when bruised or handled, from oxidation of psilocin.
botanical_description.height: Cap 2-8 cm across; stem several cm tall
botanical_description.leaves: Not applicable (fungus)
botanical_description.flowers: Not applicable (fungus); reproduces by spores
botanical_description.stem: Whitish to yellowish, often with a thin ring (annulus) that darkens with spores
botanical_description.root: Mycelium colonising dung and dung-enriched soil
botanical_description.fruit: Grey to dark purple-brown gills; bruises blue-green when handled
botanical_description.flowering_period: Fruiting bodies appear in warm, humid weather, often following rain

habitat.text: Most common in subtropical and tropical regions of the Americas (and increasingly recorded elsewhere), growing directly on the dung of grazing livestock - particularly cattle - and on dung-enriched pasture soil in warm, humid climates.

harvesting.text: Fruiting bodies are picked by hand as caps expand, typically in warm, humid weather following rain; cultivated production uses sterilised grain or dung-based substrate inoculated with mycelium under controlled humidity and temperature.
harvesting.season: Warm, humid weather, often following rain

traditional_uses.text: Psilocybe mushrooms have a documented history of ceremonial and shamanic use in Mesoamerican indigenous traditions, later popularised globally from the mid-20th century onward. Contemporary clinical research on purified psilocybin, the principal active compound, supports use under professional supervision in psilocybin-assisted therapy for depression and anxiety, distinct from unsupervised recreational use.

preparations.name: Dried whole mushroom
preparations.text: The traditional and most common form; drying concentrates and stabilises psilocybin/psilocin content.

preparations.name: Purified psilocybin (clinical)
preparations.text: The standardised, purified compound used in modern clinical trials, administered under professional supervision.

preparations.name: Tea/extract
preparations.text: Dried mushroom steeped in hot water, a traditional preparation said to reduce gastrointestinal discomfort.

dosage.name: Purified psilocybin (clinical)
dosage.text: Clinical trials of psilocybin-assisted therapy have used purified, weight-calibrated doses (commonly in the range of 20-25 mg psilocybin) administered once or twice under professional supervision in a controlled clinical setting - fundamentally different from unsupervised recreational dosing. Psilocybin is illegal in most jurisdictions outside approved clinical/research settings and should never be used outside professional supervision. Educational reference only, not a prescription or an endorsement of unsupervised use.

constituents.name: Psilocybin
constituents.note: The principal prodrug alkaloid, an indole (tryptamine) compound dephosphorylated to psilocin in the body; the primary active constituent driving the psychoactive and clinically studied antidepressant/anxiolytic effects.

constituents.name: Psilocin
constituents.note: The active metabolite of psilocybin, a serotonin (5-HT2A) receptor agonist directly responsible for the psychoactive effect.

constituents.name: Baeocystin and norbaeocystin (minor tryptamine alkaloids)
constituents.note: Minor related tryptamines present alongside psilocybin/psilocin at lower concentrations, of uncertain individual contribution.

actions.note: Psilocybin-assisted therapy for depression (clinical trials)
Reduces anxiety; preclinical fear-memory extinction

indications.note: Psilocybin-assisted therapy reduced depression in clinical trials
indications.label: Low mood / depression

indications.note: Reduced anxiety in clinical and preclinical studies
indications.label: Anxiety

contraindications.note: Psilocybin is physiologically non-toxic and non-addictive in clinical contexts. Psychological risks include anxiety, paranoia, and triggering of latent psychiatric conditions (contraindicated in personal or family history of psychosis or schizophrenia). Use only in appropriate clinical/ceremonial settings with trained supervision. Illegal in most jurisdictions. Avoid mixing with lithium (seizure risk).
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry for psilocybin mushrooms (Psilocybe cubensis) in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: The whole small brown mushroom — cap, ringed stem and gills — especially wood-growing collections. Deadly Galerina grows on the same rotting wood and wood chips as wood-loving Psilocybe.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Deadly Galerina (funeral bell) is the amatoxin mushroom most often mistaken for hallucinogenic Psilocybe and eaten by accident; the two are small brown ringed mushrooms that grow side by side on rotting wood and bark mulch. Galerina carries the same liver-destroying amatoxins as the death cap — by weight sometimes higher — and poisoning is deceptively delayed (6 to 24 hours), with a false recovery before catastrophic liver failure. Both have a ring on the stem and grow on wood, so the ring and the substrate do NOT tell them apart.
distinguishing_features: Spore-print colour is the only reliable test, and it needs a print taken at home: deadly Galerina drops a RUSTY, cinnamon-brown print; Psilocybe drops a dark purple-brown to blackish-violet print. A rusty-brown print means it is NOT a Psilocybe — do not eat it.
Psilocybe flesh usually bruises blue-green where handled; deadly Galerina does not. This is only a supporting hint — bluing can be faint or absent and must NEVER override a wrong spore print.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: There is no safe field mark. Take a spore print (gills down on white paper or foil, covered, several hours): a rusty or cinnamon-brown print = deadly Galerina or another amatoxin mushroom — do not eat it. Only a dark purple-brown to violet-black print is consistent with Psilocybe. Do not forage small brown wood-growing mushrooms without an expert mycologist and a spore print — a single wrong cap can cause fatal liver failure.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids (rosmarinic, lithospermic and salvianolic acids)
constituents.note: The main antioxidant and COX-2-inhibiting constituents; LC-MS/MS profiling of the aerial parts identified dozens of phenolic compounds (caffeic acid esters and danshensu/dicaffeic-acid conjugates), nine of them previously undescribed, with marked seasonal variation.

constituents.name: Mucilage, flavonoids, allantoin and silicic acid
constituents.note: Soothing and supporting constituents underlying the traditional respiratory use.

botanical_description.text: Low, hairy perennial herb (Boraginaceae), 15-30 cm tall. Basal leaves are long-stalked, ovate to lance-shaped, often white-spotted - the spotted pattern was historically likened to diseased lung tissue under the medieval Doctrine of Signatures, giving rise to the name 'lungwort' - and covered in bristly hairs. Funnel-shaped flowers open pink and turn blue as they mature, borne in small coiled clusters (cymes).
botanical_description.leaves: Long-stalked, ovate to lance-shaped, often white-spotted, bristly-hairy
botanical_description.flowers: Funnel-shaped, opening pink and turning blue with age, in small coiled cymes
botanical_description.fruit: Small nutlets (typical Boraginaceae)

habitat.text: Native to central and southern Europe, growing in damp, shaded woodland, hedgebanks and scrub on humus-rich soils; widely cultivated as a shade garden plant.

harvesting.text: Aerial flowering parts are cut during flowering (spring); the plant can also be gathered later in the growing season, though the phenolic constituent profile is documented to differ measurably between spring and autumn harvests.
harvesting.season: Spring (flowering); constituent profile varies spring vs autumn

traditional_uses.text: Lungwort's spotted leaves inspired its traditional use, under the Doctrine of Signatures, as a remedy for lung and respiratory complaints - coughs, bronchitis, catarrh and sore throat - valued for its soothing mucilage and astringent tannins. Modern research confirms antioxidant and anti-inflammatory (COX-2-inhibiting) activity of its phenolic-rich extract, though clinical trial evidence in humans remains limited.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried aerial parts steeped in hot water, the traditional respiratory remedy.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf
preparations.text: Occasionally used fresh in salads or soups, or as a poultice, in some traditional culinary and topical uses.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried aerial parts.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests roughly 2-4 g dried herb per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily, for short-term use; avoid prolonged or high-dose internal use given the borage family's potential pyrrolizidine-alkaloid content. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory (cyclooxygenase-2 inhibition) and antioxidant
Anti-inflammatory (cyclooxygenase-2 inhibition) and antioxidant
Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)
Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)
indications.label: Respiratory support

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis, sore throat and respiratory catarrh (mucilage and saponins)
indications.label: Sore throat

contraindications.note: As a member of the borage family (Boraginaceae), which can contain hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids, prolonged or high-dose internal use is best avoided; use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.
Traditional use only; clinical evidence in humans is limited, so a cough or respiratory complaint that persists needs medical assessment.

botanical_description.text: Vigorous, invasive rhizomatous perennial (Polygonaceae) with hollow, bamboo-like, jointed stems 1-3 m tall (occasionally taller), speckled reddish-purple. Leaves are broad, heart- to shovel-shaped with a flat base. Abundant sprays of small, creamy-white flowers appear in late summer, followed by small winged fruit.
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, heart- to shovel-shaped, flat-based
botanical_description.flowers: Small, creamy-white, in abundant sprays
botanical_description.fruit: Small winged achenes

habitat.text: Native to East Asia (Japan, China, Korea), now a notorious invasive species across Europe and North America, growing in disturbed ground, riverbanks, roadsides and waste land, spreading aggressively via an extensive rhizome network.

harvesting.text: The rhizome/root, the main medicinal part, is dug in autumn or winter when resveratrol content is highest; young spring shoots (stems) are also edible and used. Strict containment is required when harvesting, given the plant's severe invasiveness.
harvesting.season: Root in autumn/winter; young stems in spring

traditional_uses.text: Known as Hu Zhang in Traditional Chinese Medicine, Japanese knotweed root has a centuries-old use for inflammation, infections, jaundice and menstrual complaints. Modern interest centres on its resveratrol and emodin content, now studied (as Polygonum cuspidatum extract) for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective and hepatoprotective activity, and more recently for supportive use in acute respiratory infections.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried rhizome simmered in water, the classic Traditional Chinese Medicine preparation.

preparations.name: Standardised resveratrol extract (capsule)
preparations.text: The modern commercial supplement form, standardised for resveratrol content.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried rhizome.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Duke (2002) and general phytotherapy guidance treat this as an experimentally supported herb without a single agreed clinical dose; commercial resveratrol-standardised extracts should be dosed per product labelling. Educational reference only, not a prescription; avoid in pregnancy.

constituents.name: Stilbenes (resveratrol, piceid)
constituents.note: Considered the principal bioactive constituents, responsible for much of the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective activity attributed to this root.

constituents.name: Anthraquinones (emodin, physcion)
constituents.note: Emodin has laxative and anti-inflammatory activity at typical concentrations but can cause gastrointestinal upset at high doses.

constituents.name: Polysaccharides
constituents.note: Studied for immunomodulatory and other bioactivities.

constituents.name: Phenolic glycosides
constituents.note: Minor phenolic constituents of the rhizome.

contraindications.note: Japanese knotweed root contains resveratrol and emodin; high doses of emodin may cause GI discomfort and have laxative effects. Not recommended during pregnancy (emodin has potential teratogenic effects in animal studies). May interact with anticoagulants and antiplatelet medications. Quality control of commercial preparations is important as invasive weed extracts vary significantly.

Duke (2002) rates Japanese knotweed (listed as Hu-Zhang, Fallopia japonica) as +++ and highlights its rich content of resveratrol and emodin. Experimental evidence (score 1) supports COX-2 inhibitory, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, and lipid-lowering activities — largely attributed to resveratrol. Duke notes its use in traditional Chinese medicine for fractures, burns, abscesses, and gynecological conditions. No significant clinical trials were available at time of publication, but resveratrol's biological activity is well-documented in vitro (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Succulent perennial herb (Crassulaceae), 15-40 cm tall, with a thick, fleshy, branching rhizome that has a rose-like fragrance when cut - the origin of the name 'rosea'. Leaves are fleshy, greyish-green, oval to oblong. Small yellow (occasionally reddish) flowers are clustered in dense terminal heads; the species is dioecious, with separate male and female plants. Fruit follicles turn red as they ripen.
botanical_description.leaves: Fleshy, greyish-green, oval to oblong
botanical_description.flowers: Small, yellow (occasionally reddish), in dense terminal clusters
botanical_description.root: Thick, fleshy, branching rhizome, rose-scented when cut
botanical_description.fruit: Follicles, turning red as they ripen

habitat.text: Native to arctic and mountainous regions of Europe, Asia and North America (circumpolar), growing in cold, rocky, high-altitude or high-latitude terrain - cliffs, rocky slopes and tundra - tolerating harsh, exposed conditions.

harvesting.text: Rhizome and root are dug from plants at least a few years old (traditionally 4-5 years), typically in autumn, then cleaned and dried. Wild populations grow slowly, so cultivated sources are increasingly preferred for sustainability.
harvesting.season: Autumn, from plants at least 4-5 years old

traditional_uses.text: Rhodiola has a long tradition of use across Scandinavian, Russian and Central Asian folk medicine as a tonic for cold-climate hardiness, physical endurance and mental stamina. It is classified as a modern 'adaptogen', and clinical trials support benefits for stress-related fatigue, mood and cognitive performance.

preparations.name: Standardised root extract (capsule)
preparations.text: Standardised to rosavins and salidroside (commonly the SHR-5 extract), the form used in most clinical trials.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root.

preparations.name: Decoction/tea
preparations.text: Traditional preparation, less concentrated than standardised extracts.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials have most often used 340-680 mg/day of standardised root extract (e.g. SHR-5, standardised to rosavins/salidroside), typically taken in the morning to avoid overstimulation. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Rosavins (rosavin, rosarin, rosin)
constituents.note: Characteristic phenylpropanoid glycosides largely unique to Rhodiola rosea, used as a standardisation marker for commercial extracts.

constituents.name: Salidroside and tyrosol
constituents.note: Phenylethanoid glycosides considered key adaptogenic and antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Phenylpropanoids and flavonoids
constituents.note: Contribute to the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity of the root.

actions.note: Physical performance / strength improvement

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated. May cause agitation, insomnia, or vivid dreams in sensitive individuals — take in the morning. Avoid in manic episodes or severe anxiety. Theoretical interactions with MAOIs, SSRIs, and CNS stimulants. Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry for Rhodiola rosea in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Rhodiola inhibits monoamine oxidase (MAO-A/B) and raises serotonin and dopamine activity; a case report described serotonergic-syndrome-type symptoms (restlessness, trembling) when rhodiola was added to the antidepressant paroxetine. Combined with antidepressants it may add to serotonergic effects.

pairings.note: Rhodiola and eleuthero (Siberian ginseng) are classic adaptogens combined in traditional and studied fixed formulas to build resistance to stress and fatigue; used together their stress-protective effects are complementary.
Rhodiola and schisandra are adaptogens co-formulated in clinically studied combinations for stress, fatigue and mental performance; combined use is intended to be synergistic rather than harmful.

botanical_description.text: Low evergreen shrub (Ericaceae), 30-100 cm tall. Leaves are narrow, leathery, with in-rolled margins and a dense, rust-coloured, woolly felt on the underside, strongly aromatic when crushed. Small white flowers are borne in terminal clusters.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, leathery, in-rolled margins, rust-coloured woolly felt beneath, aromatic
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, in terminal clusters
botanical_description.root: Fibrous, shallow root system adapted to boggy ground
botanical_description.fruit: Small dry capsule

habitat.text: Native to boreal and subarctic wetlands across northern Europe, Asia and North America, growing in acidic peat bogs, wet coniferous forest and tundra margins.

harvesting.text: Leaves and young stems are gathered, traditionally in late summer, and dried. Only small amounts are used given the plant's ledol/grayanotoxin content, and correct identification against toxic bog look-alikes (bog rosemary) is essential.
harvesting.season: Traditionally late summer

traditional_uses.text: Labrador tea has a long Indigenous North American and Scandinavian folk-medicine tradition as a mild diuretic and respiratory remedy, and as an aromatic tea and spice, always used in small, cautious amounts given its narcotic/toxic potential at higher doses. Modern phytochemical work confirms anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity of its essential oil.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped briefly in hot water, in small quantities only.

preparations.name: Essential oil (external)
preparations.text: Used topically or aromatically; not intended for concentrated internal use.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Duke (2002) advises using Labrador tea only with caution and at low doses given its narcotic/deliriant potential at higher amounts; it is not appropriate for regular internal use. Educational reference only, not a prescription - use only small, occasional amounts of properly identified leaf.

constituents.name: Essential oil (ledol, palustrol)
constituents.note: Ledol is the characteristic, mildly toxic sesquiterpene alcohol of the leaf, particularly concentrated in fresh material.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant and astringent activity.

constituents.name: Grayanotoxins (diterpenes)
constituents.note: Toxic diterpenes shared with related Ericaceae; the basis for the caution around excessive or prolonged use.

contraindications.note: Labrador tea contains ledol, a toxic sesquiterpene alcohol, particularly concentrated in fresh leaves. Excessive consumption can cause dizziness, headache, nausea, and even toxicity (especially in the related Ledum palustre). Use only in small, controlled amounts. Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding. Identify plant correctly — confusion with other bog plants can be dangerous.

Duke (2002) rates Labrador tea as + (caution warranted) and notes primarily folkloric activities, with the plant acting as a diuretic, expectorant, and vulnerary. Importantly, Duke classifies it as having narcotic and deliriant potential — large doses can cause intoxication, hallucinations, and toxicity. It contains ericolin and ledol, which are potentially neurotoxic compounds. Duke emphasizes that Labrador tea should be used only with caution and at low doses, and notes that it is not appropriate for regular internal use given its toxicity profile at higher amounts (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Narrow evergreen leaves gathered to brew marsh 'Labrador tea'; toxic bog rosemary shares the same bog habitat and similar narrow evergreen leaves.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Marsh Labrador tea (Rhododendron tomentosum) is brewed in small amounts as a traditional herbal tea. Bog rosemary (Andromeda polifolia) grows in the same acid bogs and has similar narrow, leathery, evergreen leaves; as bushcraft guidance (Paul Kirtley) warns, it is one of the poisonous bog plants that can be confused with Labrador tea. Bog rosemary contains grayanotoxins, which can cause vomiting, low blood pressure and dangerous slowing of the heart - grayanotoxin poisoning has caused complete heart block. Marsh Labrador tea is itself used only cautiously in tiny doses (it too contains grayanotoxin and ledol), so brewing the wrong bog shrub is a real hazard.
distinguishing_features: Leaf underside (decisive): marsh Labrador tea has leaves with inrolled margins and a dense rust-coloured woolly felt on the underside, and the crushed leaf is strongly aromatic. Bog rosemary leaves are smooth and pale or whitish underneath, with NO woolly felt and NO aroma.
Flowers: bog rosemary has pink, urn-shaped (bell-like) nodding flowers, whereas marsh Labrador tea has clusters of small white flowers.
General rule: a bog shrub with narrow evergreen leaves that are smooth and pale beneath and have no smell is NOT Labrador tea.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Turn a leaf over and smell it. Marsh Labrador tea has a rusty, woolly felt on the underside of the leaf and a strong aromatic scent. If the underside is smooth and pale with no woolly felt and the leaf has no aroma - especially if the flowers are pink and bell-shaped - it is bog rosemary (or a laurel), which is toxic; do not brew it.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous shrub (Grossulariaceae), 1-2 m tall, aromatic when the leaves are crushed. Leaves are palmately lobed (3-5 lobes), toothed, with resinous glands on the underside. Small, greenish-white to dull purple, bell-shaped flowers are borne in drooping racemes, followed by clusters of glossy black berries.
botanical_description.leaves: Palmately lobed (3-5 lobes), toothed, resin-glanded beneath, aromatic
botanical_description.flowers: Small, greenish-white to dull purple, bell-shaped, in drooping racemes
botanical_description.fruit: Clusters of glossy black berries

habitat.text: Native to central and northern Europe and Siberia, growing in damp woodland, fens, riverbanks and hedgerows; widely cultivated commercially as a fruit crop in cool-temperate climates.

harvesting.text: Berries are hand- or machine-harvested in mid- to late summer once fully ripe and black. Leaves are gathered in late spring to early summer, before flowering fades, for the medicinal leaf preparation. Seed oil is cold-pressed from the seeds after juicing.
harvesting.season: Berries mid-late summer; leaf late spring-early summer

traditional_uses.text: Blackcurrant has a long northern-European food-medicine tradition: the vitamin-C- and anthocyanin-rich berries have traditionally been used for colds, flu and general vitality, while the leaf has an official EU traditional-use registration for minor joint pain and as a urinary-tract flush, and the seed oil is valued as a source of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA). Modern research confirms broad antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory and vasoprotective activity across the fruit, leaf and seed oil.

preparations.name: Fresh or dried berries
preparations.text: Eaten fresh or dried, or made into juice, jam or syrup.

preparations.name: Leaf infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water - the traditional joint-comfort/urinary-flush preparation.

preparations.name: Seed oil (capsule)
preparations.text: Cold-pressed, GLA-rich oil, taken as a supplement.

preparations.name: Standardised fruit extract
preparations.text: Concentrated anthocyanin extract used in some clinical research.

dosage.name: Leaf infusion
dosage.text: EMA-style traditional-use guidance for the leaf suggests roughly 2-4 g dried leaf per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily, for short-term joint or urinary support (not recommended under 18, or with fluid retention linked to heart/kidney disease). Berries and juice are food-safe at normal dietary amounts. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Anthocyanins (delphinidin, cyanidin glycosides)
constituents.note: Give the berries their deep colour and are the principal antioxidant constituents.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and polyphenols
constituents.note: The fruit is exceptionally rich in vitamin C, contributing to its traditional cold/flu use.

constituents.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA, seed oil)
constituents.note: The seed oil is a well-known plant source of the omega-6 fatty acid GLA.

contraindications.note: Fruit (berries) • Generally safe as food for most people; adverse effects mainly GI discomfort or allergy in sensitive individuals. • If using high-dose extracts, use extra caution with blood-thinners/anticoagulants (evidence is not definitive, but polyphenol-rich supplements are often treated cautiously in practice). Leaf (folium) • EMA classifies blackcurrant leaf as a traditional herbal medicinal product (not “well-established use”): minor joint pain and urinary-tract flushing. • Typical label cautions (EMA-style): not recommended <18, avoid if you have edema due to impaired heart/kidney function, and seek medical advice if urinary symptoms persist/worsen. • Pregnancy/lactation: food use is fine, but medicinal leaf dosing is generally used cautiously due to limited robust safety data. Seed (seed oil) • Usually well tolerated, but may cause GI upset in some people. • Caution with anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs (PUFA supplements are often used cautiously here). • Note: some trials explored maternal/infant contexts, but that does not automatically mean “recommended in pregnancy” outside medical supervision.

Duke (2002) rates blackcurrant fruit highly (+++), with evidence for anti-inflammatory, angioprotective, and antioxidant activities. Ribes nigrum extracts showed the richest anthocyanin and polyphenol content in antioxidant studies, outperforming many other common berries. Clinical use is supported for diarrhea, colds, and flu. The high vitamin C and anthocyanin content underpin its vasoprotective effects. The fruit is treated as food-grade medicine and is classified as generally safe (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Scrambling, thorny deciduous shrub (Rosaceae), 1-5 m, with arching, prickly stems bearing hooked thorns. Leaves are pinnate with 5-7 oval, toothed leaflets. Pale pink to white, five-petalled flowers are followed by bright red-orange, egg-shaped hips (the fleshy hip is an accessory fruit; the true fruits are small hairy achenes inside it) that ripen in autumn and often persist into winter.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnate, 5-7 oval, toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Pale pink to white, five-petalled
botanical_description.fruit: Bright red-orange, egg-shaped hip (accessory fruit) containing small hairy achenes

habitat.text: Native throughout Europe, north-west Africa and western Asia, common in hedgerows, woodland edges, scrub and roadside verges on a wide range of soils.

harvesting.text: Hips are picked in autumn once fully coloured, then dried for tea or powder, or processed fresh for syrup. The fine, irritant hairs surrounding the seeds inside the hip must be removed before consumption.
harvesting.season: Autumn

traditional_uses.text: Rosehip has a long European folk tradition as a vitamin-C-rich tonic for colds and general immunity, and as a mild diuretic and astringent for urinary and digestive complaints. Modern clinical trials on standardised rosehip powder show a modest, reproducible benefit for osteoarthritis pain and stiffness.

preparations.name: Rosehip powder (standardised, seedless)
preparations.text: The form used in osteoarthritis clinical trials.

preparations.name: Infusion/tea
preparations.text: Dried, deseeded hip steeped in hot water - a traditional vitamin C tonic.

preparations.name: Syrup
preparations.text: Traditional preparation, historically used as a vitamin C supplement, particularly for children.

preparations.name: Petals
preparations.text: Occasionally used for their fragrance in traditional preparations.

dosage.name: Rosehip powder
dosage.text: Clinical trials for osteoarthritis have most often used around 5 g/day of standardised rosehip powder. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and carotenoids
constituents.note: The hip is exceptionally rich in vitamin C and carotenoids, underpinning its traditional tonic use.

constituents.name: Galactolipid (GOPO)
constituents.note: A specific galactolipid fraction studied as the likely active anti-inflammatory constituent in osteoarthritis trials of standardised rosehip powder.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant activity.

contraindications.note: Rosa canina (dog rose) hips are generally recognized as safe (GRAS) for consumption, widely used in teas, jams, and supplements. They are highly nutritious but may cause minor digestive upset (nausea, diarrhea) in some users. The small, irritating hairs around the seeds should be removed before consumption.

Duke (2002) rates dog rose hips as +++ with clinical evidence (score 2) for astringent activity and experimental support for antioxidant and diuretic effects. Dog rose hips are exceptionally rich in vitamin C and bioflavonoids. They are traditionally used for cold and influenza prevention, urinary tract health, and mild diarrhea. Duke notes the diuretic (score 1) and lipoxygenase-inhibitory activities as experimentally documented. The fruit is considered food-grade safe (Duke, 2002).

botanical_description.text: Dense, thicket-forming deciduous shrub (Rosaceae), 1-1.5 m tall (occasionally to 2 m), with stems densely covered in numerous straight, bristly thorns. Leaves are pinnate with 5-9 deeply veined, glossy, leathery leaflets. Large, fragrant, deep pink to white, five-petalled flowers are followed by large, tomato-red, flattened-globose hips.
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnate, 5-9 deeply veined, glossy, leathery leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: Large, fragrant, deep pink to white, five-petalled
botanical_description.stem: Densely covered in numerous straight, bristly thorns
botanical_description.fruit: Large, tomato-red, flattened-globose hips

habitat.text: Native to coastal eastern Asia (Japan, Korea, China, far-eastern Russia), typically growing on sand dunes and coastal scrub. Widely planted and naturalised as an ornamental and hedging shrub, notably tolerant of salt spray, wind and poor sandy soils, in temperate coastal regions worldwide.

harvesting.text: Flowers/petals are picked as they open in summer; hips are picked in autumn once fully coloured and slightly softened.
harvesting.season: Flower in summer; hips in autumn

traditional_uses.text: Rosa rugosa has a long East Asian tradition - particularly in Traditional Chinese Medicine, where the flower is known as 'Mei Gui Hua' - as a mood-regulating, digestive and menstrual-cycle-supporting remedy, and the vitamin-C-rich hips are used as a food tonic. Modern research on its flavonoid- and polysaccharide-rich extracts supports broad antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory activity.

preparations.name: Flower tea/infusion
preparations.text: Dried petals or flower buds steeped in hot water - the classic Traditional Chinese Medicine preparation.

preparations.name: Hip tea/syrup
preparations.text: Dried or fresh hips prepared as tea or syrup, a vitamin C tonic.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Concentrated flavonoid/polysaccharide extract used in research.

dosage.name: Flower infusion
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests roughly 3-6 g dried flower per cup as an infusion, or hip tea/syrup at similar strength, taken once or twice daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol glycosides)
constituents.note: The dominant bioactive class, driving much of the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Polysaccharides
constituents.note: Studied for immunomodulatory, hepatoprotective and gut-microbiota-modulating effects.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and carotenoids (hips)
constituents.note: Concentrated in the hips, underpinning their traditional food-tonic use.

constituents.name: Oleamide and other root compounds
constituents.note: Isolated from the root and studied for antioxidant/neuroprotective activity.

contraindications.note: Generally safe as a food plant. Rose hips should be used after removing the achenes (seeds and inner hairs), which can cause irritation. Allergic reactions are rare. No significant drug interactions documented.
Duke (2002) does not include a dedicated entry for Japanese rose (Rosa rugosa) in the Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, Second Edition.

constituents.name: Tannins (gallotannins and ellagitannins)
constituents.note: The astringent principles of the leaf.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids (including gallic acid)
constituents.note: Antioxidant and antibacterial constituents.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and minerals (iron)
constituents.note: Minor nutritive constituents of the leaf.

botanical_description.text: Scrambling, thorny deciduous shrub (Rosaceae) forming dense thickets, with arching prickly stems that root at the tip. Leaves are palmately compound, usually with 3-5 toothed leaflets. White to pale pink, five-petalled flowers are followed by clusters of small drupelets forming the blackberry fruit, ripening from red to glossy black.
botanical_description.leaves: Palmately compound, usually 3-5 toothed leaflets
botanical_description.flowers: White to pale pink, five-petalled
botanical_description.stem: Arching, prickly, rooting at the tip
botanical_description.fruit: Cluster of drupelets, ripening red to glossy black

habitat.text: Native throughout Europe and widely naturalised elsewhere, an aggressive coloniser of hedgerows, woodland edges, scrub and waste ground. 'Rubus fruticosus' is a taxonomically complex aggregate comprising hundreds of closely related microspecies.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked fresh throughout the growing season and dried for tea; berries are picked in late summer once fully ripe.
harvesting.season: Leaf throughout the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Blackberry leaf has a long European folk-medicine tradition as an astringent gargle for sore throat and mouth ulcers, and as a remedy for mild diarrhoea, reflecting its high tannin content; the berries themselves are valued as a nutritious, antioxidant-rich food.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water, taken internally or used as a gargle/mouthwash.

preparations.name: Gargle/mouthwash
preparations.text: A stronger leaf infusion used topically for sore throat and mouth irritation.

preparations.name: Fresh or dried berries
preparations.text: Eaten as food, a rich source of antioxidants.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Traditional guidance suggests roughly 2-4 g dried leaf per cup as an infusion, up to three times daily, or used as a gargle at similar strength. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and oral mucosa inflammation
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)

indications.note: Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
indications.label: Diarrhoea

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and oral mucosa inflammation
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and oral mucosa inflammation
indications.label: Oral & throat health

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and oral mucosa inflammation
indications.label: Sore throat

contraindications.note: Tannin-rich: high or prolonged intake may cause stomach upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.
Traditional use only; diarrhoea that persists (especially in children) needs medical assessment and attention to rehydration.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Astringent constituents of the leaf.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and the alkaloid fragarine
constituents.note: Fragarine is the constituent proposed to give the uterine-toning effect.

constituents.name: Vitamin C and minerals
constituents.note: Nutritive constituents.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous, thicket-forming shrub (Rosaceae), 1-2 m tall, with upright, prickly, biennial canes (vegetative in the first year, fruiting in the second). Leaves are pinnate with 3-7 toothed leaflets, whitish-felted beneath. Small white, five-petalled flowers are followed by the aggregate red raspberry fruit, a cluster of drupelets.
botanical_description.habit: Deciduous, thicket-forming shrub with biennial canes
botanical_description.leaves: Pinnate, 3-7 toothed leaflets, whitish-felted beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Small, white, five-petalled
botanical_description.fruit: Aggregate red raspberry (cluster of drupelets)

habitat.text: Native to Europe and northern Asia, widely cultivated as a fruit crop and naturalised on woodland edges, hedgerows and disturbed ground in temperate regions.

harvesting.text: Leaves are picked fresh during the growing season - before or during flowering is considered optimal - and dried; fruit is picked when fully ripe in summer.
harvesting.season: Leaf before/during flowering; fruit in summer

traditional_uses.text: Raspberry leaf has a long, very widely known folk tradition as a 'pregnancy tea', taken in the final weeks of pregnancy as a uterine tonic to prepare for labour. Observational studies and a small randomised controlled trial suggest a possible modest effect on labour duration, though robust proof is still lacking.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water - the classic late-pregnancy preparation.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract, less commonly used than the tea.

preparations.name: Standardised leaf extract
preparations.text: Used in some observational and clinical research.

dosage.name: Infusion (tea)
dosage.text: Popular guidance and observational studies generally describe starting raspberry-leaf tea or tablets in the last few weeks of pregnancy, at roughly 1-3 cups of tea daily; use should be discussed with a midwife or doctor given the lack of robust trial evidence. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins)
Astringent for menstrual cramps (traditional); Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins)

Traditional uterine tonic taken in late pregnancy to tone the uterus and support labour - one small double-blind RCT found a shorter second stage of labour and fewer forceps deliveries (no effect on the first stage), alongside observational signals; robust proof is still lacking

contraindications.note: Despite widespread popular use, there is no robust (randomised-trial) evidence of efficacy or safety. Use in pregnancy should be discussed with a midwife or doctor and is generally reserved for the last few weeks rather than early pregnancy.

botanical_description.text: Perennial herb (Polygonaceae), 30-100 cm tall, with erect, often reddish, ridged flowering stems. Basal leaves are arrow- (hastate-) shaped with backward-pointing basal lobes, and sour-tasting from oxalic acid content. Tiny reddish-brown flowers are borne in narrow, branched spikes; the species is dioecious, with separate male and female plants.
botanical_description.leaves: Arrow- (hastate-) shaped with backward-pointing basal lobes, sour-tasting
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, reddish-brown, in narrow branched spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Small three-angled achenes

habitat.text: Native throughout Europe and temperate Asia, common in meadows, grassland, pastures and roadside verges on a wide range of soils, tolerating both damp and dry ground.

harvesting.text: Leaves are gathered fresh in spring and early summer, before flowering, when they are most tender and least bitter. Avoid harvesting near roads or industrial areas, given the plant's tendency to accumulate heavy metals.
harvesting.season: Spring to early summer, before flowering

traditional_uses.text: Common sorrel has a long European culinary-medicinal tradition as a refreshing, vitamin-C-rich spring green and digestive bitter, and folk-medicinally as a diuretic and mild antiscorbutic (anti-scurvy) remedy. Modern research confirms antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity of its leaf extracts.

preparations.name: Fresh leaf (culinary)
preparations.text: Eaten raw in salads or cooked into soups - the traditional spring tonic use.

preparations.name: Infusion (tea)
preparations.text: Dried leaf steeped in hot water, a traditional digestive/diuretic preparation.

preparations.name: Juice
preparations.text: Fresh leaf juice, traditionally used in small amounts.

dosage.name: Fresh leaf / infusion
dosage.text: Sorrel is best treated as an occasional food rather than a daily medicinal herb, given its oxalate content; traditional infusions use roughly 2-4 g dried leaf per cup, taken occasionally rather than regularly. Educational reference only, not a prescription; avoid large or frequent amounts if prone to kidney stones or gout.

constituents.name: Oxalic acid and oxalates
constituents.note: Gives the leaf its characteristic sour taste; the basis for the caution around kidney stones and mineral absorption at high intakes.

constituents.name: Anthraquinones
constituents.note: Minor constituents typical of the Rumex genus, studied alongside other isolated compounds for antibacterial activity against Helicobacter pylori.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant and mild astringent activity.

constituents.name: Vitamin C
constituents.note: The basis for the plant's traditional antiscorbutic (anti-scurvy) reputation.

contraindications.note: High in oxalates (the sour taste): Large, frequent amounts can be a bad idea if you’re prone to kidney or bladder stones, or certain “rheumatic-type” complaints (Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, n.d.). Moderation matters: Measured oxalate levels in leaves can be high, and researchers recommend treating sorrel more like an occasional delicacy than a daily staple (Tuazon-Nartea & Savage, 2013). Mineral binding: Oxalic acid can reduce how much calcium you absorb from a meal (Tuazon-Nartea & Savage, 2013). Sensitive stomach/mouth: Very sour leaves can irritate some people (common-sense caution; strongest source is the oxalate discussion above). Pregnancy/lactation: As a normal food herb in culinary amounts is generally considered fine, but avoid high-dose “medicinal” use because safety data are limited (Bello et al., 2019). Foraging safety (important): Sorrel can accumulate heavy metals depending on where it grows; avoid harvesting near roads/industrial areas (Gawęda, 2009).

Duke (2002) rates sorrel as ++ and notes diuretic, antiscorbutic (high vitamin C), and depurative activities at experimental and folkloric levels. The plant is traditionally used for anemia, fever, and scurvy prevention. High oxalic acid content is an important consideration — excessive consumption can promote kidney stone formation (oxalate stones) and may be harmful in individuals with gout or existing kidney disease. Duke advises limiting medicinal use and avoiding in those with a history of calcium oxalate kidney stones (Duke, 2002).

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Young arrow-shaped spring leaves gathered as a tangy salad or pot herb; lords-and-ladies pushes up similar arrow-shaped leaves in the same season.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Common sorrel (Rumex acetosa) is foraged in spring for its lemony arrow-shaped leaves. Lords-and-ladies (Arum maculatum, cuckoo-pint) unfurls similar arrow- to heart-shaped leaves at the same time and, as the Foraging Course Company notes, can be confused with common sorrel. Arum is one of the commonest causes of plant-poisoning hospital visits: every part contains sharp calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense burning and swelling of the mouth and throat, and severe cases can cause life-threatening throat swelling. Because young sorrel and young Arum both appear as arrow-shaped leaves in spring, this is a genuine hazard.
distinguishing_features: Flower (decisive when present): lords-and-ladies has an unmistakable hooded flower - a pale green-and-purple cowl (spathe) wrapped around a finger-like spadix. Sorrel has tall slender stems of tiny reddish-brown flowers. A hooded cowl means Arum, never sorrel.

Leaf lobes and veins: sorrel leaves have sharply pointed basal lobes that angle backwards and simple veins; Arum leaves are broader and glossy with rounded basal lobes, a netted (reticulate) vein pattern, and are often marked with dark purple-black blotches.

Taste (sorrel only - never taste a suspected Arum): true sorrel is soft and pleasantly lemony-sour, whereas Arum is acrid and instantly burns the lips. If any burning or tingling starts, spit it out at once. Do not taste-test unless the flower or leaves have already confirmed sorrel.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Look for the flower and leaf detail. A hooded green-purple cowl (spathe), glossy dark leaves with rounded lobes, netted veins and dark blotches = lords-and-ladies (Arum) - do not eat; it burns the mouth and throat. Slim reddish flower spikes with matt leaves, sharp backward-pointing lobes and a pleasant lemony taste = sorrel. If in any doubt, do not eat it.

constituents.name: Steroidal saponins (spirostanol and furostanol)
constituents.note: Aglycones ruscogenin and neoruscogenin carry the vasoconstrictive and venotonic activity.

constituents.name: Phytosterols and flavonoids
constituents.note: Supporting constituents of the rhizome.

botanical_description.text: Evergreen, rhizomatous subshrub (Asparagaceae), 30-80 cm tall. Unusually, its apparent 'leaves' are flattened, leaf-like stems (cladodes) - stiff, dark green, ending in a sharp spine; the true leaves are reduced to tiny scales. Small greenish-white flowers arise from the centre of the cladode, followed on female plants by a bright red berry.
botanical_description.leaves: True leaves reduced to tiny scales; photosynthesis occurs via leaf-like cladodes
botanical_description.flowers: Small, greenish-white, borne from the centre of the cladode
botanical_description.stem: Stiff, upright, with flattened spine-tipped cladodes
botanical_description.fruit: Bright red berry (on female plants)

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean region and western Europe, growing in dry woodland, scrub and rocky ground, often in shade under trees.

harvesting.text: The underground rhizome, the medicinal part, is dug in autumn or spring from established plants.
harvesting.season: Autumn or spring

traditional_uses.text: Butcher's broom takes its name from the traditional use of its stiff, bristly stems bundled together as a broom by butchers. Medicinally its rhizome has a long European tradition, and modern use, supported by clinical trials, is for chronic venous insufficiency (heavy, tired, swollen legs, varicose veins) and haemorrhoids, reflecting its steroidal-saponin-driven venotonic and vasoconstrictive activity.

preparations.name: Standardised extract (capsule/tablet)
preparations.text: The clinically studied commercial form, often combined with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C.

preparations.name: Topical cream/gel
preparations.text: Rhizome extract applied to affected areas for haemorrhoid symptoms.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Traditional preparation of the dried rhizome, less commonly used than standardised extracts.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for venous insufficiency have most often used standardised rhizome extract in combination products (e.g. with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C), typically providing the equivalent of roughly 72-150 mg of ruscogenins daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-oedematous (reduces leg swelling)

Venotonic / vasoconstrictive support for chronic venous insufficiency (heavy, tired, swollen legs and varicose veins) - a meta-analysis of a Ruscus-based combination (with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C) significantly reduced pain, cramps, heaviness and paraesthesia versus placebo

indications.note: Venotonic / vasoconstrictive support for chronic venous insufficiency (heavy, tired, swollen legs and varicose veins) - a meta-analysis of a Ruscus-based combination (with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C) significantly reduced pain, cramps, heaviness and paraesthesia versus placebo
indications.label: Menstrual cramps

indications.note: Venotonic / vasoconstrictive support for chronic venous insufficiency (heavy, tired, swollen legs and varicose veins) - a meta-analysis of a Ruscus-based combination (with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C) significantly reduced pain, cramps, heaviness and paraesthesia versus placebo
indications.label: Pain (general)

indications.note: Venotonic / vasoconstrictive support for chronic venous insufficiency (heavy, tired, swollen legs and varicose veins) - a meta-analysis of a Ruscus-based combination (with hesperidin methyl chalcone and vitamin C) significantly reduced pain, cramps, heaviness and paraesthesia versus placebo
indications.label: Varicose veins

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; occasional mild gastrointestinal upset is the main reported effect.
Because it is vasoconstrictive, use with caution if you have high blood pressure or take circulatory medicines; safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

constituents.name: Salicin and related salicylates
constituents.note: Metabolised in the body to salicylic acid - the 'natural aspirin' that gives the analgesic, anti-inflammatory and antipyretic effects.

constituents.name: Polyphenols, flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute additionally to the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity.

botanical_description.text: Large deciduous tree (Salicaceae), 10-25 m tall (occasionally taller), fast-growing with a broad crown. Leaves are narrow and lance-shaped, silvery-white and silky-hairy beneath, giving the tree a shimmering appearance in the wind. Tiny flowers are borne in catkins appearing with the leaves in spring; the species is dioecious. The fruit is a small capsule releasing cottony-tufted seeds.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, silvery-white and silky-hairy beneath
botanical_description.flowers: Tiny, in catkins with the leaves
botanical_description.fruit: Small capsule with cottony-tufted seeds

habitat.text: Native to Europe, western and central Asia and North Africa, growing on damp ground along riverbanks, floodplains and wet meadows; widely planted and naturalised elsewhere.

harvesting.text: Bark is stripped from young branches (2-3-year-old growth is preferred, as salicin content is highest in younger bark) in spring, when the rising sap makes the bark peel most easily, then dried.
harvesting.season: Spring

traditional_uses.text: Willow bark has one of the longest documented histories of any medicinal plant, used since antiquity (recorded by Hippocrates) as a remedy for pain and fever. Its salicin content, chemically related to aspirin, gives it official European herbal-monograph recognition for mild joint pain, headache and feverish colds, with modern clinical trials supporting benefit for low back pain.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried bark simmered in water - the traditional preparation, since salicin needs proper simmering (not just a quick infusion) to extract well.

preparations.name: Standardised extract (capsule/tablet)
preparations.text: Standardised to salicin content, the form used in clinical trials.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried bark.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for back pain have used around 240 mg salicin/day of standardised extract. Educational reference only, not a prescription; avoid if allergic to aspirin/salicylates, in children/teenagers with a viral illness (Reye's syndrome risk), or alongside anticoagulants.

actions.note: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory for low back pain (moderate evidence; dose-dependent, around 240 mg salicin/day) - in an RCT, 240 mg salicin/day left 39% pain-free vs 6% on placebo within 4 weeks
Analgesic and anti-inflammatory for low back pain (moderate evidence; dose-dependent, around 240 mg salicin/day) - in an RCT, 240 mg salicin/day left 39% pain-free vs 6% on placebo within 4 weeks
Antipyretic (reduces fever)

indications.note: Relief of joint pain, osteoarthritis and rheumatic complaints
indications.label: Arthritis / joint pain

indications.note: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory for low back pain (moderate evidence; dose-dependent, around 240 mg salicin/day) - in an RCT, 240 mg salicin/day left 39% pain-free vs 6% on placebo within 4 weeks
indications.label: Back pain

indications.note: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory for low back pain (moderate evidence; dose-dependent, around 240 mg salicin/day) - in an RCT, 240 mg salicin/day left 39% pain-free vs 6% on placebo within 4 weeks; Relief of joint pain, osteoarthritis and rheumatic complaints
indications.label: Pain (general)

contraindications.note: Because it works like aspirin, avoid willow bark if you are allergic to aspirin or salicylates, and do NOT give it to children or teenagers with a viral illness (risk of Reye's syndrome). Use caution in asthma (salicylate sensitivity).
