Anti-inflammatory and analgesic
Nervine / anxiolytic for nervous tension and mild anxiety; supports sleep (traditional) - a crude extract showed anxiolytic, sedative and anticonvulsant activity in animal models
Mild mood support / antidepressant (traditional)
Nervine / anxiolytic for nervous tension and mild anxiety; supports sleep (traditional) - a crude extract showed anxiolytic, sedative and anticonvulsant activity in animal models

May add to the effect of sedatives; use in breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of data.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Vervain is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

botanical_description.text: Low, creeping, mat-forming perennial herb with softly hairy, oval, finely toothed leaves along trailing stems that root as they spread. Small, pale lilac-blue flowers with darker veining are borne in slender upright spikes rising from the leaf axils.

traditional_uses.text: Speedwell has a long European folk history as a mild digestive tonic and traditional expectorant for cough and respiratory complaints, and has also been used topically as a soothing wash for irritated skin; modern research on its iridoid glycosides supports a molecular basis for the traditional respiratory use.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous shrub with maple-like, three-lobed, toothed leaves that turn red in autumn. Flat-topped clusters of white flowers are borne in late spring, the outer ring of larger, showy sterile flowers surrounding smaller fertile ones, followed by translucent red berries in autumn.

traditional_uses.text: Cramp bark has a long European and North American folk reputation, reflected in its common name, as an antispasmodic remedy for muscle cramps, menstrual cramps and general smooth-muscle spasm, acting as a relaxant on both skeletal and uterine muscle.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Bright red autumn berries gathered for food; woody nightshade bears similar shiny red berries in the same hedgerows.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Guelder rose (Viburnum opulus) berries are gathered (and cooked) for jellies and sauces. As Totally Wild UK warns, when picking them you must make sure the berries come from the guelder rose shrub itself and not from any scrambling vine with red berries, such as woody nightshade (Solanum dulcamara), which is highly toxic and often grows through the same hedges. Woody nightshade berries contain solanine-type glycoalkaloids and have poisoned children. Because both bear shiny red berries in autumn, checking the plant they hang on is essential.
distinguishing_features: Plant form (decisive): guelder rose is an upright shrub with maple-like three-lobed leaves; its berries hang in flat, rounded clusters. Woody nightshade is a floppy scrambling vine with arrow-shaped/lobed leaves and drooping loose clusters of berries.
Flowers/earlier signs: guelder rose has flat white flower heads (with showy outer flowers); woody nightshade has purple star-shaped flowers with a yellow central cone.
Berry shape: guelder rose berries are round and translucent-red; woody nightshade berries are more egg-shaped and hang from a scrambling stem, not a woody shrub.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Check the plant the berries hang on. Round translucent-red berries in flat clusters on an upright shrub with maple-like lobed leaves = guelder rose. Berries on a floppy scrambling vine with arrow-shaped leaves (and purple star flowers earlier in the year) = woody nightshade - toxic, do not pick them.

constituents.name: Mucilage and saponins
constituents.note: Mucilage gives the soothing demulcent action; saponins give the expectorant action.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and salicylic acid
constituents.note: Antioxidant and mild analgesic constituents.

constituents.name: Alkaloids (e.g. violine) and essential oil
constituents.note: Minor aromatic and bioactive constituents.

botanical_description.text: Low, spreading perennial herb with heart-shaped, softly toothed leaves arising from a short rootstock, spreading by runners (stolons). Fragrant, deep violet-purple (occasionally white) flowers with five petals, the lower petal spurred, are borne singly on slender stalks.
botanical_description.habit: Low, spreading perennial herb, spreading by runners
botanical_description.leaves: Heart-shaped, softly toothed
botanical_description.flowers: Fragrant, deep violet-purple (occasionally white), five-petalled, the lower petal spurred, borne singly
botanical_description.stem: Very short; leaves and flowers arise from a basal rootstock, spreading by stolons
botanical_description.fruit: Small three-valved capsule
botanical_description.flowering_period: February-April (with a lighter second flush in autumn)

habitat.text: Grows in woodland edges, hedgerows, shaded banks and gardens on moist, humus-rich soils; native to Europe, North Africa and Asia and widely naturalised and cultivated elsewhere.

harvesting.text: Flowers are picked as they open in early spring; leaves can be gathered through the growing season and dried.
harvesting.season: Flower in early spring; leaf through the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Sweet violet flower and leaf have a long European folk history as a soothing demulcent and expectorant remedy for cough, bronchitis and catarrh, and as a mild sedative for sleeplessness and headache; the flower syrup in particular has a long tradition as a children's cough remedy.

preparations.name: Syrup (flower)
preparations.text: Fresh flowers infused and combined with sugar syrup as a traditional soothing cough remedy, particularly for children.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flower or leaf infused in hot water as a traditional demulcent, calming tea.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried flower and leaf.

dosage.name: Infusion/syrup
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-4 g of dried flower and leaf per cup, up to three times daily; syrup dosed per product labelling. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and catarrh (mucilage soothes; saponins loosen mucus) - a double-blind RCT found violet-flower syrup, added to standard treatment, sped cough suppression in children with asthma

Demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and catarrh (mucilage soothes; saponins loosen mucus) - a double-blind RCT found violet-flower syrup, added to standard treatment, sped cough suppression in children with asthma
Mild sedative for sleeplessness and to ease headache (traditional)

indications.note: Demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and catarrh (mucilage soothes; saponins loosen mucus) - a double-blind RCT found violet-flower syrup, added to standard treatment, sped cough suppression in children with asthma
indications.label: Asthma

indications.note: Demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and catarrh (mucilage soothes; saponins loosen mucus) - a double-blind RCT found violet-flower syrup, added to standard treatment, sped cough suppression in children with asthma
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and catarrh (mucilage soothes; saponins loosen mucus) - a double-blind RCT found violet-flower syrup, added to standard treatment, sped cough suppression in children with asthma
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Mild sedative for sleeplessness and to ease headache (traditional)
indications.label: Headache

indications.note: Mild sedative for sleeplessness and to ease headache (traditional)
indications.label: Insomnia / sleeplessness

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated as flower/leaf preparations. It contains salicylates, so use caution if you are salicylate-sensitive; the root in large doses is emetic and purgative.
Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Sweet violet is traditionally used as a calming, sedative herb; taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants (including alcohol) it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.

dangerous_lookalikes.confused_part: Low, heart-shaped spring leaves gathered as an edible; lesser celandine forms similar low leaves at the same time and place.
dangerous_lookalikes.confusion_context: Sweet violet (Viola odorata) is foraged in early spring for its heart-shaped leaves and flowers. Lesser celandine (Ficaria verna, fig buttercup) grows in the same season and habitat and, as foraging guidance warns, its low-growing leaves closely resemble violet; lesser celandine is toxic to humans in most stages (it contains the acrid irritant protoanemonin). Because their spring leaves are alike, this is a genuine early-season foraging hazard, although cooking destroys the toxin and the acrid taste discourages eating much.
distinguishing_features: Flowers (decisive): sweet violet has purple (sometimes white) flowers with five uneven petals. Lesser celandine has glossy, bright-yellow star-like flowers with 8-12 petals. A yellow many-petalled flower means lesser celandine, never violet.
Leaves: violet leaves are matt and heart-shaped on their own stalks from the base. Lesser celandine leaves are glossier and more kidney- to arrow-shaped, sometimes with pale mottling.
Best practice: harvest violet leaves only once you can trace every leaf to a violet plant (ideally in flower), not to a yellow-flowered plant.
dangerous_lookalikes.key_test: Wait until the plant flowers, or trace each leaf to its flower. Purple five-petalled flowers with matt heart-shaped leaves = sweet violet. Glossy yellow flowers with 8-12 petals = lesser celandine - do not eat it raw. If you cannot confirm a violet flower, do not gather the leaves.

constituents.name: Cyclotides (cyclic plant peptides)
constituents.note: Bioactive cyclic peptides shown to suppress over-active immune (T-cell) responses - the rationale for use in inflammatory skin disease; the cytotoxic cyclotides vitri A, varv A and varv E have been isolated from V. tricolor. Cyclotide-enriched preparations (including hot-water decoctions) reduce T-cell proliferation and macrophage cytokine output in vitro, of interest for inflammatory bowel disease.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (rutin, violanthin), salicylates and mucilage
constituents.note: Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and soothing constituents.

botanical_description.text: Small, low-growing annual or short-lived perennial herb with oval to lance-shaped, scalloped leaves and large, leaf-like stipules. The distinctive flowers are tricoloured - purple, yellow and white in varying combinations - with five petals, the lower petal often marked with dark veining (a 'face' pattern), giving rise to the name 'heartsease'.
botanical_description.habit: Small, low-growing annual or short-lived perennial herb
botanical_description.leaves: Oval to lance-shaped, scalloped, with large leaf-like stipules
botanical_description.flowers: Tricoloured (purple, yellow, white), five-petalled, often with dark facial veining on the lower petal
botanical_description.fruit: Small three-valved capsule

habitat.text: Grows in grassland, cultivated ground, gardens and waste places on a wide range of soils; native to Europe and widely naturalised elsewhere as a common garden and wild plant.

harvesting.text: The flowering aerial parts are cut in summer while in flower and dried in a warm, shaded, airy place.
harvesting.season: Summer, at flowering

traditional_uses.text: Heartsease has a long European folk history as a soothing remedy for skin conditions such as eczema and itching, and as a traditional demulcent and expectorant for coughs and chest complaints; this traditional skin-supportive use is now linked to immunomodulatory cyclic peptides (cyclotides) unique to the Viola genus.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried flowering herb infused in hot water, taken internally for cough or used as a cooled compress for skin irritation.

preparations.name: Cream/ointment
preparations.text: Extract formulated into a topical cream or ointment for eczema and irritated skin.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried flowering herb.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 1-2 g of dried herb per cup, up to three times daily. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Anti-inflammatory / immunomodulatory for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, itching and scabs (traditional, supported by cyclotide research) - cyclotide-enriched V. tricolor preparations suppress T-cell proliferation and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion in vitro; Soothes irritated skin (demulcent and anti-inflammatory)
Cytotoxic / antiproliferative against cancer cell lines in vitro (cyclotides vitri A, varv A and varv E)
Soothes irritated skin (demulcent and anti-inflammatory); Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and chest inflammation
Soothes irritated skin (demulcent and anti-inflammatory)
Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and chest inflammation

Anti-inflammatory / immunomodulatory for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, itching and scabs (traditional, supported by cyclotide research) - cyclotide-enriched V. tricolor preparations suppress T-cell proliferation and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion in vitro

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and chest inflammation
indications.label: Bronchitis

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and chest inflammation
indications.label: Cough

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory / immunomodulatory for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, itching and scabs (traditional, supported by cyclotide research) - cyclotide-enriched V. tricolor preparations suppress T-cell proliferation and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion in vitro
indications.label: Eczema

indications.note: Traditional demulcent / expectorant for cough, bronchitis and chest inflammation
indications.label: Inflammation (general)

indications.note: Anti-inflammatory / immunomodulatory for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, itching and scabs (traditional, supported by cyclotide research) - cyclotide-enriched V. tricolor preparations suppress T-cell proliferation and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion in vitro
indications.label: Psoriasis

contraindications.note: Regarded as safe in customary traditional use; because the extract suppresses immune-cell activity, there is a theoretical caution when taking immunosuppressant medicines.
Contains salicylates, so use caution if you are salicylate-sensitive; safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established.

constituents.name: Diterpenes (e.g. rotundifuran, clerodadienols)
constituents.note: Bind dopamine D2 receptors in the pituitary, reducing prolactin release - the key mechanism.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (casticin, vitexin)
constituents.note: Antioxidant constituents of the fruit.

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (agnuside, aucubin)
constituents.note: Characteristic constituents used in standardisation.

botanical_description.text: Deciduous shrub or small tree with slender, aromatic, palmately compound leaves of narrow, lance-shaped leaflets, greyish-downy beneath. Small, fragrant, lilac to violet flowers are borne in long, slender terminal spikes, followed by small, dark, peppercorn-like berries with a peppery, aromatic taste.
botanical_description.leaves: Palmately compound, narrow lance-shaped leaflets, greyish-downy beneath, aromatic
botanical_description.flowers: Small, fragrant, lilac to violet, in long slender terminal spikes
botanical_description.fruit: Small, dark, peppercorn-like berry with a peppery, aromatic taste (the medicinal part)

habitat.text: Native to the Mediterranean region and Central Asia, growing along riverbanks, streamsides and coastal scrub; widely cultivated in warm-temperate climates worldwide.

harvesting.text: The ripe berries are collected in autumn once they have darkened and dried, then further dried and stored for use in decoctions or standardised extracts.
harvesting.season: Autumn, when fruit is ripe

traditional_uses.text: Chasteberry has a long Mediterranean tradition, reflected in its 'chaste tree' name, historically used to reduce libido in monastic settings, and, more centrally to its modern use, as a traditional women's tonic for menstrual irregularities, premenstrual complaints and cyclical breast tenderness; standardised extracts are now among the best-clinically-studied herbal remedies for premenstrual syndrome, acting via dopaminergic modulation of prolactin.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Dried fruit extract standardised to agnuside/casticin content, taken as tablets or capsules; the form used in most clinical PMS and cyclical breast pain trials.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Dried berries simmered in water as a traditional preparation.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried berries.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for PMS commonly use extracts providing around 20-40 mg of dried fruit extract daily, taken over several menstrual cycles for full effect. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

indications.note: Eases cyclical breast pain (mastalgia) and menstrual-cycle complaints including cramping
indications.label: Pain (general)

indications.note: Eases cyclical breast pain (mastalgia) and menstrual-cycle complaints including cramping; Relief of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms
indications.label: PMS / premenstrual syndrome

contraindications.note: Reported adverse events are mild and reversible (nausea, headache, gastrointestinal upset, menstrual changes, acne, itching or rash).

Because it acts on dopamine and the hormonal axis, avoid alongside dopamine-antagonist (antipsychotic) or dopamine-agonist drugs and use caution with hormonal medicines (oral contraceptives, HRT) and hormone-sensitive conditions.
Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

botanical_description.text: Erect, branching perennial shrub with soft, downy, oval to lance-shaped leaves. Small, inconspicuous, greenish-yellow bell-shaped flowers are borne in the leaf axils, followed by a small orange-red berry enclosed in a papery, lantern-like calyx, giving rise to the name 'winter cherry'.
botanical_description.leaves: Soft, downy, oval to lance-shaped
botanical_description.flowers: Small, inconspicuous, greenish-yellow, bell-shaped
botanical_description.root: Long, thick, tapering, pale brown root (the medicinal part), aromatic with a distinctive horse-like smell (the source of the name ashwagandha)
botanical_description.fruit: Small orange-red berry enclosed in a papery, lantern-like calyx

habitat.text: Native to the drier regions of India, the Middle East and parts of Africa; cultivated in warm, semi-arid climates on well-drained soils, notably in central and western India.

harvesting.text: The root is dug in winter from plants around one year old, when withanolide content is highest, then cleaned, cut and dried; the leaves are picked through the growing season.
harvesting.season: Root in winter, from around one-year-old plants; leaf through the growing season

traditional_uses.text: Ashwagandha root is one of the most important rasayana (rejuvenative) tonics of Ayurvedic medicine, traditionally used to build strength and vitality, support healthy stress response, and promote restful sleep; this traditional adaptogenic reputation is now supported by clinical trials of standardised root extract for stress, sleep and physical performance.

preparations.name: Standardised extract
preparations.text: Root extract standardised to withanolide content, taken as capsules or tablets; the form used in most clinical stress, sleep and performance studies.

preparations.name: Decoction/churna (traditional)
preparations.text: Dried root powder (churna) taken with warm milk or water, or simmered as a decoction, the classic Ayurvedic preparation.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried root.

dosage.name: Standardised extract
dosage.text: Clinical trials for stress and sleep commonly use around 300-600 mg of standardised root extract daily, in divided doses, over several weeks. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

constituents.name: Withanolides (withaferin A, withanolide A)
constituents.note: Steroidal lactones considered the principal bioactive compounds, responsible for most of the plant's adaptogenic, anti-inflammatory and anticancer research interest.

constituents.name: Alkaloids (withanine, somniferine)
constituents.note: Minor tropane-type alkaloids contributing to the traditional sedative reputation.

constituents.name: Sitoindosides and saponins
constituents.note: Glycowithanolides linked to adaptogenic and immunomodulatory activity.

actions.note: Physical performance / strength improvement

contraindications.note: Generally well-tolerated in clinical trials at doses up to 600 mg/day for up to 12 weeks. Common side effects may include mild drowsiness, gastrointestinal upset, and diarrhea at higher doses. Contraindicated during pregnancy due to potential abortifacient effects. May interact with sedatives, immunosuppressants, thyroid medications, and blood sugar medications. People with autoimmune diseases, hyperthyroidism, or scheduled for surgery should consult healthcare providers before use. May cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals. Not recommended for children without medical supervision.

Duke (2002) classifies ashwagandha activities primarily at the experimental level (score 1), with adaptogenic, sedative, immunostimulant, and anti-inflammatory actions demonstrated in animal and in vitro models. Root preparations are used at 2–3 g powdered root three times daily, or 150–300 mg of standardized extract. Importantly, the plant has documented abortifacient and antifertility properties in animal studies, and its use should be avoided during pregnancy. It may potentiate the effects of barbiturates and sedatives (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Ashwagandha can raise circulating thyroid hormone (T3/T4) and lower TSH; a randomized trial normalised thyroid indices in subclinical hypothyroidism and a case of thyrotoxicosis has been reported. Combined with thyroid medication it may add to thyroid activity, so monitor thyroid levels.

Ashwagandha has sedative, GABA-related calming activity (a meta-analysis of randomised trials found it improves sleep); taken with sedatives, sleeping tablets or other central-nervous-system depressants it may add to drowsiness and slowed reactions.
Ashwagandha can lower blood glucose; combined with diabetes medicines it may add to blood-sugar lowering and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia, so monitor blood sugar.

pairings.note: Both ashwagandha and valerian are calming, sleep-promoting herbs; using them together may add to drowsiness and sedation, so take care especially before driving or when using other sedatives.
Ashwagandha and kava both calm the central nervous system; combining them may add to sedation and drowsiness, so combined use warrants caution.

constituents.name: Flavonoids (including maysin)
constituents.note: Major antioxidant constituents of corn silk; maysin is the predominant flavonoid quantified in standardised extracts.

constituents.name: Phenolic compounds
constituents.note: Contribute to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

constituents.name: Potassium salts, polysaccharides and saponins
constituents.note: Associated with the diuretic and demulcent actions.

botanical_description.text: Tall, robust annual grass with broad, strap-like leaves and a stout, jointed stem. Male flowers form a terminal tassel; female flowers develop on the ear (cob), each floret bearing a single long, silky style and stigma ('corn silk') that protrudes from the husk to catch pollen.
botanical_description.leaves: Broad, strap-like, alternate
botanical_description.flowers: Male tassel at the top; female flowers on the ear, each with a long silky style and stigma (corn silk)
botanical_description.root: Fibrous, with prominent brace roots at the lower stem nodes
botanical_description.fruit: Kernel (grain) borne on the cob; the silky stigmas and styles are the medicinal part, collected before pollination completes

habitat.text: Cultivated worldwide as a major cereal food crop in warm-temperate to tropical climates on fertile, well-drained soils; not found wild, having been domesticated from a wild grass ancestor (teosinte) in Mesoamerica.

harvesting.text: The silky stigmas and styles are collected from the ears in mid- to late summer, just before or as pollination occurs, while still fresh and pale, then dried quickly to preserve colour and flavonoid content.
harvesting.season: Mid- to late summer, at silking

traditional_uses.text: Corn silk has a long traditional use across Chinese, Native American and European folk medicine as a gentle diuretic and soothing demulcent for urinary tract complaints, mild fluid retention and kidney support.

preparations.name: Infusion
preparations.text: Dried corn silk infused in hot water as a traditional diuretic and urinary-support tea.

preparations.name: Decoction
preparations.text: Fresh or dried corn silk simmered in water, a slightly stronger traditional preparation.

preparations.name: Tincture
preparations.text: Alcoholic extract of the dried corn silk.

dosage.name: Infusion
dosage.text: Traditional herbal references suggest around 2-3 g of dried corn silk per cup, up to three times daily, with adequate fluid intake. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory
Soothing demulcent for urinary-tract irritation and mild urinary infections; supports kidney function

Diuretic - increases urine output to support the urinary tract and mild fluid retention/swelling; in conscious rats the aqueous extract is diuretic and kaliuretic and modifies glomerular filtration and potassium excretion

indications.note: Soothing demulcent for urinary-tract irritation and mild urinary infections; supports kidney function
indications.label: Infection (general)

indications.note: Diuretic - increases urine output to support the urinary tract and mild fluid retention/swelling; in conscious rats the aqueous extract is diuretic and kaliuretic and modifies glomerular filtration and potassium excretion
indications.label: Swelling / fluid retention

indications.note: Diuretic - increases urine output to support the urinary tract and mild fluid retention/swelling; in conscious rats the aqueous extract is diuretic and kaliuretic and modifies glomerular filtration and potassium excretion; Soothing demulcent for urinary-tract irritation and mild urinary infections; supports kidney function
indications.label: Urinary tract infection (UTI)

contraindications.note: As a diuretic it should be taken with plenty of fluids; use caution alongside prescription diuretics or medicines that affect potassium.

May lower blood sugar and blood pressure, so use caution with antidiabetic or antihypertensive medication; avoid concentrated medicinal doses in pregnancy - a high-dose standardised extract increased pre-implantation losses in pregnant rats (no fetal malformations were seen).

constituents.name: Gingerols and shogaols
constituents.note: The pungent phenolic constituents of the rhizome (notably 6-gingerol, which dehydrates to 6-shogaol on drying) responsible for ginger's antiemetic and anti-inflammatory activity; a standardized regimen of 84 mg/day gingerols/shogaols was effective against chemotherapy-induced nausea.

botanical_description.text: Rhizomatous perennial herb with narrow, lance-shaped leaves arising in two ranks from slender, reed-like pseudostems. Pale yellow-green flowers with purple markings are borne in a dense, cone-like spike on a separate short stalk arising directly from the rhizome, though ginger rarely flowers in cultivation. The branching, knobbly rhizome has a pale yellow interior and a characteristic pungent, spicy aroma.
botanical_description.leaves: Narrow, lance-shaped, arising in two ranks from reed-like pseudostems
botanical_description.flowers: Pale yellow-green with purple markings, in a dense cone-like spike; rarely produced in cultivation
botanical_description.stem: Slender, reed-like pseudostem formed by overlapping leaf bases
botanical_description.root: Branching, knobbly rhizome with a pale yellow interior (the medicinal part), plus fibrous roots
botanical_description.fruit: Rarely sets seed in cultivation; propagated by rhizome division

habitat.text: Believed native to tropical Southeast Asia; cultivated extensively throughout the tropics and subtropics (India, China, Nigeria, the Caribbean) in warm, humid conditions on well-drained, fertile soil.

harvesting.text: The rhizome is dug 8-10 months after planting, once the leaves have died back, for mature ('old') ginger with the fullest pungency, or earlier for milder young ('green') ginger; cleaned and used fresh or dried and ground.
harvesting.season: 8-10 months after planting for mature rhizome; earlier for young ginger

traditional_uses.text: Ginger rhizome has one of the longest and most widespread medicinal histories of any spice, used across Chinese, Ayurvedic and Western herbal traditions as a warming digestive carminative, an antiemetic for nausea and motion sickness, and an anti-inflammatory remedy for joint pain; this traditional reputation is now supported by an extensive clinical evidence base, particularly for nausea (including pregnancy and chemotherapy-related) and osteoarthritis.

preparations.name: Fresh rhizome / infusion
preparations.text: Fresh or dried rhizome grated or sliced and infused in hot water as the classic digestive and antiemetic ginger tea.

preparations.name: Standardised extract/powder
preparations.text: Dried rhizome powder or standardised extract, taken as capsules for nausea or joint-pain studies.

preparations.name: Culinary use
preparations.text: Fresh, dried or crystallised rhizome used extensively as a food spice and flavouring.

dosage.name: Standardised extract/powder
dosage.text: Clinical trials for nausea commonly use around 1-1.5 g of dried rhizome powder daily, in divided doses (up to 1 g/day in pregnancy); osteoarthritis studies commonly use similar or somewhat higher doses. Educational reference only, not a prescription.

actions.note: Antispasmodic (cramp easing)

contraindications.note: Ginger is widely considered safe at culinary and conventional supplemental doses. At high doses (>5 g/day), ginger may cause heartburn, acid reflux, mouth irritation, and mild gastrointestinal discomfort. Ginger may modestly inhibit platelet aggregation and enhance the effects of anticoagulants (e.g., warfarin) — clinical significance at dietary amounts is low, but caution is warranted with high-dose supplementation alongside blood-thinning medications (Shalansky et al., 2007). May lower blood sugar — monitor closely if combining with antidiabetic medications.

Pregnancy: Ginger is among the most studied herbal remedies for pregnancy nausea. At doses up to 1 g/day, it is generally considered safe and effective for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (NVP), based on multiple RCTs. The EMA monograph supports its use for NVP. Avoid doses >1.5 g/day during pregnancy due to theoretical concern about anticoagulant activity. Breastfeeding: generally considered safe at food amounts; limited data for medicinal doses (European Medicines Agency, 2012a).

Duke (2002) rates ginger as a triple-plus herb (+++) with strong clinical support (score 3) for antiemetic activity, including motion sickness, morning sickness, and postoperative nausea, and score 2 evidence for dyspepsia, diarrhea, and inflammation. Typical doses are 2-4 g dry rhizome per day or 500-1000 mg fresh root three times daily; Commission E approves ginger for dyspepsia and motion sickness. Ginger has antiplatelet properties and should be used with caution alongside anticoagulant medications (Duke, 2002).

drug_class_interactions.mechanism: Ginger has mild antiplatelet activity; a controlled trial found no significant change in warfarin levels or INR at normal doses, but caution and monitoring are advised when combined with blood thinners or antiplatelet drugs.
